topics.title: Demonstration of the Working of HTC Vive, Google Cardboard, Google Daydream, and Samsung Gear VR
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCNL508 - VR LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Demonstration of the Working of HTC Vive, Google Cardboard, Google Daydream, and Samsung Gear VR

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Virtual Reality (VR) — Formal KTU Definition

**Virtual Reality (VR)** is a computer-generated three-dimensional (3D) simulated environment that can be interacted with in real-time by a human user, primarily through **Head-Mounted Displays (HMDs)**, stereo audio, and motion-tracking input devices, providing complete immersion by replacing the user's natural sensory perception of the real world with synthetic digital stimuli.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** VR is fundamentally distinguished by three pillars — **Immersion, Interaction, and Imagination (the "3 Is")**, as standardized in KTU's PCCNL508 syllabus outcomes.

## 1.2 The Four HMDs Under Demonstration

| Device | Class | Tracking | Year | Target User |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **HTC Vive** | Tethered (PC-powered) | External Lighthouse (laser) | 2016 | High-end gaming/enterprise |
| **Google Cardboard** | Mobile (passive) | Head-rotation only (3-DoF) | 2014 | Entry-level, educational |
| **Google Daydream** | Mobile (active) | 3-DoF + handheld controller | 2016 | Mid-range smartphone users |
| **Samsung Gear VR** | Mobile (active) | 3-DoF + touchpad + IMU | 2015 | Samsung Galaxy flagship owners |

> [!NOTE]
> **Degrees of Freedom (DoF)** is the most critical metric distinguishing these devices.
> - **3-DoF** = Tracking of rotation only (yaw, pitch, roll) — Google Cardboard, Daydream, Gear VR
> - **6-DoF** = Tracking of rotation **and** translation (X, Y, Z position) — HTC Vive

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: "The Magic Window vs. The Magic Room"**
> - **Google Cardboard / Daydream / Gear VR** are like **Magic Windows** — you can look around inside a 3D scene, but your body remains "glued" to one spot. Turning your head moves your *gaze*, not your *body*.
> - **HTC Vive** is a **Magic Room** — Lighthouse sensors build an invisible coordinate system around you, and you can physically walk, duck, and lean. The virtual world updates as your real body moves through space.

## 1.4 Core Hardware Building Blocks (Common to All)

1. **Display Panel(s):** LCD or OLED screens that render stereoscopic images (one per eye).
2. **Optics:** Lenses (Fresnel, aspheric, or pancake) that focus the close-by screen for relaxed eye accommodation.
3. **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU):** A fusion of a **gyroscope** (rotation), **accelerometer** (linear motion), and sometimes **magnetometer** (heading).
4. **Tracking System:** Either **inside-out** (cameras on HMD) or **outside-in** (external sensors track HMD).
5. **Processing Unit:** Either a connected **PC** (HTC Vive) or the **smartphone SoC** (Cardboard, Daydream, Gear VR).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants & Standard VR Metrics (bold for emphasis):**
> - **Refresh Rate Threshold: 90 Hz** (minimum to avoid motion sickness per KTU/industry standard)
> - **Motion-to-Photon Latency Budget: ≤ 20 ms** (end-to-end delay)
> - **Minimum Field of View: 90°** (immersive threshold)
> - **Interpupillary Distance (IPD) Range: 54 mm – 74 mm** (human adult average ~63 mm)
> - **Stereoscopic Display Standard: 60 Hz per eye minimum**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Technical Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental VR Pipeline (Why It Works)

Every VR system — from Cardboard to HTC Vive — executes the same logical sequence in real-time:

1. **Sense** → Sensors capture user motion (head rotation, position, controller input).
2. **Process** → The system fuses sensor data into a 6D pose (x, y, z, yaw, pitch, roll).
3. **Render** → A new frame is drawn from the viewpoint matching the 6D pose, with two views (stereo).
4. **Display** → The frame is presented on the HMD screen, in sync with the user's new gaze direction.
5. **Repeat** → Cycle must complete within the latency budget.

> [!NOTE]
> The **"Why"** behind every VR device demonstration: all four devices implement this pipeline, but the *manner* in which they accomplish steps 1–2 differs dramatically (this is the exam-favorite comparison point).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Technical Sheet

### 2.2.1 Optical & Display Equations

The following equations govern how the lenses and panels produce a coherent virtual image:

| # | Parameter | Formula | Description | Typical VR Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Field of View (FOV)** | $\text{FOV} = 2 \cdot \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{d}{2f}\right)$ | where $d$ = display diagonal, $f$ = focal length | HTC Vive ≈ **110°**; Gear VR ≈ **101°**; Cardboard ≈ **80°–90°** |
| 2 | **Pixels Per Degree (PPD)** | $\text{PPD} = \dfrac{\text{Horizontal Resolution}}{\text{FOV}_{\text{horizontal}}}$ | Image sharpness metric | **≥ 15 PPD** is "Retina-like" |
| 3 | **Refresh Rate vs. Frame Time** | $t_{\text{frame}} = \dfrac{1}{f_{\text{refresh}}}$ | Time per display frame | 90 Hz ⇒ **11.11 ms** per frame |
| 4 | **Motion-to-Photon Latency** | $t_{\text{M2P}} = t_{\text{sense}} + t_{\text{process}} + t_{\text{render}} + t_{\text{display}}$ | End-to-end pipeline delay | Must be **≤ 20 ms** |
| 5 | **Stereoscopic Disparity** | $\Delta x = \dfrac{I \cdot d_{\text{object}}}{d_{\text{object}} - d_{\text{screen}}}$ | Pixel offset between L/R eye views for depth $d_{\text{object}}$ | Adjusts per virtual depth |
| 6 | **Lens Magnification** | $M = \dfrac{d_{\text{virtual}}}{d_{\text{actual}}}$ | Apparent size enlargement | Cardboard lens $f \approx 45$ mm |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pipe-Symbol Trap:** When writing $\vert x \vert$ (absolute value) inside any markdown table, always use `$\lvert x \rvert$` or `$\mid x \mid$` to avoid breaking the table syntax. This is a common formatting pitfall in exam answer sheets.

### 2.2.2 Sensor Fusion Equation (IMU Working)

Modern HMDs use a **complementary filter** or **Kalman filter** to combine gyroscope and accelerometer data:

$$\theta_{\text{fused}}(t) = \alpha \cdot \left[\theta_{\text{fused}}(t-1) + \omega_{\text{gyro}} \cdot \Delta t\right] + (1-\alpha) \cdot \theta_{\text{accel}}$$

where:
- $\theta_{\text{fused}}$ = estimated orientation angle
- $\omega_{\text{gyro}}$ = angular velocity from gyroscope
- $\theta_{\text{accel}} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{a_y}{a_z}\right)$ = tilt angle from accelerometer
- $\alpha \in [0.95, 0.98]$ = weighting factor (gyro trusted short-term, accel trusted long-term)

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for the four devices:**
> - **Google Cardboard** uses *only* the smartphone's raw IMU (no fusion, prone to drift).
> - **Daydream & Gear VR** use Samsung/Google's **calibrated IMU** with sensor fusion.
> - **HTC Vive** uses Lighthouse pose **as ground truth** and the IMU only to interpolate between laser sweeps (laser hits every 8.3 ms; IMU runs at 1000 Hz).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Demonstrated Devices |
|---|---|
| **Architecture & Real Estate** | HTC Vive — walk-through BIM models, scale 1:1 building previews |
| **Medical Training** | HTC Vive / Gear VR — surgical simulation, anatomy visualization |
| **Education (Kerala Schools)** | Google Cardboard — low-cost 3D geography/biology lessons (₹500 entry cost) |
| **Industrial Design** | HTC Vive — full-scale car/jet engine prototyping (e.g., Ford, Airbus) |
| **Therapy & Phobia Treatment** | Gear VR / Daydream — exposure therapy for acrophobia/PTSD |
| **Cultural Heritage** | Cardboard — virtual museum tours for students in rural Kerala |

## 2.4 Why the Tracking Approach Differs (The Core "How")

### A. Outside-In Tracking — HTC Vive
- Two **Lighthouse base stations** sit in opposite corners of the play area.
- Each Lighthouse emits a **horizontal laser sweep** and a **vertical laser sweep** (rotating at 60 Hz).
- **Photodiodes** on the HMD/controllers detect the laser hits.
- Time-difference-of-arrival between laser passes = precise angle → triangulated 6-DoF pose.

### B. Inside-Out Tracking with Smartphone (Daydream, Gear VR)
- The smartphone's **IMU** (gyro + accelerometer) detects head rotation.
- Optionally, the front camera (Daydream) or IR sensor (Gear VR with Note 4+) supplements with optical flow.
- Only 3-DoF because there is no external reference frame to compute absolute position.

### C. No Tracking — Google Cardboard
- Pure 3-DoF via the phone's gyroscope only.
- The cardboard body is a **passive** optical holder — no electronics.
- Works with any Android/iOS phone with a gyro.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favorite Comparison Point:** A viva question almost always asks *"Why is HTC Vive more accurate despite being older than Gear VR?"* — The answer lies in **sub-millimeter Lighthouse accuracy** vs. **IMU drift accumulation**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Working & Analytical Comparison Matrix

## 3.1 HTC Vive — Complete Working Walkthrough

### Step 1: Hardware Setup
Two Lighthouse base stations are mounted diagonally (e.g., 2 m apart, 2 m high) on opposite walls, both angled 30°–45° toward the play area center.

### Step 2: Lighthouse Sweep
Each base station contains:
- An **infrared LED array** (wide cone, always on, floods the room).
- A **horizontal-rotating laser module** spinning at 3,600 rpm (= 60 Hz).
- A **vertical-rotating laser module** spinning at 3,600 rpm (= 60 Hz).

### Step 3: Photodiode Detection
The HMD and controllers each contain an array of **photodiodes** (typically 20–30). When a laser sweep crosses a photodiode, the photodiode records the exact timestamp.

### Step 4: Angle Calculation
The angles $\theta_h$ (horizontal) and $\theta_v$ (vertical) from each base station are computed:

$$\theta = \frac{t_{\text{laser-detected}} - t_{\text{sync-pulse}}}{T_{\text{sweep-period}}} \cdot 360°$$

where $T_{\text{sweep-period}} = \dfrac{1}{60} = 16.67$ ms.

### Step 5: Triangulation for 6-DoF Pose
Knowing the angles from **two base stations** to the HMD, the system triangulates:

$$\vec{P}_{\text{HMD}} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \end{bmatrix} = f_{\text{triangulate}}(\theta_{L1,h}, \theta_{L1,v}, \theta_{L2,h}, \theta_{L2,v}, \vec{P}_{L1}, \vec{P}_{L2})$$

Combined with IMU yaw/pitch/roll $\rightarrow$ **full 6-DoF pose** in the Lighthouse coordinate system.

### Step 6: Rendering & Display
- The PC's GPU (NVIDIA/AMD) renders two viewports (left eye + right eye) using **OpenVR / SteamVR** API.
- **Asynchronous Spacewarp (ASW)** and **reprojection** techniques fill in frames when GPU misses deadlines.
- Display: **Dual 3.6" AMOLED, 1080×1200 per eye, 90 Hz refresh**.

### Step 7: Latency Validation
Total motion-to-photon latency:
$$t_{\text{M2P}} = t_{\text{IMU}} + t_{\text{USB/triangulation}} + t_{\text{render}} + t_{\text{scanout}} \approx 11\text{–}18 \text{ ms}$$

This is well under the 20 ms threshold → **no motion sickness**.

## 3.2 Google Cardboard — Complete Working Walkthrough

### Step 1: Smartphone Insertion
A smartphone (Android/iOS, 4.7"–6") is slotted into the front flap of the cardboard holder.

### Step 2: App Launch
A Cardboard-compatible app is launched (e.g., Google Street View, YouTube VR, Expeditions). The app locks the display to **landscape, split-screen, side-by-side stereoscopic** mode.

### Step 3: Lens Magnification
Biconvex lenses (typically $f = 40\text{–}50$ mm focal length) magnify the small screen. The eye perceives a **virtual image** appearing to be at infinity (or 2 m for relaxed focus), creating the illusion of a large display.

The virtual image distance is given by the thin lens equation:
$$\frac{1}{d_o} + \frac{1}{d_i} = \frac{1}{f}$$

For Cardboard: $d_o = 40$ mm (phone screen to lens), $f = 45$ mm → $d_i = -360$ mm (virtual, on the same side as the object). The negative sign indicates a **virtual image**, exactly as required.

### Step 4: 3-DoF Head Tracking
The phone's built-in **gyroscope** reports angular velocity $\omega$. The OS integrates it to produce orientation:
$$\theta(t) = \int_{0}^{t} \omega(\tau) \, d\tau + \theta(0)$$

This updates the rendered viewpoint for both eyes. **No positional tracking** — moving your head sideways does not change the view (translational drift is the result).

### Step 5: Display & Sync
- The phone's GPU renders at **60 Hz typically** (sometimes 75 Hz on newer devices).
- The app's render loop is gated by the phone's `Choreographer` (Android) or `CADisplayLink` (iOS) to minimize tearing.

### Step 6: Interaction
- **Magnetic trigger** (older Cardboards): a sliding magnet that triggers the phone's **magnetometer** — used as a single button.
- **Capacitive touch** (newer "Works with Google Cardboard" v2): a conductive lever that touches the screen.

## 3.3 Google Daydream — Working Walkthrough

### Step 1: Phone Insertion (Daydream-Ready Phones Only)
Daydream requires phones meeting the **Android Daydream-Ready specification** (e.g., Pixel 1–5, Samsung Galaxy S8/S9, etc., with **AMOLED displays, ≥ 90 Hz capable, and a calibrated IMU**).

### Step 2: NFC Handshake
The Daydream headset contains an **NFC tag**. When the phone is inserted, NFC triggers:
- Launching the Daydream app.
- Configuring display mode (WQHD, 60–90 Hz).
- Pairing the **Daydream Controller** via Bluetooth.

### Step 3: Controller as a Pointer
The Daydream Controller contains:
- A **9-DoF IMU** (gyro, accel, magnetometer).
- A **capacitive touchpad** (2 buttons + click).
- Sensors track 3-DoF rotation; the controller's orientation is used to point a virtual laser.

### Step 4: Rendering Loop (Asynchronous Reprojection)
Daydream uses **Google's VR SDK** with **asynchronous timewarp**:
- The app renders a frame.
- If the next frame is not ready by vsync, the system **reprojects** the last frame using the latest pose — preventing judder.
- Target: **90 Hz** with **fallback reprojection** at 60 Hz.

### Step 5: Lens System
Uses **customized optical lenses** with a wider FOV (~90°–100°) than Cardboard, plus an **auto-focus adjustment wheel** for users with glasses or different IPDs.

## 3.4 Samsung Gear VR — Working Walkthrough

### Step 1: Phone Locking Mechanism
Gear VR has a **physical USB-C or micro-USB connector** that physically mates with a Samsung Galaxy phone (S6 → S9, Note 5 → Note 9). The phone locks into place with a button-actuated clamp.

### Step 2: Firmware Handshake
The USB connection triggers:
- Oculus Mobile OS launch.
- Sensor calibration data load (Samsung phones ship with a **factory-calibrated IMU** with sub-degree accuracy).
- Activation of the phone's **higher refresh rate mode** (e.g., Note 9 → 90 Hz on Gear VR).

### Step 3: Touchpad & Buttons
The right side of the Gear VR has:
- A **capacitive touchpad** (swipe = UI navigation).
- A **back button**.
- A **volume rocker**.
- A **focus adjustment wheel** on top.

### Step 4: Oculus Tracking Algorithm
Gear VR uses an enhanced version of the **Oculus Mobile SDK** with:
- **Sensor fusion** at 1 kHz internal rate, downsampled to 60/72/90 Hz for display.
- **Positional timewarp** to reduce apparent latency.
- **Drift correction** using the magnetometer as an absolute heading reference (corrects gyro drift).

### Step 5: Display & Optics
- Uses the phone's AMOLED display in **Note-style** (e.g., 2960×1440 → downsampled to per-eye 2560×1440 with barrel distortion correction).
- Optics: **~101° FOV**, with a focus wheel.
- Internal foam padding provides light sealing.

## 3.5 Master Comparison Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

| Parameter | **HTC Vive** | **Google Cardboard** | **Google Daydream** | **Samsung Gear VR** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **DoF** | 6 | 3 | 3 (controller: 3) | 3 |
| **Tracking Type** | Outside-in (Lighthouse laser) | IMU only (gyro) | IMU + Controller IMU | IMU (calibrated) + magnetometer |
| **Positional Accuracy** | **~0.3 mm (sub-mm)** | N/A (no positional) | N/A | N/A |
| **Display Source** | Built-in dual AMOLED | Smartphone | Smartphone | Smartphone |
| **Resolution (per eye)** | 1080×1200 | Phone-dependent (typical 1080p split) | Phone-dependent (WQHD split) | Phone-dependent (QHD split) |
| **Refresh Rate** | **90 Hz** | 60 Hz | 60–90 Hz | 60–90 Hz |
| **FOV** | ~110° | ~80°–90° | ~90°–100° | ~101° |
| **Tracking Latency** | ~11–18 ms | 30–50 ms (no fusion) | ~20 ms (with reprojection) | ~16–20 ms (with timewarp) |
| **Controller** | 2× Vive wands (6-DoF) | Magnetic/ capacitive trigger | 1× Daydream controller (3-DoF) | Built-in touchpad |
| **Power Source** | Tethered to PC | Phone battery | Phone battery | Phone battery |
| **Cost (approx.)** | ₹50,000+ (with PC ₹1 lakh+) | ₹300–₹1,000 | ₹6,000–₹10,000 | ₹8,000 (headset only) |
| **Year Released** | 2016 | 2014 | 2016 | 2015 |
| **Processor** | External PC (GPU heavy) | Smartphone SoC | Smartphone SoC | Smartphone SoC (Samsung) |
| **Lens Type** | Fresnel | Biconvex plastic | Custom optical | Custom optical |
| **Setup Complexity** | High (PC + 2 base stations) | Very Low | Low | Low–Medium |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Universal VR Working Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT SENSING STAGE"]
        IMU[Gyroscope and Accelerometer]
        MAG[Magnetometer Optional]
        CAM[External Sensors or Cameras]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS["PROCESSING STAGE"]
        FUSE[Sensor Fusion Filter]
        POSE[6D Pose Estimator x y z yaw pitch roll]
    end

    subgraph RENDER["RENDERING STAGE"]
        GPU[GPU Stereo Renderer Left and Right Views]
        WARP[Timewarp and Reprojection]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT DISPLAY STAGE"]
        DISP[HMD Display AMOLED or LCD]
        LENS[Optical Lens System]
        EYE[Human Eye]
    end

    IMU --> FUSE
    MAG --> FUSE
    CAM --> POSE
    FUSE --> POSE
    POSE --> GPU
    GPU --> WARP
    WARP --> DISP
    DISP --> LENS
    LENS --> EYE

    classDef inputStage fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    classDef processStage fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,color:#000
    classDef renderStage fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000
    classDef outputStage fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000

    class IMU,MAG,CAM inputStage
    class FUSE,POSE processStage
    class GPU,WARP renderStage
    class DISP,LENS,EYE outputStage
```

## 4.2 HTC Vive — Lighthouse Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LIGHTHOUSE["LIGHTHOUSE BASE STATION x 2"]
        L1[IR LED Flood Light]
        L2[Horizontal Laser 60 Hz]
        L3[Vertical Laser 60 Hz]
        L4[Sync Pulse Emitter]
    end

    subgraph HMDDEV["HMD AND CONTROLLER"]
        PD[Photodiode Array 20 to 30 units]
        HMDIMU[IMU 1000 Hz]
        CTRL[Controller IMU and Buttons]
    end

    subgraph PCSYS["PC PROCESSING UNIT"]
        TS[Timestamp Comparator]
        TRI[Triangulation Solver]
        POSE[6DoF Pose Output]
        GAME[Game Engine Unity or Unreal]
    end

    L1 --> PD
    L2 --> PD
    L3 --> PD
    L4 --> TS
    PD --> TS
    TS --> TRI
    HMDIMU --> TRI
    CTRL --> TRI
    TRI --> POSE

    GAME --> HMDDEV

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> **Define Virtual Reality and list its three core characteristics (the "3 Is"). Briefly explain any one with a real-world example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Virtual Reality (VR) is a computer-simulated 3D environment that replaces the user's real-world sensory input with synthetic stimuli via an HMD, allowing real-time interaction.

**Three Core Characteristics ("3 Is"):**
1. **Immersion** — the sensation of being physically present inside the virtual environment.
2. **Interaction** — the ability to modify the environment in real-time through user input.
3. **Imagination** — the capacity of the virtual world to extend beyond physical reality, enabling creative scenarios.

*Example (any one):* Immersion is achieved in the **HTC Vive** by combining a 110° FOV dual-AMOLED display with 6-DoF Lighthouse tracking, so the user feels as if they are physically inside the rendered room.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sessional]**
> **Differentiate between 3-DoF and 6-DoF tracking. State which of the four demonstrated devices (HTC Vive, Google Cardboard, Google Daydream, Samsung Gear VR) support each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | **3-DoF Tracking** | **6-DoF Tracking** |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Tracks **3 rotational axes** (yaw, pitch, roll) only | Tracks **3 rotational + 3 translational axes** (X, Y, Z position) |
| Hardware Required | IMU (gyro + accelerometer) | IMU + External reference (e.g., Lighthouse, cameras) |
| User Experience | User can "look around" but cannot physically move | User can walk, duck, lean — full body movement |
| Demonstrated Devices | Google Cardboard, Google Daydream, Samsung Gear VR | **HTC Vive** (only one of the four) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 2 questions carry 14 marks. Students must answer either **Question A** OR **Question B**.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Track A Choice

> **Q A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified]** [14 Marks]
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **working of HTC Vive** in detail. Discuss how Lighthouse base stations achieve sub-millimeter 6-DoF tracking. **[7 Marks]**
> **(b)** Compare and contrast the **optical design and display specifications** of HTC Vive, Google Cardboard, Google Daydream, and Samsung Gear VR in a single comparison table. Identify which device has the widest FOV and justify why. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working of HTC Vive — [7 Marks]**

The HTC Vive is a PC-tethered VR system based on **outside-in laser tracking**. The system comprises three main hardware components:

1. **HMD (Head-Mounted Display):** Contains two 3.6" AMOLED panels (1080×1200 per eye, 90 Hz), Fresnel lenses, an integrated IMU (gyro + accelerometer running at 1000 Hz), and an array of ~20 photodiodes on its surface.

2. **Two Lighthouse Base Stations:** Wall-mounted in opposite corners. Each contains:
   - An array of IR LEDs (always on, flood the room with IR light).
   - A horizontal-rotating laser module (60 Hz, 3,600 rpm).
   - A vertical-rotating laser module (60 Hz, 3,600 rpm).
   - A sync pulse emitter for timing reference.

3. **Two Wireless Controllers:** Each with photodiodes, IMU, trackpad, and trigger button.

**Working Steps:**
- **Step 1:** Each base station sweeps its H-laser and V-laser across the play area at 60 Hz.
- **Step 2:** When a laser strikes a photodiode on the HMD/controller, the photodiode records the exact timestamp.
- **Step 3:** The host PC compares the timestamp with the sync pulse → calculates the **angle** $\theta$ from that base station to the HMD:
$$\theta = \frac{(t_{\text{detected}} - t_{\text{sync}})}{T_{\text{sweep}}} \times 360°$$
- **Step 4:** Angles from both base stations are used in **triangulation** to compute the HMD's (x, y, z) position with sub-millimeter accuracy.
- **Step 5:** The IMU fills in fine-grained motion (1 kHz updates) between laser sweeps (8.3 ms gap).
- **Step 6:** The 6-DoF pose is sent to SteamVR/OpenVR → game engine → GPU renders stereo frames → displayed on HMD.
- **Result:** Total motion-to-photon latency ≈ 11–18 ms, well below the 20 ms threshold.

**(b) Comparison Table and FOV Analysis — [7 Marks]**

| Spec | **HTC Vive** | **Google Cardboard** | **Google Daydream** | **Samsung Gear VR** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Display | Dual 3.6" AMOLED | Smartphone (any) | Smartphone (Daydream-ready) | Samsung Galaxy AMOLED |
| Resolution/eye | 1080×1200 | Phone-dep. (split) | Phone-dep. (WQHD split) | Phone-dep. (QHD split) |
| **FOV** | **~110°** | **~80°–90°** | **~90°–100°** | **~101°** |
| Refresh Rate | **90 Hz** | 60 Hz | 60–90 Hz | 60–90 Hz |
| Lens Type | Fresnel | Biconvex plastic | Custom optical | Custom optical |
| Focus Adj. | Fixed IPD | Fixed | **Focus wheel** | **Focus wheel** |
| Tracking | Lighthouse (6-DoF) | 3-DoF (gyro) | 3-DoF | 3-DoF |
| Processor | External PC | Phone SoC | Phone SoC | Phone SoC |

**Widest FOV:** **HTC Vive (≈ 110°)** — This is because it uses a large display panel (3.6" diagonal per eye) positioned at a relatively short focal length, computed as:
$$\text{FOV} = 2 \cdot \arctan\!\left(\frac{d}{2f}\right) = 2 \cdot \arctan\!\left(\frac{91.4 \text{ mm}}{2 \times 50 \text{ mm}}\right) \approx 84° \text{ (per axis)}$$
Larger display + wider lens separation in Vive = widest FOV. **Wider FOV enhances immersion** because more of the user's peripheral vision is covered, reducing the "tunnel vision" effect common in mobile VR headsets.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Track B Choice

> **Q B. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Modified]** [14 Marks]
> **(a)** Explain the **working of Google Cardboard** with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of biconvex lenses and the formula governing virtual image formation. **[7 Marks]**
> **(b)** Discuss the **sensor fusion and tracking mechanism** in Google Daydream and Samsung Gear VR. Why does Daydream require only "Daydream-Ready" phones, while Cardboard works with any phone? **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working of Google Cardboard — [7 Marks]**

Google Cardboard is the simplest VR HMD — a **passive cardboard viewer** that holds a smartphone at a fixed distance from two biconvex plastic lenses.

**Components:**
- Cardboard body (folded) with biconvex lenses (f ≈ 45 mm).
- Magnetic or capacitive input trigger.
- Smartphone slot.
- NFC tag (v2 only) for app launch.

**Working Steps:**
1. **Smartphone Insertion:** A compatible smartphone (Android/iOS with gyroscope) is inserted into the front slot, screen facing the lenses.
2. **App Launch:** A Cardboard-compatible VR app is launched. The app outputs **side-by-side stereoscopic** images.
3. **Lens Magnification (Biconvex Lenses):** Each biconvex lens takes the small portion of the screen visible through it and projects a **virtual image** that appears far away (relaxed eye focus).

The thin lens equation governs this:
$$\frac{1}{d_o} + \frac{1}{d_i} = \frac{1}{f}$$
- $d_o$ = object distance (phone screen to lens) ≈ 40 mm
- $f$ = focal length of lens ≈ 45 mm
- $\frac{1}{d_i} = \frac{1}{45} - \frac{1}{40} = \frac{40 - 45}{1800} = -\frac{5}{1800}$
- $d_i = -360$ mm

The **negative sign** confirms a **virtual image** (on the same side as the object). The eye perceives a large, distant image — perfect for relaxed focus and the illusion of a wide FOV.

4. **3-DoF Tracking:** The phone's gyroscope detects head rotation. The orientation is integrated over time:
$$\theta(t) = \int_0^t \omega(\tau)\, d\tau$$
The rendered scene updates accordingly, allowing the user to "look around" the virtual world.
5. **Interaction:** A magnetic slider or capacitive lever triggers a single "click" event for menu selection.

**Block Diagram (inline summary):**
> Head movement → Phone Gyroscope → Angular Velocity → Integrated Orientation (3-DoF) → App Redraws Stereo Frame → Display → Biconvex Lens → User's Eyes → Perceived Virtual Scene

---

**(b) Sensor Fusion in Daydream & Gear VR + Phone Compatibility — [7 Marks]**

**Google Daydream Sensor Fusion:**
- Daydream uses the smartphone's **calibrated IMU** (gyro + accelerometer + magnetometer) running at high frequency.
- The **Daydream Controller** has its own 9-DoF IMU and a capacitive touchpad. It pairs via Bluetooth and acts as a 3-DoF pointer.
- The **Google VR SDK** performs **asynchronous timewarp**: if the next frame is not ready, the last frame is **reprojected** using the latest pose, ensuring smooth visuals.
- Drift correction: The magnetometer provides absolute heading reference, periodically re-anchoring the gyroscope's integrated orientation.

**Samsung Gear VR Sensor Fusion:**
- Uses **Oculus Mobile SDK** sensor fusion at 1 kHz internal rate.
- The phone must be a **Samsung Galaxy with factory-calibrated IMU** (e.g., Galaxy S9, Note 9).
- **Positional timewarp** adjusts the rendered image based on the latest pose.
- The USB-C/micro-USB physical connection between phone and headset enables lower-latency data transfer than Bluetooth.

**Why Daydream requires "Daydream-Ready" phones, but Cardboard does not:**

| Aspect | Google Cardboard | Google Daydream |
|---|---|---|
| IMU Calibration | **Not required** — uses any phone's gyro | **Required** — needs factory-calibrated IMU for low drift |
| Display Type | **Any** (LCD or OLED) | **AMOLED only** (for high refresh + low persistence) |
| Refresh Rate | **60 Hz acceptable** | **≥ 90 Hz required** for VR comfort |
| Tracking | **3-DoF raw gyro** | **3-DoF + SDK-level sensor fusion + timewarp** |
| Controller | **Not required** | **Required** — must pair via Bluetooth |
| Performance | Low GPU demand | High GPU demand (sustained 90 fps) |

**Justification:** Cardboard is a **passive optical viewer** with minimal software — it only needs the phone's basic gyroscope for 3-DoF. Daydream, however, runs **advanced sensor fusion, timewarp, and a paired controller** that all demand **deterministic, low-latency hardware performance**. Only "Daydream-Ready" phones guarantee the calibrated IMU, AMOLED display with ≥ 90 Hz capability, and a powerful enough SoC to render at 90 fps.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing "DoF" with "DOF" (Depth of Field):** DoF in VR = Degrees of Freedom. DoF in photography = Depth of Field. Mixing these up costs marks.
> 2. **Calling Cardboard "3-DoF" without specifying "rotation only":** Always write "3 rotational DoF" or "3-DoF rotational tracking" to be precise.
> 3. **Forgetting the role of the magnetometer in drift correction:** Many students describe the gyroscope but omit the magnetometer's role in re-anchoring absolute heading.
> 4. **Stating "HTC Vive uses cameras" — INCORRECT:** It uses **infrared laser sweeps + photodiodes**, not cameras. Cameras are used in *inside-out* tracking systems (e.g., Oculus Rift S, Windows Mixed Reality).
> 5. **Forgetting the negative sign in thin lens equation:** When $d_i$ is negative, it is a *virtual* image. Always include the sign in your derivation.
> 6. **Miscounting the Eye's role:** Stereo VR works because the **interpupillary distance (IPD) of ~63 mm** causes each eye to see a slightly different view (binocular disparity) — the brain fuses these into a 3D perception.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

### 🔑 Must-Know Definitions
- **VR** = Immersion + Interaction + Imagination
- **HMD** = Head-Mounted Display (display + optics + IMU)
- **DoF** = Degrees of Freedom (3 = rotation only, 6 = rotation + position)
- **PPD** = Pixels Per Degree (sharpness metric)
- **M2P Latency** = Motion-to-Photon Latency (target ≤ 20 ms)
- **Timewarp/Reprojection** = Re-projecting a frame with the latest pose when GPU misses deadline

### 🔑 Must-Know Numbers (Bold = Critical for KTU)
- **Refresh Rate Threshold: 90 Hz** (motion sickness prevention)
- **Latency Budget: ≤ 20 ms**
- **Human IPD: 54–74 mm** (avg 63 mm)
- **Cardboard lens focal length: ~45 mm**
- **HTC Vive: 110° FOV, 90 Hz, 0.3 mm tracking accuracy**
- **Gear VR: ~101° FOV, 60–90 Hz**
- **Cardboard: 60 Hz typical, 80–90° FOV**

### 🔑 Must-Know Distinctions
1. **HTC Vive** = **only** 6-DoF device among the four; uses **Lighthouse** (outside-in laser, not cameras).
2. **Cardboard** = passive cardboard, **no electronics**; only **magnetic/capacitive** trigger.
3. **Daydream** = requires **Daydream-Ready** phones; uses **asynchronous timewarp** + Bluetooth controller.
4. **Gear VR** = **Oculus Mobile SDK**; works only with **Samsung Galaxy** phones via **physical USB** connection.

### 🔑 Must-Know Formulas (Always include units in derivations)
- $\text{FOV} = 2 \arctan(d / 2f)$
- $\text{PPD} = \text{Resolution} / \text{FOV}$
- $t_{\text{frame}} = 1 / f_{\text{refresh}}$
- Thin lens: $\frac{1}{d_o} + \frac{1}{d_i} = \frac{1}{f}$
- Sensor fusion: $\theta_{\text{fused}} = \alpha \cdot \theta_{\text{gyro}} + (1-\alpha) \cdot \theta_{\text{accel}}$
- Lighthouse angle: $\theta = (t_{\text{detected}} - t_{\text{sync}}) / T_{\text{sweep}} \times 360°$

### 🔑 Must-Know "Why" Questions
- **Why is HTC Vive more accurate than Gear VR?** → Sub-mm Lighthouse triangulation vs. IMU drift.
- **Why does Daydream need special phones?** → Requires calibrated IMU + AMOLED + 90 Hz.
- **Why do all VR devices need a high refresh rate?** → To prevent motion sickness (vestibular mismatch).
- **Why is the Cardboard lens equation result negative?** → Virtual image (light does not actually converge — perceived only).
- **Why do Lighthouse and IMU work together in Vive?** → IMU fills 8.3 ms gaps between laser sweeps at 1 kHz.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Familiarization of Three.js, Phaser.js, and Babylon.js
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCNL508 - VR LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Familiarization of Three.js, Phaser.js, and Babylon.js

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are Web-Based 3D and 2D Rendering Libraries?

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech Virtual Reality Lab, a **Web-Based Rendering Library** is a JavaScript/TypeScript framework that abstracts the complexities of low-level graphics APIs (like **WebGL**, **WebGPU**, and the **HTML5 Canvas**) into a developer-friendly, object-oriented toolkit. These libraries act as the foundational "engine room" for rendering interactive 2D and 3D content directly inside a web browser without requiring native plug-ins.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCCNL508 - VR LAB):**
> Experiment 2 of the VR Lab explicitly mandates the *familiarization* of three industry-grade web rendering frameworks. Familiarization means the student must be able to **identify**, **install**, **architecturally compare**, and **build a minimum demonstrable program** in each framework.

The three libraries mandated in this experiment are:

| Library | Primary Rendering Domain | Underlying API |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Three.js** | 3D Scenes on the Web | WebGL |
| **Phaser.js** | 2D Browser Games | Canvas / WebGL |
| **Babylon.js** | 3D Engine / VR / XR | WebGL / WebGPU |

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Film Studio:**
> Imagine you are a film director setting up a movie set.
> - The **Camera** is your viewpoint — where the audience looks from.
> - The **Scene** is the entire physical set (walls, floor, props, actors).
> - The **Lights** illuminate the actors so the camera can capture them.
> - The **Renderer** is the film camera and the projectionist rolled into one — it actually takes the snapshot of the scene from the camera's perspective and displays it on the screen (the monitor).
> - The **Animation Loop** is the director yelling "Action!" repeatedly, 60 times per second, to keep the movie rolling.
>
> In this analogy, **Three.js** and **Babylon.js** give you the *full 3D film studio in your browser*. **Phaser.js** gives you a *specialized 2D animation studio* — perfect for cartoons and 2D platformers instead of live-action 3D films.

A more precise breakdown of each library's "personality":

- **Three.js** is the *minimalist craftsman*. It gives you the raw building blocks (Scene, Camera, Mesh, Light, Renderer) and lets you assemble them yourself. It is lightweight, widely adopted, and the *de facto* standard for showing 3D on a webpage.
- **Phaser.js** is the *2D game specialist*. It does not pretend to do 3D. Instead, it is laser-focused on 2D sprites, tile maps, physics collisions, and arcade-style gameplay running in a browser tab.
- **Babylon.js** is the *enterprise powerhouse*. It is a complete game engine with built-in physics, VR controller support, GUI systems, and a node-based material editor — closer in spirit to Unreal Engine but running in JavaScript.

### 1.3 Standard Installation & Environment Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Technical Standards (KTU Lab Evaluation Criteria):**
> The following constants and standards are what examiners will look for in your lab record:
> - **JavaScript Runtime:** ES6+ (ES2015 and above) — mandatory.
> - **Package Manager:** **npm** (Node Package Manager) version 8+ or **yarn** version 1.22+.
> - **WebGL Version Required:** **WebGL 1.0** as the minimum, **WebGL 2.0** preferred for modern effects.
> - **Browser Support:** Latest two stable versions of Chrome, Firefox, Edge, and Safari.
> - **Server:** A local HTTP server (e.g., **Live Server** VS Code extension, Python `http.server`, or Node `http-server`) is mandatory because most browsers block module loading from the `file://` protocol.

The standard installation command for all three is identical in syntax:

```bash
npm install three
npm install phaser
npm install @babylonjs/core
```

Or, for a quicker learning setup, the **CDN (Content Delivery Network)** import inside an HTML file is:

```html
<script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/three@0.160.0/build/three.min.js"></script>
<script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/phaser@3.70.0/dist/phaser.min.js"></script>
<script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/babylonjs@6.49.0/babylon.min.js"></script>
```
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Deep Dive — The Scene Graph Paradigm

All three libraries, at their philosophical core, are built around the concept of a **Scene Graph**. Understanding this single concept explains 80% of how every web 3D library works.

> **Definition (KTU Textbook Standard):**
> A **Scene Graph** is a hierarchical tree data structure in which every node represents a graphical entity (a mesh, a light, a camera, or a group of entities). Transformations applied to a parent node — translation, rotation, scaling — are *inherited* by all its descendants.

The conceptual flow is identical across Three.js, Babylon.js, and (to a lesser extent) Phaser.js:

1. **Create a Scene** — the root container that holds all visible entities.
2. **Define a Camera** — establishes the viewpoint and projection (Perspective vs. Orthographic).
3. **Construct Geometry** — define the mathematical shape (vertices, faces).
4. **Apply a Material** — define the surface properties (color, texture, shininess).
5. **Wrap Geometry + Material in a Mesh** — the actual renderable object.
6. **Position the Mesh inside the Scene** — attach it to the graph.
7. **Add Light Sources** — illuminate the meshes so the camera can perceive them.
8. **Instantiate a Renderer** — point it at the canvas and call `render()` every frame.

### 2.2 Comparative Library Analysis (The "Why" Behind Choosing One)

#### 2.2.1 Three.js — The Minimalist's 3D Toolkit

- **Author & Origin:** Ricardo Cabello (a.k.a. *Mr.doob*), first released in **2010**.
- **GitHub Stars (industry proxy):** Consistently above **95,000+** — the most-starred 3D library on GitHub.
- **License:** **MIT License** — free for commercial and academic use.
- **Rendering Engine:** Wraps **WebGL** natively; a parallel `WebGPURenderer` is in active development.
- **Architectural Philosophy:** *You bring the architecture, we bring the bricks.* Three.js deliberately omits a built-in physics engine, a scene editor, and a GUI library. You assemble those from community packages.
- **Use Cases:** Product configurators, 3D data visualizations, architectural walkthroughs, web-based VR experiences (via WebXR), creative coding art installations.
- **Strength:** Lightweight (core is ~600 KB minified), enormous community, thousands of `examples/` on the official site.
- **Weakness:** No first-party physics, no built-in GUI — you must integrate Cannon.js, Ammo.js, dat.GUI, or leva separately.

#### 2.2.2 Phaser.js — The 2D Game Framework

- **Author & Origin:** Richard Davey (Photon Storm), first released in **2013**.
- **License:** **MIT License**.
- **Rendering Engine:** Dual renderer — **HTML5 Canvas** (for broad compatibility) and **WebGL** (for performance).
- **Architectural Philosophy:** A complete, opinionated *game framework*. It is not just a rendering library; it bundles a scene manager, an input handler, a sound system, an animation system, and a physics engine into a single coherent API.
- **Built-in Physics Engines:** **Arcade Physics** (simple AABB collisions) and **P2.js** (full 2D rigid-body physics with constraints).
- **Use Cases:** 2D platformers, side-scrollers, top-down RPGs, mobile browser games, advergames.
- **Strength:** *Phaser's documentation* is famously excellent; the "Make Your First Phaser 3 Game" tutorial is the gold standard for the genre.
- **Weakness:** Strictly 2D — there is no first-class 3D support, although a community WebGL plugin exists.

#### 2.2.3 Babylon.js — The Enterprise 3D Engine

- **Author & Origin:** Maintained by **Microsoft** and a large open-source community, first released in **2013**.
- **License:** **Apache 2.0 License** — permissive, but with an explicit patent grant.
- **Rendering Engine:** **WebGL**, **WebGL 2**, and **WebGPU** (full support added in v6+).
- **Architectural Philosophy:** *A complete game engine in the browser.* Babylon.js ships with built-in GUI (Babylon GUI), built-in physics (Cannon, Oimo, Ammo, Havok plug-ins), a **Node Material Editor** (visual shader programming), a **Playground** sandbox, and a full **WebXR** implementation with hand-tracking and controller support.
- **Use Cases:** Industrial simulations, medical training, AAA-quality web games, true cross-platform VR/AR (Quest, Vision Pro, HoloLens), architectural visualization.
- **Strength:** Most feature-complete; first-class TypeScript definitions; **Babylon.js Playground** allows in-browser code editing.
- **Weakness:** Steeper learning curve; larger bundle size (~2 MB minified for the full engine).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU "Cheat Sheet" — Master These Constants and You Will Pass:**
> This table is what KTU examiners expect every student to memorize before stepping into the VR Lab. It is heavily weighted in the viva voce.

| Concept | Three.js | Phaser.js | Babylon.js |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Latest Stable Version (2024) | **r160+** | **3.80+** | **6.49+ / 7.x** |
| License | **MIT** | **MIT** | **Apache 2.0** |
| Primary Language | JavaScript | JavaScript / TypeScript | **TypeScript-first** |
| Scene Root Object | `THREE.Scene` | `Phaser.Scene` | `BABYLON.Scene` |
| Default Camera | `PerspectiveCamera` | `Phaser.Cameras.Scene2D` | `UniversalCamera` |
| Default Renderer | `WebGLRenderer` | Auto (Canvas or WebGL) | `Engine` (auto-selects WebGL/WebGPU) |
| Built-in Physics | **None** (use external) | **Arcade + P2.js** | **Cannon / Oimo / Ammo / Havok** |
| WebXR / VR Support | Via `WebXR` module | None | **First-class** |
| Bundle Size (Minified) | ~600 KB | ~1.1 MB | ~2.0 MB |
| Official Playground | threejs.org/editor | labs.phaser.io | **playground.babylonjs.com** |

### 2.4 The Rendering Pipeline — A Common Mental Model

Although the API names differ, every one of these libraries funnels graphics through the same conceptual pipeline. Memorize this flow — it is the answer to the most common viva question on this experiment.

$$
\text{Source Data} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Vertex Shader} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Rasterization} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Fragment Shader} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Frame Buffer} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Screen}
$$

The "Source Data" is your geometry (positions, normals, UVs). The vertex shader projects 3D coordinates to 2D screen space. The fragment shader decides the color of every pixel inside those projected triangles. The frame buffer holds the finished image that the browser paints on the screen.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Three.js** powers NASA's **"Experience Curiosity"** Mars Rover web demo and is used in Mozilla Hubs for browser-based social VR.
- **Phaser.js** has been used in major advertising campaigns (e.g., promotional games for Disney and the BBC) because of its tiny load time and mobile-friendly nature.
- **Babylon.js** is the rendering engine behind Microsoft's **Mesh** 3D meeting platform and is officially supported in the **Apple Vision Pro** WebXR implementation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analysis & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:**
> This section does not contain algebraic derivations in the strict mathematical sense — Experiment 2 is a *familiarization* lab on JavaScript rendering libraries. Instead, the "derivation" here is the step-by-step logical build-up of the canonical "Hello World" program for each of the three libraries, exposing the underlying reasoning behind every line of code. Every architectural decision is justified.

### 3.1 Three.js — The Canonical "Rotating Cube" Program

The goal is to render a **3D cube** that **rotates continuously** on a webpage. We will derive this from first principles.

#### Step 1: Establish a Canvas Container

The browser must know *where* to draw. We create a full-window `<canvas>` element inside the HTML body.

```html
<canvas id="three-canvas"></canvas>
```

**Reasoning:** A canvas is a bitmap surface the browser lets JavaScript draw into. Without a canvas, the renderer has nowhere to paint.

#### Step 2: Instantiate the Scene, Camera, and Renderer

```javascript
const scene = new THREE.Scene();
const camera = new THREE.PerspectiveCamera(75, window.innerWidth / window.innerHeight, 0.1, 1000);
const renderer = new THREE.WebGLRenderer({ canvas: document.getElementById("three-canvas") });
renderer.setSize(window.innerWidth, window.innerHeight);
```

**Reasoning for the PerspectiveCamera parameters:**

$$
\text{fov} = 75^\circ, \quad \text{aspect} = \frac{\text{width}}{\text{height}}, \quad \text{near} = 0.1, \quad \text{far} = 1000
$$

- **Field of View (FOV) = 75°** — a wide angle that gives a slightly immersive, "first-person" feel. A typical human eye FOV is ~60°, so 75° feels game-like.
- **Aspect Ratio** — must match the canvas, otherwise the image will appear stretched.
- **Near Plane = 0.1** — anything closer than 0.1 units is clipped (not rendered).
- **Far Plane = 1000** — anything beyond 1000 units is clipped. This range is the **view frustum**.

#### Step 3: Construct the Geometry

```javascript
const geometry = new THREE.BoxGeometry(1, 1, 1);
```

**Reasoning:** `BoxGeometry(1,1,1)` creates a unit cube — 1 unit along x, 1 along y, 1 along z. Internally, this generates **24 vertices** (4 per face × 6 faces) and **12 triangles** (2 per face × 6 faces).

#### Step 4: Construct the Material

```javascript
const material = new THREE.MeshStandardMaterial({ color: 0x00ff00 });
```

**Reasoning:** `MeshStandardMaterial` is a *physically-based* material. It responds to lights via the PBR (Physically Based Rendering) equations. Without lights, this material would appear black.

#### Step 5: Combine Geometry + Material into a Mesh

```javascript
const cube = new THREE.Mesh(geometry, material);
scene.add(cube);
```

**Reasoning:** A `Mesh` is the *renderable* wrapper. Geometry alone is just data; material alone is just a description. The mesh is the contract between the two.

#### Step 6: Add Illumination

```javascript
const light = new THREE.DirectionalLight(0xffffff, 1);
light.position.set(2, 2, 5);
scene.add(light);
```

**Reasoning:** `MeshStandardMaterial` needs light. A `DirectionalLight` simulates a distant sun — parallel rays from a single direction. Intensity **1** is a neutral baseline.

#### Step 7: Position the Camera and Start the Animation Loop

```javascript
camera.position.z = 5;

function animate() {
  requestAnimationFrame(animate);
  cube.rotation.x += 0.01;
  cube.rotation.y += 0.01;
  renderer.render(scene, camera);
}
animate();
```

**Reasoning:** `requestAnimationFrame(animate)` synchronizes the render with the browser's repaint cycle (typically 60 Hz, or ~16.67 ms per frame). This is the heart of any real-time graphics application.

---

### 3.2 Phaser.js — The Canonical "Moving Sprite" Program

The goal is to render a 2D image (sprite) and move it on key-press.

#### Step 1: Create the Phaser Game Configuration

```javascript
const config = {
  type: Phaser.AUTO,
  width: 800,
  height: 600,
  physics: { default: 'arcade', arcade: { gravity: { y: 0 } } },
  scene: { preload, create, update }
};
new Phaser.Game(config);
```

**Reasoning:** `Phaser.AUTO` lets Phaser choose between Canvas and WebGL at runtime — it tries WebGL first and falls back to Canvas if unavailable. The `scene` object points to three lifecycle functions: `preload` (load assets), `create` (instantiate objects), `update` (run every frame).

#### Step 2: Preload the Asset

```javascript
function preload() {
  this.load.image('player', 'assets/player.png');
}
```

**Reasoning:** The `preload` function runs once at startup. It populates Phaser's asset cache with a key `'player'` bound to an image file.

#### Step 3: Create the Sprite

```javascript
function create() {
  this.player = this.physics.add.sprite(400, 300, 'player');
}
```

**Reasoning:** `physics.add.sprite` (rather than `add.sprite`) automatically attaches an **Arcade Physics body** to the image, giving it `velocity`, `acceleration`, and collision-detection properties for free.

#### Step 4: Handle Input and Update

```javascript
function update() {
  this.player.setVelocity(0, 0);
  if (this.cursors.left.isDown)  this.player.setVelocityX(-160);
  if (this.cursors.right.isDown) this.player.setVelocityX(160);
  if (this.cursors.up.isDown)    this.player.setVelocityY(-160);
  if (this.cursors.down.isDown)  this.player.setVelocityY(160);
}
```

**Reasoning:** The `update` function runs every frame. The velocity reset to zero each frame is a common Phaser idiom — *continuous* key checks override the *momentary* velocity. Velocity of **160** pixels/second is a comfortable walking pace for a 800×600 stage.

---

### 3.3 Babylon.js — The Canonical "Spinning Sphere" Program

#### Step 1: Obtain a Canvas and Create the Engine

```javascript
const canvas = document.getElementById("babylon-canvas");
const engine = new BABYLON.Engine(canvas, true);
```

**Reasoning:** Unlike Three.js, Babylon's `Engine` auto-detects WebGL 1, WebGL 2, or WebGPU based on browser capability. The boolean `true` enables antialiasing.

#### Step 2: Create the Scene

```javascript
const scene = new BABYLON.Scene(engine);
scene.clearColor = new BABYLON.Color4(0.1, 0.1, 0.1, 1.0);
```

**Reasoning:** `Color4(r,g,b,a)` sets the background. Alpha=1 means fully opaque.

#### Step 3: Add the Camera

```javascript
const camera = new BABYLON.ArcRotateCamera("cam", -Math.PI/2, Math.PI/2.5, 5, BABYLON.Vector3.Zero(), scene);
camera.attachControl(canvas, true);
```

**Reasoning:** The `ArcRotateCamera` orbits a target (`Vector3.Zero()`). The user can drag with the mouse to rotate the view, courtesy of `attachControl`.

#### Step 4: Add a Light and a Mesh

```javascript
const light = new BABYLON.HemisphericLight("hemi", new BABYLON.Vector3(0, 1, 0), scene);
const sphere = BABYLON.MeshBuilder.CreateSphere("s", { diameter: 2 }, scene);
```

**Reasoning:** `HemisphericLight` is a cheap, ambient-style light. The `MeshBuilder.CreateSphere` factory function is Babylon's idiomatic alternative to Three.js's `new SphereGeometry()`.

#### Step 5: Start the Render Loop

```javascript
engine.runRenderLoop(() => {
  sphere.rotation.y += 0.01;
  scene.render();
});
window.addEventListener("resize", () => engine.resize());
```

**Reasoning:** `runRenderLoop` is Babylon's built-in equivalent of `requestAnimationFrame`, and it also handles window resize automatically via the `engine.resize()` event listener.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Library

| Scenario | Recommended Library | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Quick 3D product viewer on a marketing page | **Three.js** | Smallest bundle, fastest time-to-prototype. |
| 2D side-scroller mobile game for a hackathon | **Phaser.js** | Built-in physics, sprite handling, and input are unmatched. |
| WebXR experience for Meta Quest 3 | **Babylon.js** | First-class hand-tracking, controller, and passthrough support. |
| Architectural visualization with PBR materials | **Three.js** or **Babylon.js** | Both support PBR; Babylon's Node Material Editor is more powerful. |
| A web-based VR chemistry simulation | **Babylon.js** | GUI system + physics + VR controllers in one package. |
| Retro 8-bit browser game | **Phaser.js** | Canvas renderer is perfect for pixel art. |
| Data visualization (e.g., 3D bar charts) | **Three.js** | Minimal overhead, great examples in the `examples/` folder. |
| Real-time 3D multiplayer browser game | **Babylon.js** | Built-in collision system and Colyseus integration examples. |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
*Define a Scene Graph. How is it implemented in Three.js? (3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
A **Scene Graph** is a hierarchical tree data structure used in computer graphics to organize the spatial and logical relationships between objects in a virtual world. Every node in the tree represents a graphical entity such as a mesh, a light, or a camera, and transformations applied to a parent node are automatically inherited by all its child nodes.

In **Three.js**, the scene graph is implemented via the `THREE.Scene` class, which serves as the root node. A developer instantiates objects like `THREE.Mesh` (a 3D object), `THREE.Light` (an illumination source), or `THREE.Group` (a logical container) and adds them to the scene using the `scene.add()` method. The hierarchical inheritance of transformations is achieved by adding child objects to a parent via `parent.add(child)`. For example, a `THREE.Group` representing a "robot arm" can have its "forearm" mesh as a child, so rotating the parent automatically rotates the child, mimicking the behavior of a real robotic linkage.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
*Mention any three differences between Phaser.js and Three.js. (3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Dimension | Phaser.js | Three.js |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Primary Domain** | 2D browser games | 3D web graphics |
| **Rendering API** | HTML5 Canvas + WebGL | WebGL only (WebGPU in development) |
| **Built-in Physics** | Yes — Arcade Physics and P2.js | No — relies on external libraries like Cannon.js or Ammo.js |
| **Use Case Focus** | Side-scrollers, mobile games, advergames | Product viewers, data visualization, WebXR |
| **Scene Lifecycle** | Explicit `preload`, `create`, `update` functions | A single `requestAnimationFrame` loop |
| **Bundle Size** | ~1.1 MB minified | ~600 KB minified |

Any three of the above differences would earn full marks. The first three are the most commonly expected.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Scene Graph architecture** shared by Three.js, Babylon.js, and Phaser.js. Highlight the role of the **Camera**, **Light**, and **Mesh** in this architecture. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete Three.js program to create a **rotating cube** with a directional light source. Explain every line of code. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution to Question A:

#### Part (a) — Scene Graph Architecture (7 Marks)

The **Scene Graph** is the foundational data structure upon which all three libraries are built. The shared architecture consists of the following nodes:

1. **Scene (Root Node):** The top-level container that holds all graphical entities. Created via `new THREE.Scene()` in Three.js, `new BABYLON.Scene(engine)` in Babylon.js, and `new Phaser.Scene()` in Phaser.js.

2. **Camera Node:** Establishes the viewpoint and the projection type (perspective vs. orthographic). Determines what the user *sees* and how 3D coordinates are mapped to 2D screen pixels. The Perspective Camera parameters — Field of View (FOV), aspect ratio, near plane, and far plane — define the **view frustum**.

3. **Light Nodes:** Without lights, materials using the PBR (Physically Based Rendering) model appear black. Types include DirectionalLight (parallel rays, like the sun), PointLight (omnidirectional), SpotLight (cone-shaped), and HemisphericLight (ambient sky + ground).

4. **Mesh Nodes:** The actual renderable 3D objects. A mesh is the *union* of a **Geometry** (vertex data defining shape) and a **Material** (shader code defining surface appearance).

5. **Group Nodes (Three.js specific):** Used to logically cluster meshes so that transformations can be applied to the group as a whole.

**The Reference Block Diagram:**

```
                       ┌─────────────────────────┐
                       │   Browser Canvas (DOM)  │
                       └────────────┬────────────┘
                                    │
                       ┌────────────▼────────────┐
                       │   Renderer / Engine     │
                       │ (THREE.WebGLRenderer,   │
                       │  BABYLON.Engine,        │
                       │  Phaser.Game)           │
                       └────────────┬────────────┘
                                    │
                       ┌────────────▼────────────┐
                       │       SCENE ROOT        │
                       │  (THREE.Scene /         │
                       │   BABYLON.Scene /       │
                       │   Phaser.Scene)         │
                       └──┬─────────┬─────────┬──┘
                          │         │         │
                ┌─────────▼─┐ ┌─────▼───┐ ┌───▼─────────┐
                │  CAMERA   │ │ LIGHTS  │ │    MESHES   │
                │  NODE     │ │  NODES  │ │    NODES    │
                └─────┬─────┘ └────┬────┘ └──┬──────┬───┘
                      │            │         │      │
                      │            │      ┌──▼──┐ ┌─▼────┐
                      │            │      │Geom.│ │Mater.│
                      │            │      └─────┘ └──────┘
                      │            │
                      ▼            ▼
              ┌────────────────────────────────┐
              │   GPU: Vertex & Fragment       │
              │   Shaders → Frame Buffer       │
              └────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Mark Distribution Hint:** Diagram (3 marks), explanation of Camera/Light/Mesh (3 marks), conclusion about the shared pipeline (1 mark).

---

#### Part (b) — Three.js Rotating Cube Program (7 Marks)

```html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head><title>Three.js Cube</title></head>
<body>
  <canvas id="c"></canvas>
  <script type="module">
    import * as THREE from 'https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/three@0.160.0/build/three.module.js';

    // 1. Scene, Camera, Renderer
    const scene = new THREE.Scene();
    const camera = new THREE.PerspectiveCamera(75, innerWidth/innerHeight, 0.1, 1000);
    const renderer = new THREE.WebGLRenderer({ canvas: document.getElementById("c") });
    renderer.setSize(innerWidth, innerHeight);

    // 2. Geometry, Material, Mesh
    const geometry = new THREE.BoxGeometry(1, 1, 1);
    const material = new THREE.MeshStandardMaterial({ color: 0x44aa88 });
    const cube = new THREE.Mesh(geometry, material);
    scene.add(cube);

    // 3. Lighting
    const light = new THREE.DirectionalLight(0xffffff, 1);
    light.position.set(2, 2, 5);
    scene.add(light);

    // 4. Camera Position
    camera.position.z = 5;

    // 5. Animation Loop
    function animate() {
      requestAnimationFrame(animate);
      cube.rotation.x += 0.01;
      cube.rotation.y += 0.01;
      renderer.render(scene, camera);
    }
    animate();
  </script>
</body>
</html>
```

**Line-by-Line Explanation:**

- **Line `const scene = new THREE.Scene();`** — Creates the empty scene-graph root.
- **Line `PerspectiveCamera(75, ...)`** — A 75° FOV camera with a near plane of 0.1 and a far plane of 1000 units.
- **Line `WebGLRenderer({canvas})`** — Attaches the renderer to the existing canvas; if you omit `canvas`, it creates one.
- **Line `BoxGeometry(1,1,1)`** — Creates a 1×1×1 unit cube.
- **Line `MeshStandardMaterial({color: 0x44aa88})`** — A PBR material that responds to lights. The hex `0x44aa88` is a teal color.
- **Line `THREE.Mesh(geometry, material)`** — Couples the geometry and material into a renderable object.
- **Line `scene.add(cube)`** — Attaches the cube to the scene graph so it is rendered.
- **Line `DirectionalLight(0xffffff, 1)`** — A white parallel-ray light of unit intensity.
- **Line `light.position.set(2,2,5)`** — Positions the light 2 units right, 2 up, 5 forward.
- **Line `camera.position.z = 5`** — Pulls the camera 5 units back so the cube is in view.
- **Line `requestAnimationFrame(animate)`** — Schedules the next frame in sync with the browser's repaint cycle.
- **Line `cube.rotation.x += 0.01`** — Increments the rotation by 0.01 radians per frame on the X axis.
- **Line `renderer.render(scene, camera)`** — Issues the WebGL draw call for this frame.

**Mark Distribution Hint:** Correct program syntax (4 marks), line-by-line explanation (3 marks).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:

**(a)** Compare **Three.js** and **Babylon.js** in terms of their origin, license, rendering engine, built-in physics support, and WebXR capabilities. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a Phaser.js program that loads a sprite, displays it on screen, and moves it left/right/up/down using the **arrow keys**. Explain the Phaser **scene lifecycle**. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution to Question B:

#### Part (a) — Three.js vs Babylon.js (7 Marks)

| Parameter | Three.js | Babylon.js |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Origin & Year** | Ricardo Cabello (Mr.doob), 2010 | Microsoft + community, 2013 |
| **License** | **MIT** | **Apache 2.0** |
| **Language** | JavaScript (with TypeScript definitions) | TypeScript-first |
| **Rendering Engine** | WebGL 1/2, experimental WebGPU | WebGL 1/2, **production-ready WebGPU** |
| **Built-in Physics** | None — integrates Cannon.js, Ammo.js, Rapier | **Cannon, Oimo, Ammo, Havok** (plugin architecture) |
| **WebXR / VR Support** | Available via `WebXR` module, community-driven | **First-class** with controller and hand-tracking APIs |
| **GUI System** | None — use `dat.GUI` or `leva` | **Babylon GUI** built-in |
| **Material Editor** | External: drei, leva | **Node Material Editor** (visual shader graph) |
| **Bundle Size** | ~600 KB | ~2 MB |
| **Best For** | Lightweight 3D, data viz | Full game engine, VR, simulations |

**Key Takeaway:** Three.js is the *minimalist craftsman* — you assemble your own architecture from small components. Babylon.js is the *enterprise powerhouse* — batteries are included.

---

#### Part (b) — Phaser.js Sprite Movement Program (7 Marks)

```javascript
const config = {
  type: Phaser.AUTO,
  width: 800,
  height: 600,
  physics: { default: 'arcade', arcade: { gravity: { y: 0 } } },
  scene: { preload: preload, create: create, update: update }
};
const game = new Phaser.Game(config);

function preload() {
  this.load.image('player', 'assets/player.png');
}

function create() {
  this.player = this.physics.add.sprite(400, 300, 'player');
  this.cursors = this.input.keyboard.createCursorKeys();
}

function update() {
  this.player.setVelocity(0);
  if (this.cursors.left.isDown)  this.player.setVelocityX(-160);
  if (this.cursors.right.isDown) this.player.setVelocityX(160);
  if (this.cursors.up.isDown)    this.player.setVelocityY(-160);
  if (this.cursors.down.isDown)  this.player.setVelocityY(160);
}
```

**The Phaser Scene Lifecycle (3-Mark Sub-Explanation):**

1. **`preload()`** — Called *once* when the scene starts. Used to load images, sounds, fonts, and tilemaps into the asset cache. The `this` context refers to the current scene.
2. **`create()`** — Called *once* after `preload()` completes. Used to instantiate sprites, set up cameras, create UI, and initialize game state.
3. **`update()`** — Called *every frame* (typically 60 times per second). Used to handle input, update positions, check collisions, and run game logic.

This three-phase lifecycle (`preload → create → update`) is one of the most distinctive features of Phaser.js and is what differentiates it from Three.js's flat `requestAnimationFrame` pattern.

**Mark Distribution Hint:** Phaser program correctness (4 marks), lifecycle explanation (3 marks).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to import the library as a module** — When using ES6 `import * as THREE from '...'`, students often forget the `type="module"` attribute on the `<script>` tag. This causes a `ReferenceError: THREE is not defined`.
> 2. **Blocking CORS / `file://` errors** — Browsers refuse to load ES modules from local files. Students must run a *local HTTP server* (Live Server, `python -m http.server`, or `npx http-server`). Failing to do so is the **#1 reason** lab programs fail on the evaluation machine.
> 3. **Confusing `requestAnimationFrame` with `setInterval`** — `setInterval` is *not* synchronized with the browser's repaint cycle. Using it causes stuttering and dropped frames. Always use `requestAnimationFrame`.
> 4. **Not setting the canvas size** — A common bug is rendering into a canvas of size 0×0, resulting in a blank black page. Always call `renderer.setSize(width, height)`.
> 5. **Mixing up coordinate systems** — In Three.js and Babylon.js, the Y axis points **up** by default. In Phaser.js, the Y axis points **down** (screen coordinates). Forgetting this leads to the sprite moving the wrong way or appearing upside-down.
> 6. **Confusing `Phaser.AUTO` with `Phaser.WEBGL`** — `Phaser.AUTO` is the safer choice. If you force `Phaser.WEBGL` and the user's browser lacks WebGL, the game will fail to start.
> 7. **Forgetting `arcade` in the physics config** — Writing `physics: { default: 'arcade' }` *without* the nested `arcade: { ... }` block throws a runtime warning that students frequently miss.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Lab Exam:**

- [ ] **Three.js** is a 3D rendering library, **Phaser.js** is a 2D game framework, **Babylon.js** is a full 3D engine.
- [ ] All three are written in **JavaScript** and run inside a **web browser** without plug-ins.
- [ ] The **Scene Graph** is the common architectural foundation of all three libraries.
- [ ] A **Mesh** = **Geometry** + **Material**. Remember this equation by heart.
- [ ] The **animation loop** is powered by `requestAnimationFrame`, NOT `setInterval`.
- [ ] Three.js uses `THREE.WebGLRenderer`; Babylon uses `BABYLON.Engine`; Phaser uses `Phaser.Game` with `Phaser.AUTO`.
- [ ] **Lighting is mandatory** for PBR materials like `MeshStandardMaterial` — without it, the mesh appears black.
- [ ] **PerspectiveCamera** parameters: **FOV** (degrees), **aspect ratio**, **near plane**, **far plane**.
- [ ] Phaser's scene lifecycle: **`preload` → `create` → `update`**.
- [ ] Babylon.js has **first-class WebXR** support; Three.js has it via a separate module.
- [ ] You **must serve your project over HTTP** — the `file://` protocol blocks ES module loading.
- [ ] Three.js license = **MIT**; Phaser.js license = **MIT**; Babylon.js license = **Apache 2.0**.
- [ ] Coordinate system: Y is **up** in Three.js and Babylon.js; Y is **down** in Phaser.js.
- [ ] Built-in physics: **None** in Three.js, **Arcade + P2.js** in Phaser.js, **Cannon/Oimo/Ammo/Havok** in Babylon.js.
- [ ] The canonical "Hello World" projects: rotating **cube** (Three.js), moving **sprite** (Phaser.js), spinning **sphere** (Babylon.js).
- [ ] For VR/AR in the browser, prefer **Babylon.js**; for quick 3D on a webpage, prefer **Three.js**; for 2D games, prefer **Phaser.js**.
