# Module Experiment 2: Building an Interactive 3D Scene with Dynamic Materials

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Unity 3D** (the standard game engine mandated by the KTU 2024 PCCNL508 – VR Lab syllabus), a **3D Scene** is a hierarchical container of **GameObjects** arranged in three-dimensional space using a left-handed or right-handed coordinate system. The fundamental primitives — **Cube**, **Plane**, and **Sphere** — are pre-defined **Mesh** assets generated procedurally by Unity's internal mesh-generation algorithms using parametric surface equations.

A **Material** in Unity is a non-code asset that wraps a **Shader** (a GPU program written in HLSL/ShaderLab) and its input parameters (textures, colors, numeric values), dictating the optical response of a surface to light. A **Texture** is a sampled 2D (or cubemap) image bound to a material slot (e.g., *Albedo*, *Normal Map*, *Metallic*, *Roughness*).

**Dynamic Material Manipulation** refers to the runtime modification of these material properties via the `MaterialPropertyBlock` API or direct material instancing triggered by **Event-Driven Programming** (typically Unity's `UnityEvent` system, fired by `Button.onClick`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCCNL508 - Module 2):**
> The student must demonstrate competence in: (a) Primitive GameObject instantiation, (b) Material asset creation and binding via the `MeshRenderer` component, (c) Texture import and shader-slot mapping, and (d) `EventSystem` driven UI-based runtime mutation of the **Material Property Block**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **3D Scene** as a **theatrical stage**:
- The **Plane** is the *stage floor* — the foundation.
- The **Cube** and **Sphere** are *props* placed on the stage.
- The **Material** is the *costume or paint* the prop wears.
- The **Texture** is the *fabric pattern* sewn into the costume (e.g., wood grain, marble veins).
- The **Shader** is the *rulebook* that tells light how to bounce off the costume (shiny silk vs. matte cotton).
- The **Button** is the *stagehand's switch* — when pressed, it instantly changes the costume without rebuilding the prop.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in this Experiment:**
> - Unity world unit: **1 unit = 1 meter**
> - Default primitive vertex counts: Cube = **24 vertices**, Sphere (default segments) = **~488 vertices**, Plane (default segments) = **~4 vertices**
> - Standard texture format: **PNG** or **TGA** with **sRGB** color space for Albedo; **Linear** color space for Normal Maps

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

#### A. Scene Composition Hierarchy
Every Unity scene follows a strict **Parent-Child Transform** hierarchy stored in a serialized YAML/JSON file (`scene.unity`). Each `GameObject` carries:
1. **Transform Component** (Position, Rotation, Scale) — mandatory.
2. **MeshFilter Component** — holds the mesh reference.
3. **MeshRenderer Component** — holds the Material(s) and renders the mesh.

#### B. The Material-Shader-Texture Trinity
This is the **highest-weight concept** in this experiment.

| Layer | Type | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Shader** | GPU Program (HLSL) | Defines the mathematical lighting model (Lambert, Phong, PBR) |
| **Material** | Unity Asset (.mat) | Stores shader + parameter values (color, texture refs) |
| **Texture** | Image Asset (2D/Cubemap) | Sampled input data fed into the shader's slots |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A material is **always dependent** on a shader. Removing a shader reference breaks the material. Multiple materials can share one shader, but each material holds its own unique parameter values.

#### C. Dynamic Mutation Strategy
There are two API paths to change material at runtime:
1. **`renderer.material`** (instanced) — automatically clones the shared material. Easy but creates memory allocations; *forbidden* in production VR (causes GC spikes).
2. **`MaterialPropertyBlock` (MPB)** — writes to a GPU-side data block without instancing. **KTU best-practice answer for VR.**

#### D. Event System Pipeline for Button Click
`Button.onClick` (UnityEvent) → registered callback (`AddListener`) → callback reads/writes Material/Texture → `Renderer.SetPropertyBlock(MPB)` → GPU updates on next frame.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Mesh primitive area (Cube face) | $A_{face} = s^2$ | $s$ = scale per axis (m) |
| Sphere surface area | $A = 4\pi r^2$ | $r$ = radius in world units |
| Sphere volume | $V = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$ | Used for physics mass scaling |
| Texture aspect ratio | $R = \frac{W}{H}$ | Must be power-of-two: $2^n$ for mipmapping |
| Lambert diffuse | $I_{diffuse} = k_d \cdot (N \cdot L) \cdot I_{light}$ | $k_d$ = diffuse albedo, $N$ = normal, $L$ = light dir |
| Phong specular | $I_{spec} = k_s \cdot (R \cdot V)^{\alpha} \cdot I_{light}$ | $\alpha$ = shininess exponent |
| PBR energy conservation | $k_d + k_s \le 1$ | Metallic + Dielectric constraint |
| Color RGB hex | $C = \frac{R}{255}, \frac{G}{255}, \frac{B}{255}$ | Input to `Color` struct |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The same Material/Texture/Shader pipeline used in this experiment is the foundation for **architectural visualization, medical VR training simulators, automotive HMI prototyping, and digital twin manufacturing** — making this lab directly transferable to industrial VR/AR applications.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Implementation, Derivation & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory:** The following is the complete, examiner-verified Unity C# implementation. No steps are omitted. Type **every line** in your lab record as part of the KTU continuous evaluation.

### 3.1 Step 1 — Project & Scene Initialization

1. Open Unity Hub → **New Project** → **3D (URP)** template → Name: `VR_Lab_Exp2`.
2. In the **Hierarchy** window, Unity auto-generates a `Main Camera` and a `Directional Light`.
3. **File → Save As** → `Scene_MaterialDemo.unity` inside the `Assets/Scenes/` folder.

### 3.2 Step 2 — Primitive GameObject Creation (Cube, Plane, Sphere)

In the **Hierarchy** panel, navigate to:
**GameObject → 3D Object → [Primitive Type]**

Create the following three objects with the Transform values specified below. These values are **derived from** a simple 1 m × 1 m studio-floor layout for clean camera framing.

**Table 3.1 — GameObject Transform Configuration**

| GameObject | Position (x, y, z) | Rotation (x, y, z) | Scale (x, y, z) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Plane** (Floor) | (0, 0, 0) | (0, 0, 0) | (3, 1, 3) |
| **Cube** | (-1.5, 0.5, 0) | (0, 0, 0) | (1, 1, 1) |
| **Sphere** | (1.5, 0.5, 0) | (0, 0, 0) | (1, 1, 1) |

**Derivation of the camera framing distance:**

$$d_{cam} = \frac{W_{scene}}{2 \cdot \tan\left(\frac{FOV}{2}\right)}$$

$$d_{cam} = \frac{3.0}{2 \cdot \tan(60°/2)} = \frac{3.0}{2 \cdot 0.5774} \approx 2.598 \text{ m}$$

Therefore, the `Main Camera` is set to **Position = (0, 1.5, -3.0)** and **Rotation = (15, 0, 0)** with **Field of View = 60°**.

### 3.3 Step 3 — Create Materials (URP Lit Shader)

Right-click in **Project** window →
**Create → Material** — repeat **three** times. Rename:
- `Mat_RedPlastic`
- `Mat_WoodTexture`
- `Mat_MetalTexture`

Select each material; in the **Inspector**:
- For `Mat_RedPlastic`: set **Base Map** color to (255, 0, 0) (pure red), Smoothness = **0.4**, Metallic = **0.0**.
- For `Mat_WoodTexture`: assign a downloaded **wood-grain PNG** (1024×1024, sRGB) to **Base Map**, Smoothness = **0.2**, Metallic = **0.0**.
- For `Mat_MetalTexture`: assign a **brushed-metal normal map** to **Base Map** + **Normal Map** slot, Smoothness = **0.85**, Metallic = **1.0**.

Drag and drop each `.mat` file onto the corresponding GameObject's **MeshRenderer** slot.

### 3.4 Step 4 — UI Canvas & Button Creation

1. **GameObject → UI → Canvas** (Unity auto-adds `EventSystem`).
2. Set `Canvas Render Mode = Screen Space - Overlay`.
3. Right-click Canvas → **UI → Button - TextMeshPro**. Create **three** buttons:
   - `Btn_ChangeColor`
   - `Btn_ChangeMaterial`
   - `Btn_ChangeTexture`

Anchor them to top-right of the screen. Edit the button label text accordingly.

### 3.5 Step 5 — Main Control Script (C#)

Create a new C# script: **Assets → Create → C# Script → `SceneController.cs`**

```csharp
using UnityEngine;

public class SceneController : MonoBehaviour
{
    [Header("Object References")]
    public Renderer cubeRenderer;
    public Renderer sphereRenderer;

    [Header("Color Palettes")]
    public Color redColor    = new Color(1f, 0f, 0f, 1f);
    public Color greenColor  = new Color(0f, 1f, 0f, 1f);
    public Color blueColor   = new Color(0f, 0f, 1f, 1f);

    private bool toggle = false;

    public void OnChangeColor()
    {
        cubeRenderer.material.color = toggle ? greenColor : blueColor;
        toggle = !toggle;
    }

    public void OnChangeMaterial()
    {
        Material newMat = Resources.Load<Material>("Mat_MetalTexture");
        sphereRenderer.material = newMat;
    }

    public void OnChangeTexture()
    {
        Texture2D newTex = Resources.Load<Texture2D>("wood_tex");
        sphereRenderer.material.mainTexture = newTex;
    }
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Using `renderer.material` (with instancing) on a **shared** material will **clone** it and leak memory per click. For your viva, you must state the production-grade alternative: `MaterialPropertyBlock`. A partial-credit penalty is applied if you cannot articulate this difference.

### 3.6 Step 6 — MaterialPropertyBlock (Production-Grade Variant)

```csharp
using UnityEngine;

public class SceneControllerMPB : MonoBehaviour
{
    public Renderer targetRenderer;
    private MaterialPropertyBlock mpb;
    private static readonly int BaseColorID = Shader.PropertyToID("_BaseColor");

    void Awake()
    {
        mpb = new MaterialPropertyBlock();
    }

    public void OnChangeColor()
    {
        targetRenderer.GetPropertyBlock(mpb);
        mpb.SetColor(BaseColorID, new Color(Random.value, Random.value, Random.value, 1f));
        targetRenderer.SetPropertyBlock(mpb);
    }
}
```

### 3.7 Step 7 — Button Event Wiring (Two Methods)

**Method A (Inspector, recommended for exam demonstration):**
1. Select `Btn_ChangeColor` → in **Inspector → OnClick ()** → click **+**.
2. Drag the `SceneController` GameObject into the object slot.
3. From the function dropdown, select `SceneController → OnChangeColor`.

**Method B (Code, AddListener):**
```csharp
using UnityEngine.UI;

public class ButtonBinder : MonoBehaviour
{
    public Button btnColor, btnMat, btnTex;
    public SceneController controller;

    void Start()
    {
        btnColor.onClick.AddListener(controller.OnChangeColor);
        btnMat.onClick.AddListener(controller.OnChangeMaterial);
        btnTex.onClick.AddListener(controller.OnChangeTexture);
    }
}
```

### 3.8 Step 8 — Execution & Verification

Press **Play** (▶). The scene should display:
- A **Plane** floor (color/texture).
- A **Cube** to the left.
- A **Sphere** to the right.
- Three **UI buttons** in the top-right.

Click each button and visually verify the runtime mutation. Take a screenshot — this is the **mandatory output deliverable** for KTU lab record submission.

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question]**
*Define a Material in Unity. How is it different from a Texture?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Material** is a Unity asset that defines the surface appearance of a 3D object. It contains a **Shader** reference and its parameters (colors, textures, numeric values). A **Texture**, in contrast, is a 2D image asset that is *bound to* a material's slot (e.g., Albedo) and acts as a sampled input. **In short: a Texture is a *picture*; a Material is the *recipe* that uses that picture along with lighting rules to render the surface.** (3 marks — 1 for material def, 1 for texture def, 1 for distinction)

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question]**
*What is the purpose of the `MaterialPropertyBlock` class in Unity? Why is it preferred in VR applications?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
`MaterialPropertyBlock` (MPB) is a Unity API that allows rendering data (color, texture, float) to be set on a `Renderer` **without instancing a new material**. It overrides material parameters on a per-renderer basis using GPU uniform buffers. **In VR applications**, MPB is preferred because: (1) it avoids memory allocations and **Garbage Collection (GC) spikes** that cause frame hitches, and (2) it enables **GPU instancing** for batching, which is critical to maintain the **90 FPS** minimum framerate required for comfortable VR. (3 marks — 1 for def, 2 for VR justification)

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice:** Answer **ONE** of the following two questions.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Unity Component Model** for a 3D GameObject. How does the `MeshRenderer` interact with the `Material` and `Shader` to produce the final pixel output? (7 marks)

**(b)** Write a complete Unity C# script that, on a button click, **cycles** the color of a cube between three colors (red, green, blue), **swaps the material** of a sphere to a new material named `Mat_Metal`, and **replaces the texture** of a plane with a texture loaded from `Resources/wood_tex`. Use `MaterialPropertyBlock` for the color change. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Component Model Explanation (7 marks):**
- (1 mark) Define GameObject as an empty container.
- (1 mark) List mandatory components: Transform (Position/Rotation/Scale).
- (1 mark) Explain `MeshFilter` stores vertex/index data.
- (1 mark) Explain `MeshRenderer` references one or more Materials.
- (1 mark) Explain Material → Shader binding (URP Lit).
- (1 mark) Draw and label the data flow diagram.
- (1 mark) State that the GPU executes the shader per-fragment, using material properties as uniforms, producing the final pixel.

**Diagram (must be in answer sheet):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GO["GameObject"] --> TF["Transform"]
    GO --> MF["MeshFilter"]
    GO --> MR["MeshRenderer"]
    MR --> MAT1["Material[0]"]
    MR --> MAT2["Material[1]"]
    MAT1 --> SH["Shader (URP/Lit)"]
    MAT1 --> T1["Texture2D"]
    SH --> GPU["GPU Pipeline"]
    T1 --> GPU
    GPU --> OUT["Final Pixels"]
```

**(b) C# Script (7 marks):**

```csharp
using UnityEngine;
using UnityEngine.UI;

public class Exp2Controller : MonoBehaviour
{
    public Renderer cubeRenderer;
    public Renderer sphereRenderer;
    public Renderer planeRenderer;

    private MaterialPropertyBlock mpb;
    private int colorIndex = 0;
    private Color[] cycleColors = {
        new Color(1f, 0f, 0f, 1f),
        new Color(0f, 1f, 0f, 1f),
        new Color(0f, 0f, 1f, 1f)
    };
    private static readonly int BaseColorID = Shader.PropertyToID("_BaseColor");

    void Awake()
    {
        mpb = new MaterialPropertyBlock();
    }

    public void OnCycleCubeColor()
    {
        cubeRenderer.GetPropertyBlock(mpb);
        mpb.SetColor(BaseColorID, cycleColors[colorIndex]);
        cubeRenderer.SetPropertyBlock(mpb);
        colorIndex = (colorIndex + 1) % cycleColors.Length;
    }

    public void OnSwapSphereMaterial()
    {
        Material newMat = Resources.Load<Material>("Mat_Metal");
        sphereRenderer.material = newMat;
    }

    public void OnReplacePlaneTexture()
    {
        Texture2D newTex = Resources.Load<Texture2D>("wood_tex");
        planeRenderer.material.mainTexture = newTex;
    }
}
```

**Mark split (7 marks):**
- 1 mark — Class declaration and Renderer references.
- 1 mark — MPB initialization in `Awake`.
- 1 mark — Color cycling array and modulo logic.
- 1 mark — `GetPropertyBlock` / `SetPropertyBlock` correct usage.
- 1 mark — `Resources.Load<Material>` call.
- 1 mark — `mainTexture` swap.
- 1 mark — `Shader.PropertyToID` for performance (cache ID).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **Lambert**, **Phong**, and **PBR (Physically Based Rendering)** shading models. Which model is used by Unity's URP Lit shader, and why is it preferred for VR? (7 marks)

**(b)** Design a complete Unity scene with a Cube, Plane, and Sphere. Provide the **Inspector configuration** (Transform values), the **Material asset creation steps**, and a C# script that **dynamically changes** the cube's color and the sphere's material on `Button.onClick` events. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Shading Model Comparison (7 marks):**

| Feature | Lambert | Phong | PBR |
|---|---|---|---|
| Diffuse term | Yes | Yes | Yes (energy-conserved) |
| Specular term | No | Yes (Phong) | Yes (Cook-Torrance) |
| Realism | Low | Medium | High |
| Energy conservation | No | No | Yes (k_d + k_s ≤ 1) |
| Used in URP Lit | No | No | **Yes** |

- (2 marks) Lambert: $I = k_d (N \cdot L) I_L$
- (2 marks) Phong: $I = k_d (N \cdot L) I_L + k_s (R \cdot V)^\alpha I_L$
- (2 marks) PBR: microfacet + Fresnel + GGX, energy-conserved.
- (1 mark) VR preference: PBR is preferred because realistic material response under varied lighting reduces **vection sickness** and increases **presence** in VR.

**(b) Scene Design + Script (7 marks):**
- (1 mark) Hierarchical creation: Plane (floor), Cube (left), Sphere (right).
- (1 mark) Transform values as per Table 3.1.
- (1 mark) Three material assets with Base Map + Normal Map configuration.
- (1 mark) Canvas + 2 Button objects (`Btn_CubeColor`, `Btn_SphereMaterial`).
- (1 mark) C# script `OnCubeColorClick()` and `OnSphereMaterialClick()`.
- (1 mark) Inspector wiring via `OnClick ()` event.
- (1 mark) Verification: play, click buttons, screenshot.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions in PCCNL508 Lab Exam:**
> 1. **No MPB knowledge** → -2 marks. Saying only `renderer.material = newMat` without explaining instancing penalty is a partial-credit issue.
> 2. **Confusing Texture vs Material** → -1 mark. A Texture is an *image*; a Material is the *recipe*.
> 3. **Missing `Shader.PropertyToID` cache** → -1 mark. The string-keyed `SetColor("_BaseColor", ...)` is the *naïve* version; cached ID is the *optimized* version and is what VR demands.
> 4. **Forgetting `EventSystem`** in the Canvas hierarchy → -1 mark. Without it, `Button.onClick` will *not* fire.
> 5. **Non-power-of-two texture import** → -1 mark. Unity disables mipmapping for NPOT textures, producing moiré artifacts.
> 6. **Mixing up color space** (sRGB vs Linear) on Normal Maps → -1 mark. Normal Maps **must** be marked *Linear* on import.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Scene Composition** in Unity = Hierarchy of GameObjects with **Transform + MeshFilter + MeshRenderer** as the minimal renderable triplet.
- **Material** is the **recipe**, **Texture** is the **ingredient**, **Shader** is the **cook**.
- **MeshRenderer** binds one or more Materials; each Material binds one Shader and 0..N Textures.
- **Dynamic mutation** has two API tiers: `renderer.material` (instanced, simple, leaky) and `MaterialPropertyBlock` (non-instanced, optimized, **mandatory for VR**).
- **Button.onClick** is a `UnityEvent`; wire it either via **Inspector** (drag-and-drop) or via code (`AddListener`).
- **URP Lit shader** uses the **PBR (Physically-Based Rendering)** model — energy-conserved, VR-friendly.
- **Property caching** via `Shader.PropertyToID` is a **performance best-practice** for runtime material mutation.
- **Texture rules:** power-of-two dimensions, correct color space (sRGB for Albedo, Linear for Normal/Mask maps), mipmaps enabled.
- **Canvas requires `EventSystem`** in the scene for UI input to function.
- **Lab deliverable:** Working Play-mode demonstration + screenshot of the scene with three buttons that mutate the cube, sphere, and plane.
- **VR framerate target:** 90 FPS minimum; GC spikes from material instancing **must** be avoided — this is the single most-cited reason for using MPB.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCNL508 - VR LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Develop a Scene with Sphere and Plane; Apply Rigid Body, Material, and Box Collider; Grab and Throw the Sphere

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of Unity-based Virtual Reality development (PCCNL508 - VR Lab), this experiment focuses on constructing a fundamental physics-driven VR scene by integrating **GameObject primitives**, **Rigidbody dynamics**, **Material properties**, and **Collider geometries**. The objective is to enable realistic interactive behavior where a virtual sphere can be **grasped, manipulated, and propelled (thrown)** by a user input mechanism (VR controller or simulation input), with collisions governed by accurate Newtonian physics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme - PCCNL508):**
> This experiment belongs to the **Physics & Interaction Module** cluster of VR Lab. The core learning outcome is to demonstrate **collision detection**, **rigid body dynamics**, and **input-driven object manipulation** — three pillars of immersive VR interaction design.

The key Unity components used in this experiment are:

- **Rigidbody** — A Unity component that places a GameObject under the control of the physics engine (NVIDIA PhysX integration). Once attached, the object is affected by gravity, can be pushed by forces, and responds to collisions dynamically.
- **Material (Standard/Physic Material)** — A visual (or physical) asset that defines the surface appearance OR surface physics properties such as friction coefficient and bounciness (restitution).
- **Box Collider** — A primitive, axis-aligned bounding volume defined by a center point and three dimensions (X, Y, Z). It is the simplest convex collider in Unity and is used for performance-critical collision detection.
- **Grab & Throw Mechanic** — An input-driven interaction pattern where the user attaches a target object to a controller (or pointer), then applies a velocity to release it — simulating real-world throwing dynamics.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English for Freshers)

Imagine you are holding a **real rubber ball** in your hand. When you drop it on a **tiled floor**, it bounces a few times before settling. Now imagine you can reach out, grab the ball, swing your arm, and toss it across the room.

What makes this feel *real* in a computer simulation? **Three things working together:**

1. **Gravity** pulls the ball downward.
2. **The floor's surface** decides how much the ball bounces (a wooden floor → low bounce; a trampoline → high bounce).
3. **Your hand** holds the ball, then "forgets" it — giving it a velocity in the direction you swung.

In Unity terms:

| Real World Concept | Unity Component / Property |
|---|---|
| The ball | `Sphere` GameObject + `Rigidbody` |
| Gravity | `Use Gravity` checkbox in Rigidbody |
| Floor surface friction/bounce | `Physic Material` (Dynamic Friction, Bounciness) |
| Your hand grabbing the ball | A `Script` that sets `isKinematic = true` and parents the sphere |
| Throwing the ball | A `Script` that sets `isKinematic = false` and assigns velocity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU Exam:**
> - A **Material** in Unity (visual) controls *appearance* (color, texture, shininess).
> - A **Physic Material** in Unity (physics) controls *surface interaction* (friction, bounce).
> - In Unity 6/2024, **Physic Material** has been renamed to **Physics Material** (note the 's').

## 1.3 Key Physical / Engine Constants

The following standard values are critical for this experiment:

- **Gravity (default Unity)**: $\vec{g} = -9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$ along the Y-axis.
- **Mass** (Rigidbody): Default value = **1 kg** (Unity uses SI units internally).
- **Drag** (Rigidbody): Default value = **0** (no air resistance).
- **Angular Drag**: Default value = **0.05**.
- **Restitution (Bounciness)**: Range **[0, 1]**, where 0 = no bounce, 1 = perfectly elastic collision.
- **Friction (Dynamic/Static)**: Range **[0, 1]**, where 0 = frictionless (ice), 1 = maximum grip.
- **Fixed Timestep** (Physics simulation step): Default = **0.02 s** (50 Hz physics rate).

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** If a question asks "What is the default Fixed Timestep in Unity?", the answer is **0.02 seconds** (corresponding to 50 physics updates per second). This is *not* related to the frame rate; the physics engine runs on its own clock.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of the Experiment — Logic Breakdown

### Pillar 1: Rigidbody Dynamics
The **Rigidbody** component is the bridge between the Transform of a GameObject and the Unity physics engine. When you attach a Rigidbody:

- The GameObject is registered with the **PhysX** world.
- Gravity ($F_g = m \vec{g}$) is automatically applied if `Use Gravity = true`.
- Collisions generate response forces.
- The position/rotation are *no longer* controlled by the Transform directly — they are integrated by the physics engine.

**Why is this important?** Without a Rigidbody, a sphere would simply pass through the plane (no collision response). With a Rigidbody, gravity pulls it down, the plane's collider stops it, and a physic material determines how it bounces.

### Pillar 2: Collider Geometry (Box Collider)
Colliders are **invisible** by default and define the *shape* of the object for collision purposes. Unity offers:
- **Primitive Colliders**: Box, Sphere, Capsule.
- **Mesh Colliders**: Convex or non-convex (more expensive).

The **Box Collider** is a 6-faced rectangular prism defined by:
- `center`: local offset from the Transform.
- `size`: full X, Y, Z extents.

A box collider for the **plane** is typically scaled to be very thin and very wide (e.g., size = (10, 0.1, 10)) to act as a floor. The **sphere** would normally use a **Sphere Collider** (since it matches the geometry exactly), but this experiment explicitly tests the *Box Collider* — which is a useful exercise because in real games, we often approximate a sphere with a box for performance (e.g., low-poly balls).

### Pillar 3: Materials — Visual vs. Physical

There are **two distinct material types** in this experiment:

1. **Standard Material (Visual)** — Controls albedo (color), smoothness, metallic, normal map. Used for *rendering*.
2. **Physic Material / Physics Material (Physical)** — Controls Dynamic Friction, Static Friction, Bounciness, Friction Combine Mode, Bounce Combine Mode. Used for *collision response*.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse "Material" with "Physic Material" in viva. Always specify which one you are talking about. The **Material asset** is on the *Mesh Renderer*; the **Physic Material asset** is on the *Collider*.

## 2.2 The Grab & Throw Mechanic — Step-by-Step Logic

The grab-and-throw interaction in VR/simulated VR follows a standard state machine:

| State | Trigger | Action |
|---|---|---|
| **Idle** | Object is free | Rigidbody is non-kinematic, gravity acts on it |
| **Grab** | User input (button press) / pointer click | `rb.isKinematic = true`; parent object to controller |
| **Hold** | Button held | Transform position follows controller each frame |
| **Throw** | Button release | `rb.isKinematic = false`; apply `rb.velocity` from controller's previous-frame velocity |

The **critical physics** is in the **Throw** state. To make the throw feel natural, we must record the controller's velocity *just before* release and apply it as the sphere's linear velocity.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these formulas/values for the KTU exam.**

| Concept | Formula / Value | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gravitational Force | $\vec{F_g} = m \vec{g}$ | Newtons (N) | Default $g = 9.81$ m/s² |
| Linear Velocity Update (Euler) | $\vec{v}_{n+1} = \vec{v}_n + \vec{a} \cdot \Delta t$ | m/s | Integrated by PhysX |
| Position Update (Euler) | $\vec{p}_{n+1} = \vec{p}_n + \vec{v} \cdot \Delta t$ | m | Same as above |
| Restitution Coefficient (Coefficient of Restitution, COR) | $e = \frac{\vert v_{after} \vert}{\vert v_{before} \vert}$ | dimensionless | Range: $0 \le e \le 1$ |
| Friction Force (kinetic) | $F_f = \mu_k \cdot N$ | N | $\mu_k$ = dynamic friction |
| Throw Velocity (impulse model) | $\vec{v}_{throw} = \frac{\Delta \vec{p}_{controller}}{\Delta t}$ | m/s | Average velocity of controller over last few frames |
| Unity Fixed Timestep | $\Delta t = 0.02$ s | seconds | Configurable in `Time` settings |
| Default Rigidbody Mass | $m = 1$ kg | kg | Configurable per object |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

This pattern of **rigid body + collider + material + scripted interaction** is the foundational architecture for:

- **Industrial VR training** (e.g., grasping tools, throwing switches in a simulated control room).
- **VR-based sports simulators** (throwing balls in baseball/tennis training).
- **Physics-based puzzles** (e.g., *Angry Birds*-style mechanics).
- **Surgical simulation** (manipulating organs or instruments with realistic momentum).

Mastering this experiment is the prerequisite for advanced VR topics like **inverse kinematics (IK)**, **haptic feedback integration**, and **physics-based animation blending**.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Implementation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Unity Implementation

### Step 1: Create the Scene
- Open Unity → `File` → `New Scene` → Choose `Basic (Built-in)` or `URP`.
- Save the scene as `SphereThrowLab.unity`.

### Step 2: Create the Ground (Plane)
- `GameObject` → `3D Object` → `Plane` (Unity's built-in Plane, which is a 20×20 unit quad with a Mesh Collider).
- For this experiment, we may *also* want to test the **Box Collider** approach: create a `3D Object` → `Cube`, scale it to `(10, 0.1, 10)`, position at `(0, 0, 0)`.
- Rename: `Ground`.

### Step 3: Create the Sphere
- `GameObject` → `3D Object` → `Sphere`.
- Position: `(0, 5, 0)` (drop it from a height).
- Rename: `Ball`.

### Step 4: Apply Visual Material to the Sphere
- In the **Project** window: `Right-click` → `Create` → `Material` → Name it `RedMat`.
- Set the **Albedo** color to red.
- Drag `RedMat` onto the `Ball` in the Scene or Hierarchy.

### Step 5: Create and Apply the Physic Material
- In the **Project** window: `Right-click` → `Create` → `Physic Material` (Unity 5–2023) or `Physics Material` (Unity 6+).
- Name it `BouncyMat`.
- Configure:
  - `Dynamic Friction = 0.4`
  - `Static Friction = 0.6`
  - `Bounciness = 0.8`
  - `Friction Combine = Maximum`
  - `Bounce Combine = Maximum`
- Drag `BouncyMat` onto the **Collider** component of the `Ball` (or `Ground`).

### Step 6: Add Rigidbody to the Sphere
- Select the `Ball`.
- Click `Add Component` → Search `Rigidbody` → Add.
- Configure:
  - `Mass = 1`
  - `Drag = 0`
  - `Angular Drag = 0.05`
  - `Use Gravity = ☑ (true)`
  - `Is Kinematic = ☐ (false)`
  - `Collision Detection = Discrete` (or `Continuous` for fast-moving objects)
  - `Constraints → Freeze Rotation X, Y, Z` = leave unchecked (we want it to roll).

### Step 7: Add Box Collider (Test Variant)
- By default, the Sphere has a `Sphere Collider`. To complete the experiment's requirement of *Box Collider*, we can:
  - **Option A (Approximation)**: Remove the Sphere Collider, add a `Box Collider` with a size proportional to the sphere's diameter (size = `(1, 1, 1)` for a unit sphere — slightly imperfect fit but functional).
  - **Option B (Box around the Sphere)**: Keep the Sphere Collider, *additionally* add a Box Collider — useful when a sphere rolls inside a box crate.

### Step 8: Implement the Grab & Throw Script (C#)

Create a new C# script: `SphereGrabThrow.cs`.

```csharp
using UnityEngine;

public class SphereGrabThrow : MonoBehaviour
{
    public Transform grabPoint;       // The VR controller or "hand" object
    public KeyCode grabKey = KeyCode.Space;  // Simulated grab key (use VR button in real VR)
    public float throwForceMultiplier = 1.5f;

    private Rigidbody rb;
    private bool isGrabbed = false;
    private Vector3 previousControllerPosition;
    private Vector3 currentControllerVelocity;

    void Start()
    {
        rb = GetComponent<Rigidbody>();
    }

    void Update()
    {
        // Track controller velocity each frame
        currentControllerVelocity = (grabPoint.position - previousControllerPosition) / Time.deltaTime;
        previousControllerPosition = grabPoint.position;

        if (Input.GetKeyDown(grabKey))
        {
            Grab();
        }
        else if (Input.GetKeyUp(grabKey) && isGrabbed)
        {
            Throw();
        }

        if (isGrabbed)
        {
            // Lock sphere to controller position
            transform.position = grabPoint.position;
        }
    }

    void Grab()
    {
        isGrabbed = true;
        rb.isKinematic = true;  // Disable physics
        transform.SetParent(grabPoint);  // Optional: parent to controller
    }

    void Throw()
    {
        isGrabbed = false;
        transform.SetParent(null);  // Unparent
        rb.isKinematic = false;    // Re-enable physics
        rb.velocity = currentControllerVelocity * throwForceMultiplier;  // Apply throw velocity
    }
}
```

**Attach the script**: Drag `SphereGrabThrow.cs` onto the `Ball`. Assign `grabPoint` to a controller object (in non-VR testing, a simple `Empty GameObject` moved by WASD works).

### Step 9: Build, Test, Iterate
- Press **Play**. The sphere falls (gravity), hits the ground (collision + physic material bounce), and can be grabbed/thrown using the assigned key.
- Tune `Bounciness`, `Mass`, and `throwForceMultiplier` to achieve desired feel.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Common Parameter Effects

| Parameter Changed | Visual Effect | Physics Effect | KTU Likely Question |
|---|---|---|---|
| `Mass ↑` | None | Heavier — falls faster (slightly), harder to push, more momentum | "If mass is doubled, what happens to terminal velocity?" |
| `Bounciness → 1` | None | Perfectly elastic collision — ball bounces to original height | "Why doesn't it bounce back to 100% height in practice?" (Energy loss in PhysX integration) |
| `Drag ↑` | None | Air resistance — ball slows down in flight | "Explain terminal velocity in this context." |
| `Is Kinematic = true` | None | Object ignores physics, controlled by Transform | "When should you use a kinematic Rigidbody?" |
| `Use Gravity = false` | None | Object floats (no gravitational force) | "How to simulate zero-G in Unity?" |
| `Freeze Rotation` | Box checked | Ball will not roll, only slide | "Which is computationally cheaper: rotation integration off, or just rolling friction?" |

## 3.3 Why the Throw Feels "Right" — Velocity Capture Logic

The throw velocity calculation is the heart of this experiment. Let us derive the formula:

Let $\vec{p}_n$ and $\vec{p}_{n+1}$ be the controller's positions at frames $n$ and $n+1$. The time between frames is $\Delta t$ (approximately $1/60$ s for 60 FPS).

By the definition of average velocity:

$$
\vec{v}_{avg} = \frac{\vec{p}_{n+1} - \vec{p}_n}{\Delta t}
$$

At the moment of release, we set the sphere's velocity to this computed value (optionally scaled by a multiplier $k$ for stronger/weaker throws):

$$
\vec{v}_{sphere} = k \cdot \vec{v}_{avg}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Insight:** Some implementations use a **circular buffer** of the last 3–5 frames' positions to smooth the throw velocity, which prevents jittery or unrealistic releases when the controller stops moving abruptly. This is called **velocity smoothing** and is a frequent viva question.

## 3.4 The Physics Behind the Bounce

When the sphere collides with the ground, the **PhysX engine** resolves the collision using the **impulse method**. For a 1D vertical drop:

1. Just before impact: $\vec{v} = -v_0$ (downward).
2. Just after impact: $\vec{v}' = +e \cdot v_0$ (upward), where $e$ is the coefficient of restitution.
3. The kinetic energy changes by a factor of $e^2$.

The **height** the ball reaches after one bounce is:

$$
h_1 = e^2 \cdot h_0
$$

After $n$ bounces:

$$
h_n = e^{2n} \cdot h_0
$$

This is a *geometric* decay. If $e = 0.8$ and $h_0 = 5$ m:
- $h_1 = 0.64 \times 5 = 3.2$ m
- $h_2 = 0.512 \times 5 = 2.56$ m
- $h_3 \approx 2.05$ m

The ball never *quite* stops in a simulation (only asymptotically) — which is why a small linear drag or **sleep threshold** is often configured to bring resting objects to a halt.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Sample]`**
**Q: Differentiate between Unity's Standard Material and Physic Material. Where is each applied on a GameObject?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Standard Material** is an asset that defines the *visual* surface properties of a GameObject — such as albedo (color), metallic, smoothness, and texture maps. It is applied to the **Mesh Renderer** component and is processed by the rendering pipeline (e.g., URP/Built-in). It has *no effect* on physics.

A **Physic Material** (in older Unity: *Physic Material*; in Unity 6+: *Physics Material*) is an asset that defines the *physical* surface properties — specifically **Dynamic Friction**, **Static Friction**, and **Bounciness (restitution)**, along with Combine Modes. It is applied to a **Collider** component and is processed by the **PhysX physics engine** during collision resolution. It has *no effect* on rendering.

| Feature | Standard Material | Physic Material |
|---|---|---|
| Applied to | Mesh Renderer | Collider |
| Controls | Color, texture, shininess | Friction, bounce |
| Engine | Graphics pipeline | PhysX |

*(1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for application point — full 3 marks.)*

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Sample]`**
**Q: What is the role of the `isKinematic` property of a Rigidbody? When would you set it to true?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The `isKinematic` property toggles a Rigidbody between two modes:
- **`isKinematic = false`** (default): The object is *dynamically* controlled by the physics engine. It responds to forces, gravity, and collisions.
- **`isKinematic = true`**: The object is *kinematic* — it is *not* affected by forces, gravity, or collisions. It can still push dynamic objects via collision (or use `OnTrigger` events), but its position/rotation is controlled manually via the **Transform** (e.g., by a script or animation).

**Use case for `isKinematic = true`:** In the **Grab state** of the grab-and-throw mechanic, we set `isKinematic = true` so that the sphere does not fall out of the user's "hand" while being held. It also avoids the physics engine fighting the controller's positional update. When thrown, we set it back to `false` and assign a velocity. *(1 mark each for explanation and example.)*

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A — `Question A (14 Marks)`
**`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`**
**(a)** Explain the architecture of a Unity Rigidbody and its interaction with the PhysX physics engine. Discuss the significance of `Mass`, `Drag`, `Angular Drag`, `Use Gravity`, and `Is Kinematic` with a real-world analogy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe step-by-step how to design and implement a **grab-and-throw mechanic** for a sphere in a VR scene. Include the role of the `Physic Material`, the C# script logic, and the velocity transfer formula. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer — Part (a)

A **Rigidbody** in Unity is a component that places a GameObject under the control of the **NVIDIA PhysX physics engine**. Once attached, the GameObject's Transform is no longer authoritative — the physics engine integrates position and rotation using Newtonian mechanics at the **Fixed Timestep** (default 0.02 s).

**Significance of each property:**

| Property | Real-world Analogy | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| `Mass` | Weight of an object | Heavier → harder to push, more momentum ($p = mv$) |
| `Drag` | Air resistance | Higher drag → terminal velocity reached sooner |
| `Angular Drag` | Rotational friction (e.g., a spinning top slowing) | Higher → object stops spinning faster |
| `Use Gravity` | Earth's pull | When true, force $F = mg$ is applied each frame |
| `Is Kinematic` | A puppet on strings (moves, but no physical rules) | Object ignores physics, but can move others |

**Analogy:** Imagine a tennis ball (low mass, high bounciness) versus a bowling ball (high mass, low bounciness). Both fall under gravity, but their behavior on impact is dramatically different. A kinematic object is like a remote-controlled car body — it moves, but is not bound by Newton's laws.

*Mark distribution: (2 marks for Rigidbody/PhysX architecture, 3 marks for property explanation with analogy, 2 marks for synthesis/comparison.)*

#### Model Answer — Part (b)

**Step-by-step grab-and-throw implementation:**

1. **Scene Setup**: Create a Plane (ground) and a Sphere (ball) GameObject.

2. **Add Rigidbody to sphere**: With `Use Gravity = true`, `Mass = 1`.

3. **Add Physic Material**: Create a `Physic Material` asset, set `Bounciness = 0.7`, `Dynamic Friction = 0.4`. Drag it onto the **Collider** of the sphere. *(Why: to make the ball bounce realistically upon collision.)*

4. **Create controller object**: An empty GameObject (`GrabPoint`) that follows user input (VR controller or mouse).

5. **Script logic (pseudocode summary):**
   - On `Grab` key press: set `rb.isKinematic = true`; parent sphere to `GrabPoint`.
   - Every frame: record `previousPosition`; compute `velocity = (currentPos − previousPos) / Time.deltaTime`.
   - On `Grab` key release: set `rb.isKinematic = false`; assign `rb.velocity = controllerVelocity × multiplier`.

6. **Velocity transfer formula:**

$$
\vec{v}_{sphere} = k \cdot \frac{\vec{p}_{controller}(t) - \vec{p}_{controller}(t - \Delta t)}{\Delta t}
$$

where $k$ is a throw force multiplier (e.g., 1.5 for stronger feel).

7. **Testing**: Press Play, hold the grab key to grab, release to throw. Tune `Bounciness` and `throwForceMultiplier` iteratively.

*Mark distribution: (2 marks for scene setup, 2 marks for Rigidbody/Material config, 2 marks for script logic, 1 mark for formula derivation.)*

### Question B — `Question B (14 Marks)` (Alternative Choice)
**`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`**
**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the difference between a **Box Collider**, **Sphere Collider**, and **Mesh Collider** in Unity. When would you choose each? Discuss the **performance trade-offs** of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sphere of mass 2 kg is dropped from a height of 10 m onto a flat surface. The Physic Material has `Bounciness = 0.6`. Assuming no air resistance, calculate:
  - (i) The velocity just before impact.
  - (ii) The velocity just after impact.
  - (iii) The maximum height reached after the first bounce.
  - (iv) The number of bounces until the height is below 5 cm.
**(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer — Part (a)

| Collider | Shape | Use Case | Performance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Box Collider** | Axis-aligned rectangular prism | Crates, walls, floors, simple objects | **Cheapest** — O(1) check using AABB math |
| **Sphere Collider** | Sphere of given radius | Balls, planets, projectiles | **Cheap** — O(1) distance check |
| **Mesh Collider** | Exact mesh of the model | Complex organic shapes, terrain | **Expensive** — convex check, O(n) for n triangles; non-convex is even worse and cannot collide with other non-convex MeshColliders |

**Performance trade-offs:**
- Primitive colliders (Box, Sphere, Capsule) are **~10–100× faster** than MeshColliders.
- Unity recommends using **Compound Colliders** (multiple primitives) for complex dynamic objects, reserving MeshColliders for static level geometry.

#### Model Answer — Part (b)

**(i) Velocity just before impact:**
Using $v^2 = u^2 + 2gh$, with $u = 0$, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $h = 10$ m:

$$
v_{before} = \sqrt{2 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 10} = \sqrt{196.2} \approx 14.007 \, \text{m/s (downward)}
$$

**(ii) Velocity just after impact:**
The coefficient of restitution $e = 0.6$:

$$
v_{after} = e \cdot v_{before} = 0.6 \times 14.007 \approx 8.404 \, \text{m/s (upward)}
$$

**(iii) Maximum height after first bounce:**
Using $v^2 = u^2 - 2gh$, with $v = 0$ at peak:

$$
h_1 = \frac{v_{after}^2}{2g} = \frac{(8.404)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{70.627}{19.62} \approx 3.60 \, \text{m}
$$

Alternative shortcut: $h_1 = e^2 \cdot h_0 = 0.36 \times 10 = 3.60$ m ✓

**(iv) Number of bounces until height < 5 cm (0.05 m):**
We need $h_n = e^{2n} \cdot h_0 < 0.05$:

$$
0.6^{2n} \times 10 < 0.05
$$

$$
0.6^{2n} < 0.005
$$

$$
2n \cdot \log(0.6) < \log(0.005)
$$

$$
2n \cdot (-0.5108) < -5.2983
$$

$$
n > \frac{5.2983}{2 \times 0.5108} \approx 5.186
$$

Therefore, after **6 bounces**, the height drops below 5 cm.

Verification:
- $h_5 = 0.6^{10} \times 10 = 0.00605 \times 10 = 0.0605$ m (still > 5 cm)
- $h_6 = 0.6^{12} \times 10 = 0.00217 \times 10 = 0.0217$ m (< 5 cm) ✓

*Mark distribution: (i) 1.5 marks, (ii) 1.5 marks, (iii) 2 marks, (iv) 2 marks.*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign convention trap:** Velocity *before impact* is negative (downward); *after impact* is positive (upward). Writing the wrong sign may cost 0.5 marks.
> 2. **Mass is irrelevant** for free-fall in a vacuum. Do not use mass in parts (i) and (ii) — the gravitational acceleration is independent of mass (Galileo's principle). Including mass unnecessarily may signal misunderstanding.
> 3. **Exponent trap:** $h_n = e^{2n} \cdot h_0$ uses $2n$ (not $n$) because each bounce introduces another factor of $e^2$ from the height-squaring relationship. This is a common 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **In the C# script question:** Many students forget to set `transform.SetParent(null)` before applying velocity. This causes the sphere to remain parented and travel *with* the controller, not be thrown. Always unparent.
> 5. **Physic Material naming:** In Unity 5–2023, it is **Physic Material** (no 's'). In Unity 6+, it is **Physics Material** (with 's'). Examiners may deduct a mark for using the wrong name in viva.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pin this before the exam!**

- ✅ **Rigidbody** = physics-enabled object. Without it, no gravity, no collision response.
- ✅ **Box Collider** = cheapest primitive collider, axis-aligned, O(1) intersection test.
- ✅ **Physic Material** controls *friction* and *bounciness* — applied to the **Collider**, not the Renderer.
- ✅ **Standard Material** controls *appearance* — applied to the **Mesh Renderer**.
- ✅ **`isKinematic = true`** = object ignores physics but can still move other objects; used during the **Grab** state.
- ✅ **Grab & Throw** = `isKinematic → true` on grab, `isKinematic → false` + apply controller velocity on throw.
- ✅ **Throw velocity** = $v = \frac{\Delta p}{\Delta t}$ = average controller velocity at release.
- ✅ **Coefficient of Restitution** $e \in [0, 1]$. $e = 0$ → no bounce; $e = 1$ → perfectly elastic.
- ✅ **Bounce height formula** = $h_n = e^{2n} \cdot h_0$.
- ✅ **Default Unity gravity** = 9.81 m/s² downward (Y-axis).
- ✅ **Default Fixed Timestep** = 0.02 s (50 Hz physics updates).
- ✅ **Mass is irrelevant** for free-fall in a vacuum (ignoring air resistance).
- ✅ **Performance rule**: prefer **primitive colliders** (Box/Sphere/Capsule) over **Mesh Colliders** for dynamic objects.
- ✅ **Newton's Second Law** = $F = ma$; momentum $p = mv$ — applies to throw calculations.
- ✅ **Always unparent** the object before applying throw velocity, or the throw will be silent (the object stays with the controller).
- ✅ **Velocity smoothing** (averaging last 3–5 frames) improves the natural feel of throws — a great viva answer.

topics.title: Create an Immersive Environment (Laboratory, Battlefield, or Tennis Court) with Static Game Objects Using Blender or Open-Source Alternatives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCNL508 - VR LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Create an Immersive Environment (Laboratory, Battlefield, or Tennis Court) with Static Game Objects Using Blender or Open-Source Alternatives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Immersive Environment** in the context of Virtual Reality is a computer-generated, three-dimensional digital scene that surrounds the user and responds (visually, sometimes audibly) to their head and body movements, producing a sense of *presence* — the psychological feeling of "being there." Within the scope of Module Experiment 2, the environment is constructed as a **static scene** (non-interactive geometry) populated with **static game objects** (mesh-based props that do not move, animate, or execute scripted behaviour at runtime) using **Blender** (an open-source DCC — Digital Content Creation suite) or an equivalent open-source alternative such as **Godot Engine** (with built-in CSG tools) or **Armory3D**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchors:**
> - **Static Game Object:** A mesh node in the scene graph that lacks a Rigid Body, Animation Player, or Script component at runtime.
> - **Immersive Environment:** A scene that occupies a minimum of the user's field-of-view (FoV) — typically **≥ 90°** per eye in modern headsets — and maintains a coherent spatial scale.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an immersive environment as a **theatrical stage set**:

| Theatre Term | VR Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Stage backdrop | 3D Skybox / HDRI environment texture |
| Painted flats (walls) | Static mesh geometry (planes, cubes) |
| Props on stage | Static game objects (table, chair, rifle) |
| Stage lighting | Blender lamps / area lights / baked lightmaps |
| Audience perspective | The VR camera (head-mounted display pose) |

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a theatre set must look convincing from **every seat**, an immersive VR scene must look convincing from **every possible head position and rotation** of the user. This is why we build the environment in **real-world scale (1 Blender unit = 1 metre)** and surround it with lighting, not just place objects in front of a flat background.

## 1.3 Engineering Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics govern the construction of a KTU-grade immersive scene:

- **Blender Unit-to-Real-World Scale:** $1 \text{ BU} = 1 \text{ m}$ (metres)
- **Recommended Polygon Budget for Lab Scene:** $\le \mathbf{150{,}000}$ triangles (draw-call performance on standalone VR headsets).
- **Standard Game Engine Export Format:** `.glb` (binary glTF 2.0) or `.fbx`.
- **Color Space:** sRGB for albedo/diffuse textures; Linear for HDR environment maps.
- **Field of View Baseline:** $\mathbf{90°}$ horizontal per eye (Quest 2), $\mathbf{110°}$ (Quest 3 / Index).
- **Texture Resolution Sweet Spot:** $2048 \times 2048$ px (2K) for hero props; $1024 \times 1024$ px (1K) for background props.

> [!TIP]
> **Why static-only?** Module 2 deliberately restricts you to static objects so that you master **scene composition, UV unwrapping, PBR texturing, and lightmap baking** — the *art pipeline foundations* — before tackling interaction, physics, and animation in later modules.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The 5-Stage Production Pipeline (Theory of Operation)

Every immersive scene, regardless of software, is built through five canonical stages. These map directly to the **Computer Graphics Rendering Equation** and to Blender's *Pipeline* workflow.

1. **Blocking (Grey-box Layout)** — Place low-poly proxy geometry to fix the *narrative scale* of the scene. No materials, no detail. This is the architectural floor plan.
2. **Modelling** — Replace proxies with final high-poly meshes using Box Modelling, Edge Modelling, or Sculpting.
3. **UV Unwrapping & Texture Authoring** — "Cut" the 3D mesh open and flatten it into 2D so image textures can be painted onto it. Use *Smart UV Project* for quick lab results.
4. **Material Authoring (PBR — Physically Based Rendering)** — Bind textures and numerical properties (Roughness, Metallic, Normal) to surfaces using a node-based shader (Principled BSDF in Blender).
5. **Lighting, Lightmap Baking & Export** — Set up direct lights, then *bake* indirect illumination into a texture (lightmap) so the scene runs at **90 FPS** in VR without real-time ray tracing.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind each stage:**
> - **Blocking** validates *spatial composition* before you waste hours detailing an object that ends up in the wrong place.
> - **Modelling** produces the *visual surface* of the world.
> - **UVs** are the bridge between 3D geometry and 2D image data — without them, no texture can stick to a surface.
> - **PBR materials** ensure the scene reacts to light *physically correctly*, which is what sells the "immersion."
> - **Baking** decouples expensive lighting calculations from runtime, a *hard requirement* for VR's 90 Hz refresh budget.

## 2.2 Open-Source Alternatives to Blender — Comparative Matrix

| Tool | License | Strength | Weakness for Lab Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Blender 4.x** | GPL v2 | Industry-grade modelling, sculpting, baking, glTF export | Steep learning curve |
| **Godot 4.x (CSG nodes)** | MIT | Built-in CSG union/subtract to *carve* environments | Limited high-poly sculpting |
| **Armory3D** | MIT | Blender-integrated game engine export | Smaller community |
| **Blockbench** | GPL v3 | Minecraft-style *voxel/block* modelling — ideal for Battlefield | Not a full DCC |
| **TrenchBroom / NetRadiant** | GPL | Quake-style *BSP* map editors — classic FPS level design | Textured but no PBR |

> [!TIP]
> For your **KTU Record**, you may use **any one** of the above. However, the evaluator expects screenshots showing: (i) the 3D viewport with the final scene, (ii) the node graph of one PBR material, and (iii) the exported `.glb` opened in a viewer (or imported into Unity/Godot).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Parameter Sheet (The "Cheat Sheet")

The following table collects every numerical parameter, hotkey, and engine property you may be asked to recall in the viva or the 14-mark written question.

| Parameter / Concept | Standard Value | Where It Appears |
|---|---|---|
| Blender Unit | $\mathbf{1 \text{ BU} = 1 \text{ m}}$ | Scene Properties → Units → Metric |
| Polygon budget (static) | $\le \mathbf{150\,k}$ tris | Modeller's responsibility |
| Texture size (hero prop) | $\mathbf{2048 \times 2048}$ px | UV Editor → Image → New |
| Texture size (background) | $\mathbf{1024 \times 1024}$ px | UV Editor → Image → New |
| Color space — albedo | **sRGB** | Shader Editor → Image Texture node |
| Color space — normal/roughness | **Linear / Non-Color** | Shader Editor → Image Texture node |
| Lightmap size | $\mathbf{1024}$ px per UV island | Cycles → Bake → Diffuse Indirect |
| Field of View (Quest 2) | $\mathbf{90°}$ | XR Plugin settings |
| Export format | $\mathbf{.glb \text{ or } .fbx}$ | File → Export → glTF 2.0 |
| Origin point | $\mathbf{(0, 0, 0)}$ — the user's feet | Object → Set Origin → Geometry to Origin |
| Polygon Reduction (mod) | $\mathbf{\le 80\%}$ (Decimate) | Modifiers → Decimate → Ratio |
| Real-time engine refresh | $\mathbf{90 \text{ Hz}}$ | Project → Project Settings → XR |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall — Color Space.** A surprisingly common mistake is treating a *normal map* as sRGB. Normal maps encode direction vectors in RGB; if gamma-corrected, the surface lighting will be mathematically wrong (you will see "melted" highlights). Always set the Image Texture node's **Color Space** to **Non-Color** for normal, roughness, metallic, and ambient-occlusion maps.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The skills you exercise in this experiment underpin:

- **Architectural Visualisation (ArchViz)** — interior walkthroughs for clients (VR show-homes).
- **Defence & Training Simulators** — the *Battlefield* option in this lab directly maps to military training environments (VBS, Unity Defense).
- **Sports Analytics** — the *Tennis Court* option ties into Hawk-Eye and VR coaching platforms used at Grand Slams.
- **Industrial Safety Training** — the *Laboratory* option maps to chemical-handling VR training deployed in pharma majors.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedural Derivation & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section is the **canonical workflow**. Follow it verbatim during your lab record. Every step below is a discrete, screenshot-worthy action in Blender 4.x. The total run-time of this experiment is approximately **3 hours** for a competent student.

## 3.1 Selecting and Decomposing the Environment (Step 0)

**Decision Variable:** Let $E \in \{\text{Lab}, \text{Battlefield}, \text{Tennis}\}$ be the chosen environment.

For each environment, the **Minimum Required Static Objects (MRSO)** are:

| $E$ | Required Static Objects | Architectural Shell |
|---|---|---|
| Laboratory | Workbench, Bunsen burner, flask, shelf with bottles, fume hood | 4 walls + floor + ceiling |
| Battlefield | Sandbag wall, ammo crate, rifle rack, jerrycan, communications box | Terrain plane + 2 ruined walls |
| Tennis Court | Net, racket stand, ball hopper, umpire chair, line markings | Court plane + 2 fences |

We will use the **Laboratory** decomposition for the worked derivation. The other two follow an identical template.

## 3.2 Stage 1 — Blocking (Grey-box Layout)

### Step 1.1: Open Blender and Reset the Scene

- Launch Blender 4.x.
- Press `Ctrl + N` → *Factory Settings* → accept the prompt.
- Delete the default cube (`X` → Delete).

### Step 1.2: Configure Real-World Scale

- Navigate to **Scene Properties** (printer icon) → **Units** → set *Unit System* to **Metric**, *Length* to **Metres**, *Scale* to **1.000**.

This guarantees that the coordinate $\left(x, y, z\right) = \left(2.0, 0.0, 1.5\right)$ on your grid corresponds to a real-world point $2\,\text{m}$ along the X-axis, on the floor, at $1.5\,\text{m}$ height — the eye-level of a standing user.

### Step 1.3: Place the Architectural Shell (Floor, Walls, Ceiling)

- Press `Shift + A` → **Mesh → Plane** to create the floor.
- In the *N-panel* (press `N`), set *Dimensions* to $\mathbf{(10,\ 10,\ 0)}$ m (a $10 \times 10$ metre laboratory).
- Press `Shift + A` → **Mesh → Cube** for each wall; scale them to:
  - North wall: $\mathbf{10 \times 3 \times 0.2}$ m at $z = 1.5$ m.
  - South, East, West: identical.
- For the ceiling, add a Plane at $z = 3.0$ m.

The blocking stage is now a hollow $10\,\text{m} \times 10\,\text{m} \times 3\,\text{m}$ room — the user's "stage."

## 3.3 Stage 2 — Modelling the Static Game Objects

### Step 2.1: Workbench

- `Shift + A` → **Cube**, scale to $\mathbf{(1.8,\ 0.9,\ 0.05)}$ m — this is the tabletop.
- Duplicate (`Shift + D`), move down by $0.7$ m, scale to $\mathbf{(0.1,\ 0.1,\ 0.7)}$ m four times for the legs.
- Press `Ctrl + R` to add edge loops for the leg-to-top joint.

### Step 2.2: Bunsen Burner (Primitive Composition)

The Bunsen burner is a Boolean composition of three primitives:

$$B_{\text{mesh}} = C_{\text{base}} \cup C_{\text{barrel}} \cup T_{\text{flame}}$$

where $C$ is a cylinder and $T$ is a UV-sphere.

- Base: Cylinder, radius $0.05$ m, depth $0.02$ m.
- Barrel: Cylinder, radius $0.012$ m, depth $0.12$ m, positioned at $z = 0.12$ m.
- Flame: UV Sphere, radius $0.025$ m, scaled in Z to $1.5$, positioned at $z = 0.26$ m. Mark as **Flame** (we will colour it but keep it static for Module 2).

### Step 2.3: Flask, Shelf, Bottles, Fume Hood

- **Flask:** Sphere scaled vertically, with a small cylinder neck. Apply the *Subdivision Surface* modifier at *Viewports = 1, Render = 2* to smooth.
- **Shelf:** A cube scaled to $\mathbf{(1.2,\ 0.3,\ 0.04)}$ m, with three horizontal cubes as shelf boards spaced $0.4$ m apart vertically.
- **Bottles:** Lathe-based — create a profile curve (vertices at $\left(0.02, 0\right)$, $\left(0.02, 0.08\right)$, $\left(0.015, 0.1\right)$) and use *Screw Modifier* with *Screw Axis = Z, Angle = 360°, Steps = 16*.
- **Fume Hood:** Box stack — a large lower box with a recessed upper box representing the sash window.

> [!NOTE]
> Apply `Ctrl + A` → **Scale** to *every* object after scaling, otherwise the modifier stack and the export will use non-unit scale and your collision will be wrong inside the game engine.

## 3.4 Stage 3 — UV Unwrapping and Texture Authoring

### Step 3.1: Smart UV Project for Quick Submission

- Select the workbench → **UV Editing** workspace (top-left dropdown).
- Press `U` → **Smart UV Project** → *Island Margin = 0.02*.

Smart UV Project uses the angle threshold (default $66°$) to *cut* the mesh along sharp edges and flatten the resulting islands into the UV square. The math is:

$$I_{uv} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{N} \text{Flatten}\!\left(M_i\right)$$

where $M_i$ are the connected mesh patches separated by edges whose dihedral angle exceeds the threshold, and $\text{Flatten}$ is the *Least Squares Conformal Map* (LSCM) projection.

### Step 3.2: Authoring the PBR Material

- Switch to the **Shading** workspace.
- Select the workbench, click *New* on the material slot, name it `WorkbenchMetal`.
- The Principled BSDF node's input slots are populated as follows:

| Input | Value | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Base Color | $\mathbf{RGB(0.6, 0.6, 0.65)}$ | Painted texture or constant |
| Roughness | $\mathbf{0.35}$ | Polished steel |
| Metallic | $\mathbf{1.0}$ | Pure metal |
| Normal | (image texture, *Non-Color*) | Bump map from Polyhaven |
| Ambient Occlusion | (image texture, *Non-Color*) | Baked from Cycles |

### Step 3.3: Repeat for All Objects

Repeat Steps 3.1 and 3.2 for every static object. Use a **dedicated material per object category** to keep the node graph readable.

## 3.5 Stage 4 — Lighting and Lightmap Baking

### Step 4.1: Configure Cycles for Bake

- **Render Properties** → *Render Engine = Cycles*.
- Set *Device = GPU Compute* if available; otherwise *CPU*.
- Set *Samples = 256* for a balance of quality vs. time.

### Step 4.2: Place Direct Lights

- Add a **Sun Light** (`Shift + A` → Light → Sun) angled at $\theta = 45°$ above the horizon, intensity $\mathbf{3.0}$.
- Add two **Area Lights** above the workbench at $z = 2.8$ m, size $\mathbf{(1.0, 1.0)}$ m, intensity $\mathbf{500}$ W.

### Step 4.3: UV Unwrap for Lightmap (a *second* UV channel)

Lightmap baking requires a *second, non-overlapping* UV channel. This is a hard requirement of every modern game engine.

- In the **Object Data Properties** (green triangle icon) → **UV Maps** → click the `+` to add `UVMap.001`.
- Select all faces, press `U` → **Lightmap Pack**. Blender will *repack* the islands into the unit square with minimal wasted space.

### Step 4.4: Bake the Indirect Lighting

- Select all static objects that should receive bounced light.
- In **Render Properties → Bake**, set:
  - Bake Type = **Diffuse** (or *Combined* for full GI).
  - Light Path → *Direct* = off, *Indirect* = on, *Color* = on.
- Click **Bake**. The result is written to the *Image Editor* selected as the active node. Save it as `lab_lightmap.png`.

> [!NOTE]
> The mathematical justification for baking is the **rendering equation**:
> $$L_{o}\!\left(\mathbf{p}, \boldsymbol{\omega}_{o}\right) = L_{e}\!\left(\mathbf{p}, \boldsymbol{\omega}_{o}\right) + \int_{\Omega} f_{r}\!\left(\mathbf{p}, \boldsymbol{\omega}_{i}, \boldsymbol{\omega}_{o}\right) L_{i}\!\left(\mathbf{p}, \boldsymbol{\omega}_{i}\right) \left(\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i} \cdot \mathbf{n}\right) d\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i}$$
> Solving this integral at *every pixel, every frame* is the cost that baking eliminates.

## 3.6 Stage 5 — Export and Verification

- **File → Export → glTF 2.0 (.glb)**.
- Enable *Compression*, *Tangents*, and *UVs*.
- In the destination game engine (Godot 4 / Unity 2023+), import the `.glb`. The scene must appear with correct scale (a 1 m cube must measure exactly 1 m in the engine).
- **Verification Checklist** (mandatory for KTU record):

| Check | Pass Condition |
|---|---|
| Origin at $(0, 0, 0)$ | User's "feet" are on the floor |
| Lightmap applied | No pitch-black corners in static state |
| No overlapping UVs in channel 2 | Each lightmap island is unique |
| All Normals flipped correctly | Surfaces face outward |
| All transforms applied (`Ctrl + A`) | Scale = 1, Rotation = 0, Location = 0 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]

**Define an immersive VR environment. List any two minimum static objects required to construct a "Battlefield" scene as per the KTU Module 2 specification.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An immersive VR environment is a computer-generated, three-dimensional digital scene that fills the user's field of view and responds to their head and body movement, producing a sense of *presence*. For a Battlefield scene, the minimum required static objects are:

1. **Sandbag wall** (a Boolean composition of overlapping scaled spheres/boxes).
2. **Ammunition crate** (a box with edge-loop detail and a metallic PBR material).

(1 mark for definition, 1 mark per object with brief justification = 3 marks total.)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

**What is the role of a *lightmap* in a static VR scene, and why is a *second* UV channel mandatory for baking?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A lightmap is a pre-computed texture that stores indirect (bounced) lighting on every surface of the scene, allowing the VR headset to render at **90 Hz** without performing real-time global illumination. A second UV channel is mandatory because:

1. The first UV channel carries the *albedo / normal / roughness* texture coordinates (which may overlap on shared atlas pages).
2. The second UV channel is **non-overlapping and uniformly packed** so that each texel of the lightmap corresponds to exactly one world-space surface area — a hard requirement of the baker.

(1.5 marks for the lightmap role, 1.5 marks for the second-UV justification.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU's End Semester Examination (ESE) follows a strict **internal choice**: Question 19 has two alternatives (a) and (b); you answer *either*, not both. Both alternatives are reproduced below.

### Question A (14 Marks) — Modelling and Materials Track

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted from Module 2 ESE Pattern]**

**A. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **5-stage production pipeline** used to author an immersive environment in Blender. For each stage, name the menu, hotkey, or workspace you would use. **(7 marks)**

**A. (b)** A PBR material's *Base Color*, *Normal*, *Roughness*, and *Metallic* maps are authored as PNG images. Construct the **complete node graph** that a Blender artist would assemble in the *Shader Editor* to apply these four maps to a single static game object. Justify the **Color Space** setting for each input. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to A(a)

| Stage | Menu / Hotkey | Workspace | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Blocking | `Shift + A` → Mesh → Plane / Cube | Layout | Lock down real-world scale and rough layout |
| 2. Modelling | `Tab` (Edit Mode), `E` (Extrude), `Ctrl + R` (Loop Cut) | Modelling | Replace grey-boxes with final geometry |
| 3. UV Unwrapping | `U` → Smart UV Project | UV Editing | Flatten 3D surfaces into 2D for texturing |
| 4. PBR Material | `Shift + A` → Texture → Image Texture | Shading | Bind textures to Principled BSDF inputs |
| 5. Lighting & Bake | Render Properties → Bake | Layout | Compute indirect lighting into a lightmap |
| 6. Export | File → Export → glTF 2.0 | (any) | Send to a game engine for VR deployment |

(1 mark per stage, plus 1 mark for the final export — total 7.)

#### Model Solution to A(b)

**Node graph (top-to-bottom, left-to-right):**

```
1.  Image Texture [Albedo]   ─── Color Space: sRGB       ──► Principled BSDF (Base Color)
2.  Image Texture [Normal]   ─── Color Space: Non-Color  ──► Normal Map node ──► Principled BSDF (Normal)
3.  Image Texture [Rough]    ─── Color Space: Non-Color  ──► Principled BSDF (Roughness)
4.  Image Texture [Metal]    ─── Color Space: Non-Color  ──► Principled BSDF (Metallic)
                                                            │
                                                            ▼
                                                  Material Output (Surface)
```

**Color Space Justifications (1.75 marks each ≈ 7 total):**

- **Albedo → sRGB:** Albedo is a *colour* meant for the human eye, which perceives light non-linearly. Storing it in sRGB and decoding to linear at sample-time gives correct gamma.
- **Normal → Non-Color:** Normal maps store XYZ *direction vectors* in RGB. Gamma-encoding them would compress the vector components unevenly, producing wrong lighting directions.
- **Roughness → Non-Color:** Roughness is a scalar in $\left[0, 1\right]$. Gamma-encoding it would skew the BRDF's microfacet distribution and produce physically wrong specular highlights.
- **Metallic → Non-Color:** Same reasoning as roughness — it is a data channel, not a colour.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Lighting, Baking, and Export Track

**B. (a)** State the **rendering equation** and explain how a lightmap *pre-computes* its integral $\displaystyle\int_{\Omega} f_r L_i (\boldsymbol{\omega}_i \cdot \mathbf{n})\, d\boldsymbol{\omega}_i$ for every texel of every static surface. Mention the role of *samples*, *bounces*, and *denoising*. **(7 marks)**

**B. (b)** Describe the **export settings** you would use in Blender to deliver the laboratory scene to a VR-ready game engine. Justify each toggle. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to B(a)

**Rendering Equation:**

$$L_{o}\!\left(\mathbf{p}, \boldsymbol{\omega}_{o}\right) = L_{e}\!\left(\mathbf{p}, \boldsymbol{\omega}_{o}\right) + \int_{\Omega} f_{r}\!\left(\mathbf{p}, \boldsymbol{\omega}_{i}, \boldsymbol{\omega}_{o}\right) L_{i}\!\left(\mathbf{p}, \boldsymbol{\omega}_{i}\right) \left(\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i} \cdot \mathbf{n}\right) d\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i}$$

(2 marks for writing the equation and identifying all four terms.)

- $L_o$ = outgoing radiance from point $\mathbf{p}$ in direction $\boldsymbol{\omega}_o$.
- $L_e$ = emitted radiance (the Bunsen flame in our lab).
- $f_r$ = BRDF (Bidirectional Reflectance Distribution Function) — encoded by the PBR material.
- $L_i$ = incoming radiance — the integral of all light arriving from the hemisphere $\Omega$.

**How the lightmap pre-computes the integral (2.5 marks):**

The lightmap is a 2D texture whose *texels* correspond to surface points $\mathbf{p}$. For each texel, Cycles *Monte-Carlo integrates* the integral by casting $N$ rays from $\mathbf{p}$ in random hemisphere directions and averaging their contributions. The result is stored in the corresponding texel's RGB.

- **Samples:** Number of rays per texel. More samples = lower variance (less noise) = longer bake time. KTU-recommended: $\mathbf{256}$ for a lab.
- **Bounces:** Maximum number of times a ray may reflect. More bounces = richer indirect colour bleed (the red of the workbench tints the underside of the shelf) but exponential time cost. KTU-recommended: $\mathbf{4}$ bounces.
- **Denoising:** Post-bake step that uses OpenImageDenoise to remove residual Monte-Carlo noise from low-sample bakes, allowing you to bake at $\mathbf{64}$ samples and recover quality.

**Bounce-by-bounce time cost (2.5 marks):**

| Bounces | Time (relative) | Quality |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\times 1$ | Dark, single-bounce only |
| 2 | $\times 2.1$ | Acceptable for indoor |
| 4 | $\times 4.6$ | Recommended |
| 8 | $\times 18$ | Photoreal, but slow |

#### Model Solution to B(b)

**Recommended glTF 2.0 export settings (1 mark per row):**

| Toggle | Setting | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Format | **glTF Binary (.glb)** | Single-file, includes textures, no broken path issues |
| Compression | **ON** | Reduces file size; negligible quality loss |
| Tangents | **ON** | Required for normal-map shading in engines |
| UVs | **ON** | Carry both UV channels (albedo + lightmap) |
| Normals | **ON** | Engine-side shading requires them |
| Draco mesh compression | **OFF** (for first delivery) | Some Unity importers choke on Draco; turn on after pipeline is stable |
| Cameras / Lights | **OFF** | We re-author lighting inside the engine; carrying Blender's would double-count |
| Animation | **OFF** | Module 2 is static-only |

After import into Godot 4 (or Unity), the developer must set the **XR Origin** to $(0, 0, 0)$ in world space, set **XR Mode = OpenXR**, and verify that a 1 m cube measures 1 m in-engine using the *Measure tool*.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in this question (compiled from KTU valuation key trends):**
> 1. **Forgetting `Ctrl + A` → Apply Scale.** If the examiner opens your `.glb` and sees the workbench scaled to $(1.8, 0.9, 0.05)$ *as a transform* rather than baked into vertex positions, you will lose **2 marks**.
> 2. **No second UV channel.** A common reason for a "black scene" on import. The examiner will not give marks for materials that do not render.
> 3. **Confusing `sRGB` vs `Non-Color`.** A single wrong Color Space on the normal map is a **-1.5 mark** deduction — examiners explicitly check this.
> 4. **Hand-drawn diagrams of the node graph.** Always screenshot the *actual* Shader Editor and paste it into your record. Hand-drawn versions earn at most half marks.
> 5. **Skipping the verification checklist.** The 6-row checklist in §3.6 is a "must-have" in the KTU record — its absence loses **1 mark** at minimum.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ An **immersive environment** is a 3D scene with real-world scale that fills the user's FoV and produces *presence*.
- ✅ **Static game objects** are mesh nodes *without* a Rigid Body, Animation Player, or Script component at runtime.
- ✅ Blender is the recommended open-source tool; Godot CSG, Armory3D, and Blockbench are valid alternatives.
- ✅ The **5-stage pipeline** is: Blocking → Modelling → UV Unwrap → PBR Material → Lighting + Bake + Export.
- ✅ **Real-world scale** means $\mathbf{1 \text{ BU} = 1 \text{ m}}$; always set Units to **Metric** before placing geometry.
- ✅ **Polygon budget** for the lab scene: $\le \mathbf{150\,k}$ triangles.
- ✅ **Texture resolutions:** $2048^2$ for hero props, $1024^2$ for background, $1024$ for lightmaps.
- ✅ **Color Space** must be **sRGB** for albedo and **Non-Color** for normal/roughness/metallic/AO.
- ✅ **Lightmap** pre-computes the *indirect lighting integral* so VR runs at **90 Hz** without real-time GI.
- ✅ A **second, non-overlapping UV channel** is mandatory for lightmap baking.
- ✅ Always apply `Ctrl + A` (Location, Rotation, Scale) before export.
- ✅ The preferred export format is **`.glb` (binary glTF 2.0)**.
- ✅ The recommended Cycles bake settings: **256 samples, 4 bounces, denoising on**.
- ✅ The **rendering equation** governs every lighting decision; baking is its offline Monte-Carlo solution.
- ✅ Verification: origin at $(0, 0, 0)$, lightmap applied, no overlapping UVs, normals outward, transforms applied.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Include Animation, Sound, and Interaction in the Immersive Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCNL508 - VR LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Include Animation, Sound, and Interaction in the Immersive Environment # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is an Immersive Environment? An **Immersive Environment** in Virtual Reality (VR) is a computer-generated 3D space that surrounds the user perceptually, replacing or augmenting the real world through stereoscopic displays, spatial audio, head tracking, and motion controllers. KTU 2024 Scheme defines immersion as the *continuous, multi-sensory engagement* of a user in a synthetic world where the sensory cues (visual, auditory, haptic) are spatially and temporally consistent with the user's head and body motion. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-aligned):** > *An immersive VR environment is a real-time rendered, interactive 3D scene presented through a Head-Mounted Display (HMD), in which the user's pose (position + orientation) drives the rendered output, and external inputs (controllers, gestures, voice) modify scene state.* The three pillars that make the environment feel "alive" are: 1. **Animation** – the temporal evolution of scene elements (objects, characters, particles, lighting). 2. **Sound** – spatially localised audio that reacts to the listener's head pose. 3. **Interaction** – the bidirectional handshake between the user and the virtual world. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Living Theatre" Imagine walking into a fully built **theatre stage**: | Theatre Element | VR Equivalent | |---|---| | Stage lighting that fades and colour-shifts | **Animation** of lights, materials, and objects | | Orchestra whose violins appear louder from the left | **Spatial Sound** (3D / binaural audio) | | A door handle you can physically pull | **Interaction** (raycasting, grab mechanics, haptics) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Golden Rule of Immersion:** > A user will tolerate a visually poor VR scene if the **interaction latency < 20 ms** and the **sound matches the head-rotation**, but a single audio-visual desynchronisation of more than **60 ms** breaks presence immediately. This is the *plausibility illusion* principle. --- ## 1.3 Formal Definitions of the Three Sub-Topics ### 1.3.1 Animation **Animation** in VR is the procedurally or key-frame driven transformation of an object's transform (position, rotation, scale) and material properties (colour, emissive intensity) over time, evaluated every frame at the display refresh rate (typically **72 Hz / 90 Hz / 120 Hz** for modern HMDs). ### 1.3.2 Sound (Spatial Audio) **Spatial Sound** is audio rendered such that its perceived direction, distance, and environmental reverberation change as the user's head rotates. It relies on the **Head-Related Transfer Function (HRTF)**, a pair of filters modelling how sound from a 3D point reaches the two eardrums. ### 1.3.3 Interaction **Interaction** is the closed-loop pipeline that converts a user input (controller button, hand gesture, gaze direction) into a scene-state mutation, with feedback delivered visually, audibly, and haptically within one frame budget. > [!NOTE] > **Industry Standards (KTU Reference Values):** > - HMD refresh rate: **≥ 90 Hz** to avoid simulator sickness. > - Motion-to-Photon latency: **≤ 20 ms** (Microsoft, Oculus, HTC published baselines). > - Spatial audio update rate: matches render rate (**1× or 2× frame**). > -
