Controller polling rate: **≥ 250 Hz** (Meta Quest 2 controller spec). --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Animation Subsystem — Operational Pipeline The animation subsystem in any modern VR engine (Unity, Unreal, Godot) executes the following logic per frame: 1. **Sampling** the AnimationClip at the current time $t$. 2. **Blending** multiple clips using weights (e.g., upper-body *waving* blended with lower-body *walking*). 3. **Layering / Masking** to apply clips only to selected bones. 4. **IK Solving** (Inverse Kinematics) to drive end-effectors (hands, head) to controller targets. 5. **Applying** the resulting local transforms to the SkinnedMeshRenderer, then re-tessellating the mesh on the GPU. 6. **Submitting** to the render queue, ensuring the **V-Sync** boundary is met. ### 2.1.1 Key Mathematical Models #### (a) Linear Interpolation (Lerp) Used for the simplest animations (sliding doors, fading lights). $$P(t) = (1 - t) \cdot P_0 + t \cdot P_1, \quad t \in [0, 1]$$ Where $P_0$ is the start transform and $P_1$ is the end transform. #### (b) Cubic Bezier Curve (Smooth Animation) Used for camera easing, UI pop-ups. $$B(t) = (1 - t)^3 P_0 + 3(1 - t)^2 t \cdot P_1 + 3(1 - t) t^2 \cdot P_2 + t^3 P_3$$ #### (c) Frame Budget for Animation To guarantee smooth VR animation: $$f_{animation} \geq 2 \cdot f_{display}$$ That is, animation must update at **at least double the display rate** to prevent judder. For a **90 Hz HMD**, the animation tick must be **≥ 180 Hz**. --- ## 2.2 Sound Subsystem — Operational Pipeline The 3D audio engine (Unity: AudioSource + AudioListener; Unreal: Sound Attenuation + Spatialization) performs: 1. **Source classification** — point, line, area, ambient. 2. **Distance attenuation** — gain falls as the listener moves away. 3. **HRTF convolution** — the listener's head-shadow and pinna filters are applied. 4. **Doppler shift** — pitch changes with relative velocity. 5. **Reverb / occlusion** — environmental acoustic model applied. 6. **Binaural mix** — final two-channel stereo buffer sent to headphones. ### 2.2.1 Key Sound Equations #### (a) Inverse-Square Attenuation Sound intensity follows the inverse-square law in a free field: $$I(r) = \frac{I_0}{(1 + k r)^2} \approx \frac{I_0}{r^2} \quad \text{(free field, } r \gg \lambda \text{)}$$ Where $k$ is a roll-off factor. In game engines this is clamped to a `minDistance` and `maxDistance` to avoid the singularity at $r = 0$. #### (b) Doppler Frequency $$f_{\text{observed}} = f_{\text{source}} \cdot \frac{c + v_{\text{listener}}}{c + v_{\text{source}}}$$ Where $c \approx 343 \text{ m/s}$ is the speed of sound in air at **20 °C**. #### (c) HRTF — Conceptual Form The pressure at the left eardrum from a source at direction $(\theta, \phi)$ and distance $r$: $$p_L(t) = \frac{1}{r} \cdot h_L(\theta, \phi, t) * s(t)$$ Where $h_L$ is the **left-ear HRTF** (a finite impulse response filter) and $*$ denotes convolution. Engine implementations use a pre-measured HRTF database (e.g., SOFA-format CIPIC, MIT KEMAR). #### (d) Inter-Aural Time Difference (ITD) The path-length difference between the two ears for a source at azimuth $\theta$ (head radius $a \approx 0.0875$ m): $$\text{ITD} \approx \frac{a}{c} \cdot (\sin\theta + \theta) \quad \text{(low-frequency Woodworth model)}$$ For a head radius of $a = 8.75 \text{ cm}$ and a far-field source at $\theta = 90°$: $$\text{ITD}_{\max} \approx \frac{0.0875}{343} \cdot (1 + 1.5708) \approx 6.55 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 0.655 \text{ ms}$$ This is the *physical ceiling* of human sound localisation in the horizontal plane. --- ## 2.3 Interaction Subsystem — Operational Pipeline The interaction pipeline mirrors the classic HCI feedback loop: 1. **Polling** the input device (controller IMU at 250–1000 Hz, hand-tracking cameras at 60–120 Hz). 2. **Predicting** the user's intended pose to compensate for the **20 ms** display lag. 3. **Raycasting** from the controller tip or hand position to determine the targeted object. 4. **State machine** transition (e.g., *Idle → Hover → Grabbed → Released*). 5. **Physics / Constraint update** — applying spring-damper, joint, or rigidbody forces. 6. **Feedback dispatch** — visual highlight, audio click, controller vibration. ### 2.3.1 Key Interaction Equations #### (a) Ray-AABB Intersection (Object Selection) For a ray $\mathbf{R}(t) = \mathbf{O} + t \cdot \mathbf{D}$ and an axis-aligned bounding box $[x_{\min}, x_{\max}] \times [y_{\min}, y_{\max}] \times [z_{\min}, z_{\max}]$, the hit parameter is: $$t_{\text{hit}} = \max(t_{x,\min}, t_{y,\min}, t_{z,\min})$$ Where each $t_{i,\min} = (B_{i,\min} - O_i) / D_i$. #### (b) Spring-Damper Grab (Hand-Object Coupling) The classic critically-damped control law used by VR engines to make a grabbed object "stick" to the hand: $$\mathbf{F} = k (\mathbf{x}_{\text{target}} - \mathbf{x}_{\text{object}}) - c \cdot \mathbf{v}_{\text{object}}$$ With critical damping $c = 2\sqrt{k \cdot m}$, where $k$ is stiffness, $c$ is damping coefficient, $m$ is the grabbed object's mass. #### (c) Dead reckoning / Pose Prediction The controller's pose at the next frame is predicted by extrapolating velocity: $$\mathbf{x}_{\text{predicted}}(t + \Delta t) = \mathbf{x}(t) + \mathbf{v}(t) \cdot \Delta t + \tfrac{1}{2} \mathbf{a}(t) \cdot \Delta t^2$$ This pre-compensates for the **20 ms motion-to-photon** delay. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units / Typical Value | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Linear Interpolation | $P(t) = (1-t)P_0 + t P_1$ | $P_0, P_1$ = start/end; $t \in [0,1]$ | Dimensionless | | 2 | Cubic Bezier | $B(t) = (1-t)^3 P_0 + 3(1-t)^2 t P_1 + 3(1-t)t^2 P_2 + t^3 P_3$ | Control points $P_0..P_3$ | Dimensionless | | 3 | Animation Frame Rate | $f_a \geq 2 f_d$ | $f_a$ = animation Hz, $f_d$ = display Hz | Hz | | 4 | Inverse-Square Sound Law | $I(r) = I_0 / r^2$ | $I_0$ = source intensity at 1 m | W/m² | | 5 | Doppler Shift | $f' = f \cdot (c + v_L)/(c + v_S)$ | $c$ ≈ **343 m/s**, $v_L, v_S$ = velocities | Hz | | 6 | HRTF Convolution | $p_L(t) = \tfrac{1}{r} h_L(\theta,\phi,t) * s(t)$ | $h_L$ = left-ear impulse response | Pa | | 7 | ITD (Woodworth) | $\text{ITD} \approx \tfrac{a}{c}(\sin\theta + \theta)$ | $a$ = head radius **0.0875 m** | s | | 8 | Ray-AABB Hit | $t_{\text{hit}} = \max(t_{x,\min}, t_{y,\min}, t_{z,\min})$ | Bounding box extremes | m (world units) | | 9 | Spring-Damper Grab | $\mathbf{F} = k(\mathbf{x}_T - \mathbf{x}) - c \mathbf{v}$ | $k$ = stiffness, $c$ = damping | N | | 10 | Critical Damping | $c_{\text{crit}} = 2\sqrt{k m}$ | $m$ = object mass | N·s/m | | 11 | Pose Prediction | $\mathbf{x}(t+\Delta t) = \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{v}\Delta t + \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{a}\Delta t^2$ | $\Delta t$ = prediction horizon (~20 ms) | m | | 12 | Frame Budget | $t_{\text{frame}} = 1/f_d$ | $f_d$ = 90 Hz → 11.11 ms | s | | 13 | Pixel-to-Degree | $\alpha = 2 \arctan(\text{PPI}/(2 \cdot \text{PPM}))$ | PPI = pixels/inch, PPM = pixels/metre | ° (degrees) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-world engineering utility:** > - Items 1–3 drive **all** smooth motion design (games, flight sims, surgical training). > - Items 4–7 underpin **military and medical spatial-audio** research (Bose AR, Microsoft Soundscape). > - Items 8–11 are the literal **equations coded inside Unity XR Interaction Toolkit and Unreal's Motion Controller Component**. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Critical Damping Coefficient for a Grabbed VR Object **Problem:** A virtual toolbox of mass $m = 0.6 \text{ kg}$ is grabbed by the user. The XR Interaction Toolkit uses a spring-damper with stiffness $k = 800 \text{ N/m}$. Derive the damping coefficient $c$ such that the object follows the hand without oscillation (critically damped). ### Step-by-Step **Step 1 — Write the equation of motion of a mass-spring-damper system.** By Newton's Second Law, the net force on the grabbed object equals mass times acceleration: $$m \ddot{x} = -k(x - x_T) - c \dot{x}$$ Rearranging into standard form (homogeneous ODE): $$m \ddot{x} + c \dot{x} + k(x - x_T) = 0$$ **Step 2 — Define the displacement variable $y = x - x_T$.** Since $x_T$ is the target (hand) position, $y$ is the deviation of the object from the hand. Substituting $\ddot{y} = \ddot{x}$ and $\dot{y} = \dot{x}$: $$m \ddot{y} + c \dot{y} + k y = 0$$ **Step 3 — Assume a trial solution of the form $y(t) = e^{\lambda t}$.** (This is the standard approach for linear ODEs with constant coefficients.) $$m \lambda^2 e^{\lambda t} + c \lambda e^{\lambda t} + k e^{\lambda t} = 0$$ Dividing both sides by $e^{\lambda t}$ (which is never zero): $$m \lambda^2 + c \lambda + k = 0$$ **Step 4 — Solve the characteristic quadratic for $\lambda$.** $$\lambda = \frac{-c \pm \sqrt{c^2 - 4mk}}{2m}$$ **Step 5 — Identify the discriminant regimes.** $$\Delta = c^2 - 4mk$$ - $\Delta > 0$ → **over-damped** (sluggish, no overshoot) - $\Delta = 0$ → **critically damped** (fastest return, no overshoot) - $\Delta < 0$ → **under-damped** (oscillates — *bad* for VR grips!) **Step 6 — Set $\Delta = 0$ to find the critical damping coefficient.** $$c^2 - 4mk = 0 \implies c_{\text{crit}} = 2\sqrt{mk}$$ **Step 7 — Substitute the numerical values** $m = 0.6 \text{ kg}$, $k = 800 \text{ N/m}$. $$ \begin{aligned} c_{\text{crit}} &= 2\sqrt{0.6 \times 800} \\ &= 2\sqrt{480} \\ &= 2 \times 21.9089 \\ &= 43.818 \text{ N·s/m} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 8 — Interpretation.** A damping coefficient of $c \approx 43.82 \text{ N·s/m}$ guarantees that the toolbox snaps to the hand's pose in the **shortest possible time** without any visible spring oscillation. Any value below this (e.g., $c = 20$) would cause the object to "wobble" — a major immersion-breaking artefact. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — ITD (Inter-Aural Time Difference) Maximum **Problem:** For a human head of radius $a = 0.0875 \text{ m}$ and sound speed $c = 343 \text{ m/s}$, derive the maximum ITD that the auditory system can exploit for horizontal-plane localisation. Assume the source is in the far field. ### Step-by-Step **Step 1 — Geometry of the path difference.** For a source at azimuth $\theta$ (measured from the median plane), the path to the *far* ear is longer than the path to the *near* ear by: $$\Delta d = a(\theta + \sin\theta)$$ This is the **Woodworth formula** (valid for all $\theta \in [-\pi/2, \pi/2]$), derived by treating the head as a rigid sphere and the source as a plane wave in the far field. **Step 2 — Convert path difference to time difference.** $$\text{ITD} = \frac{\Delta d}{c} = \frac{a(\theta + \sin\theta)}{c}$$ **Step 3 — Find the maximum by differentiating with respect to $\theta$ and setting to zero.** $$\frac{d}{d\theta}\text{ITD} = \frac{a(1 + \cos\theta)}{c} = 0$$ $$ \begin{aligned} 1 + \cos\theta &= 0 \\ \cos\theta &= -1 \\ \theta_{\max} &= \pi \text{ rad} = 180° \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Recognise the constraint.** The Woodworth approximation is only valid for $|\theta| \leq 90°$ (a source cannot be "behind" the head symmetrically — the wave comes from the side at most). Therefore, the maximum is at the boundary $\theta = 90°$: $$\theta_{\max} = \frac{\pi}{2} \text{ rad}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute $\theta = \pi/2$ into the ITD formula.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{ITD}_{\max} &= \frac{a \left(\frac{\pi}{2} + \sin\frac{\pi}{2}\right)}{c} \\ &= \frac{a \left(\frac{\pi}{2} + 1\right)}{c} \\ &= \frac{0.0875 \times 2.5708}{343} \\ &= \frac{0.224945}{343} \\ &= 6.558 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} \\ &\approx 0.656 \text{ ms} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Interpretation.** A horizontal-plane sound source can be lateralised using only **timing cues up to ~656 μs**. This explains why spatial-audio engines quantise ITD at a sub-millisecond resolution — anything coarser blurs the azimuth. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pose Prediction for 20 ms Latency Compensation **Problem:** A VR controller is sampled at $t = 0$ with position $x_0 = 0.45 \text{ m}$, velocity $v_0 = 1.8 \text{ m/s}$, and acceleration $a_0 = 6.0 \text{ m/s}^2$. The HMD pipeline adds a constant 20 ms delay before rendering. Predict the controller's position at the *moment of display*. ### Step-by-Step **Step 1 — Identify the prediction horizon.** $$\Delta t = 20 \text{ ms} = 0.020 \text{ s}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the constant-acceleration kinematic equation.** $$x(t + \Delta t) = x(t) + v(t) \cdot \Delta t + \frac{1}{2} a(t) \cdot \Delta t^2$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the values.** $$ \begin{aligned} x_{\text{predicted}} &= 0.45 + (1.8)(0.020) + \frac{1}{2}(6.0)(0.020)^2 \\ &= 0.45 + 0.0360 + \frac{1}{2}(6.0)(0.0004) \\ &= 0.45 + 0.0360 + 0.0012 \\ &= 0.4872 \text{ m} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Compute the error if no prediction is used.** $$\epsilon_{\text{no-pred}} = x(t + \Delta t) - x(t) = 0.4872 - 0.45 = 0.0372 \text{ m} = 3.72 \text{ cm}$$ This 3.7 cm error is visible to the user as a "lag" between the controller's physical position and the virtual hand — enough to break presence. **Step 5 — Engine comment.** Unity's `XRController` and OpenXR's `XRPredictiveTrackingComponent` use exactly this Taylor-expansion-based extrapolation. Higher-order terms (jerk) are added in research-grade systems (e.g., Facebook's *DeepSLAM*). --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Property | Linear Lerp | Cubic Bezier | PhysX Rigidbody | AnimationClip | |---|---|---|---|---| | Curve type | $C^0$ continuous | $C^2$ continuous | ODE-integrated | $C^1$ sampled | | Computational cost | Trivial | 4 mults per axis | $O(n)$ body step | Memory-bound | | Best for | Doors, switches | UI pop-ups, camera | Thrown objects, ragdoll | Skeletal characters | | VR suitability | High (predictable) | High (smooth) | Medium (may jitter) | High (engine-optimised) | | Frame-budget cost | ~0.01 ms | ~0.05 ms | ~0.5–2 ms | ~0.1–1 ms | | Failure mode | Visible linearity | Overshoot if poorly tuned | Tunneling at high speed | Pop-in at low framerate | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level Architecture of an Immersive Environment ```mermaid flowchart TB A0[HMD Head Pose
90 Hz IMU poll] --> B1[Prediction Engine
20 ms compensation] A1[Controller IMU
250 Hz poll] --> B1 A2[Hand Tracking
60 Hz cameras] --> B1 B1 --> C1[Render Thread
90 Hz minimum] B1 --> C2[Audio Thread
180 Hz update] B1 --> C3[Physics Thread
Fixed 50 Hz] C1 --> D1[GPU Compositor] C2 --> D2[Binaural Output
Headphones] C3 --> D3[Haptic Actuator
LRA motors] D1 --> E0[User Perception] D2 --> E0 D3 --> E0 E0 -.feedback loop.-> A0 E0 -.feedback loop.-> A1 ``` ## 4.2 Animation Subsystem Flow (Per-Frame) ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[AnimationClip 1] --> B1[Sample at t] A2[AnimationClip 2] --> B1 A3[Procedural Noise] --> B1 B1 --> C1[Blender weights] C1 --> D1[IK Solver
Two-bone + FABRIK] D1 --> E1[Skinned Mesh Write] E1 --> F1[GPU Skinning] F1 --> G1[Render Submission] G1 --> H1[V-Sync Barrier] H1 --> I1[Display Frame 90 Hz] ``` ## 4.3 Spatial Sound Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TB S1[Source Audio Clip] --> S2[Resample to 48 kHz] S2 --> S3[Compute Distance r] S2 --> S4[Compute Azimuth theta] S2 --> S5[Compute Elevation phi] S3 --> S6[Inverse-Square Gain] S4 --> S7[HRTF Select from SOFA DB] S5 --> S7 S6 --> S8[Apply Reverb] S7 --> S8 S8 --> S9[Doppler Resample] S9 --> S10[Occlusion Test Raycast] S10 --> S11[Binaural Mix L and R] S11 --> S12[Headphone Output] ``` ## 4.4 Interaction State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> stateIdle stateIdle --> stateHover : Raycast hit stateHover --> stateHover : Object remains targeted stateHover --> stateIdle : Raycast miss stateHover --> stateGrabbed : Trigger pressed stateGrabbed --> stateHover : Trigger released stateGrabbed --> stateGrabbed : Spring-damper update stateHover --> stateSelected : Touchpad tap stateSelected --> stateHover : Touchpad release stateGrabbed --> stateIdle : Out of range ``` ## 4.5 Interaction Event Sequence Diagram ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User Hand participant C as Controller Driver participant E as XR Engine participant O as Virtual Object participant H as Haptic Motor U->>C: Button press 0 ms C->>E: Input event at 4 ms E->>E: Raycast + IK at 6 ms E->>O: State to Grabbed at 8 ms O-->>E: New pose at 9 ms E->>H: Buzz command 60 percent at 10 ms E->>E: GPU render at 11 ms H-->>U: Tactile pulse 12 to 18 ms E-->>U: Visual frame 20 ms total ``` ## 4.6 Data-Flow of an Immersive Scene Update ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph PreFrame[Pre-Frame Setup] A1[Load SOFA HRTF] A2[Compile Shaders] A3[Bind Audio Bus] end subgraph FrameLoop[Per-Frame Loop] B1[Update Input] B2[Update Animation] B3[Update Audio] B4[Update Physics] B5[Update Interaction] B6[Submit to GPU] end subgraph PostFrame[Post-Frame] C1[Present via V-Sync] C2[Trigger Haptics] end PreFrame --> FrameLoop FrameLoop --> PostFrame FrameLoop -.next frame.-> FrameLoop ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams:** > - **Node IDs** (`A1`, `B2`, etc.) are alphanumeric-safe — they satisfy the Mermaid Compilation Safeguard rules. > - **Directional arrows** indicate *control flow*, not data type. > - **Boxes with double borders** mark hardware boundaries (HMD, GPU, motor). --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define spatial sound in an immersive VR environment. List any two HRTF-based cues the brain uses to localise a sound source.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (1 mark):** Spatial sound is audio rendered such that its perceived direction, distance, and environmental reverberation change in response to the listener's head pose and position. It is implemented using binaural rendering driven by a Head-Related Transfer Function (HRTF). > **Cue 1 — Inter-Aural Time Difference (ITD) (1 mark):** The path-length difference between the two ears produces a small arrival-time difference. For an azimuth of 90°, ITD ≈ **0.66 ms** (a = 0.0875 m, c = 343 m/s). The auditory brainstem uses this for low-frequency localisation (below ~1.5 kHz). > **Cue 2 — Inter-Aural Level Difference (ILD) (1 mark):** The head casts an acoustic shadow, attenuating the far ear by up to **20 dB** at high frequencies (> 3 kHz). The brain uses this for elevation and high-frequency azimuth. --- ### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is meant by "Motion-to-Photon Latency" in VR? State its recommended maximum value and explain why exceeding it causes simulator sickness.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (1 mark):** Motion-to-Photon Latency (M2P) is the elapsed time between a real physical movement of the user's head (or controller) and the corresponding updated pixels becoming visible on the HMD display. > **Recommended value (1 mark):** The accepted industry maximum is **≤ 20 ms** (with ideal systems targeting ≤ 11 ms for 90 Hz operation). > **Reason for sickness (1 mark):** When latency exceeds ~20 ms, the vestibular system (inner-ear balance) reports motion that the visual system has not yet seen, producing a **sensory mismatch**. The brain interprets this as possible neuro-toxin ingestion, triggering nausea, disorientation, and cold sweat — the symptoms of *simulator sickness* (a form of motion sickness). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]` **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **three-stage pipeline** of integrating animation, sound, and interaction in an immersive VR environment. For each stage, state its primary input, processing block, and output. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A virtual door of mass **2.5 kg** is grabbed by the user through a VR controller using a spring-damper coupling. The spring stiffness is **$k = 1200 \text{ N/m}$**. Compute the critical damping coefficient and describe what happens if the engine uses **half** this value. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks) **Step 1 (2 marks) — Block Diagram:** The three-stage pipeline is **Input → Processing → Feedback**. | Stage | Input | Processing | Output | |---|---|---|---| | 1. Sensing | HMD IMU, controller pose, hand-tracker skeleton | Sensor fusion (complementary / Kalman filter) | Pose with predicted latency compensation | | 2. Scene Update | Predicted pose, animation clips, audio buffers, physics state | Animation blending, HRTF convolution, physics integration, interaction state machine | New transforms, new audio samples, new forces | | 3. Feedback | New scene state | Render to GPU, mix binaural audio, drive LRA haptics | Updated pixels, sound at ears, vibration on skin | **Step 2 (2 marks) — Animation sub-pipeline:** - Sample clip at time *t* → blend with weights → mask to bones → apply IK → skin mesh → submit. **Step 3 (2 marks) — Sound sub-pipeline:** - Compute distance & direction → attenuate via inverse-square → apply HRTF → add Doppler → mix L/R → headphone. **Step 4 (1 mark) — Interaction sub-pipeline:** - Raycast → detect object → state machine (Idle → Hover → Grabbed) → spring-damper update → visual highlight + haptic buzz. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks) **Given:** $m = 2.5 \text{ kg}$, $k = 1200 \text{ N/m}$. **Step 1 (1 mark) — Write the critical damping formula:** $$c_{\text{crit}} = 2\sqrt{mk}$$ **Step 2 (2 marks) — Substitute and compute:** $$ \begin{aligned} c_{\text{crit}} &= 2\sqrt{2.5 \times 1200} \\ &= 2\sqrt{3000} \\ &= 2 \times 54.7723 \\ &= 109.5446 \text{ N·s/m} \\ &\approx 109.5 \text{ N·s/m} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 (1 mark) — State the half-damping value:** $$c_{\text{half}} = \frac{1}{2} c_{\text{crit}} \approx 54.77 \text{ N·s/m}$$ **Step 4 (2 marks) — Classify the regime.** The discriminant of the characteristic equation is: $$\Delta = c^2 - 4mk = (54.77)^2 - 4(2.5)(1200) = 3000 - 12000 = -9000 < 0$$ Hence the system is **under-damped** ($\Delta < 0$). The door will oscillate around the hand's position before settling. **Step 5 (1 mark) — Engineering consequence.** A visibly oscillating door in the user's hand breaks the plausibility illusion and may trigger motion sickness. The engine should use $c = c_{\text{crit}} \approx 109.5 \text{ N·s/m}$ (or slightly over-damped, e.g., $1.2 \times c_{\text{crit}}$) for a "weighty" but stable grip. --- ### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]` **(a)** Derive the **Woodworth formula for Inter-Aural Time Difference (ITD)** for a sound source at azimuth $\theta$ in the horizontal plane. Use $a = 0.0875 \text{ m}$ and $c = 343 \text{ m/s}$ to compute the maximum ITD and state its significance in spatial-audio engine design. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **Inverse-Kinematics (IK) two-bone solver** used in VR to position a virtual hand on a grabbed object's surface. Show how the elbow position is computed from the shoulder, hand, and upper-arm / forearm lengths. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks) **Step 1 (2 marks) — Geometric setup.** Treat the head as a rigid sphere of radius $a$. The source is in the far field at azimuth $\theta$ (angle from the median plane). The path to the near ear is shorter by: $$\Delta d = a(\theta + \sin\theta)$$ This is the **Woodworth formula**, derived by computing the arc-length difference between the two great-circle paths from the source plane to each ear. **Step 2 (1 mark) — Convert path to time:** $$\text{ITD}(\theta) = \frac{\Delta d}{c} = \frac{a(\theta + \sin\theta)}{c}$$ **Step 3 (2 marks) — Maximise.** The derivative with respect to $\theta$ is: $$\frac{d}{d\theta}\text{ITD} = \frac{a(1 + \cos\theta)}{c}$$ This is zero at $\theta = \pi$, but the formula is only valid for $|\theta| \leq \pi/2$, so the maximum occurs at the boundary $\theta_{\max} = \pi/2$ rad = 90°. **Step 4 (2 marks) — Numerical evaluation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{ITD}_{\max} &= \frac{0.0875 \left(\frac{\pi}{2} + \sin\frac{\pi}{2}\right)}{343} \\ &= \frac{0.0875 \times 2.5708}{343} \\ &= \frac{0.2249}{343} \\ &\approx 6.56 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} \\ &\approx 0.656 \text{ ms} \end{aligned} $$ **Significance (included in Step 4):** Spatial-audio engines must quantise ITD at sub-millisecond resolution (typically **≤ 10 μs**) to preserve the full azimuth localisation cue. Sub-optimal ITD latency produces blurred or "in-the-head" localisation, breaking immersion. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks) **Step 1 (1 mark) — Define the IK chain.** Let the **shoulder** be at $\mathbf{S}$, the **hand** (end-effector) at $\mathbf{H}$, the **upper-arm** of length $L_1$, the **forearm** of length $L_2$, and the **elbow** at $\mathbf{E}$. **Step 2 (1 mark) — Compute the shoulder-to-hand distance:** $$d = \vert \mathbf{H} - \mathbf{S} \vert$$ **Step 3 (1 mark) — Use the law of cosines to find the elbow angle at the shoulder $\alpha$:** $$\cos\alpha = \frac{L_1^2 + d^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 d}$$ **Step 4 (2 marks) — Compute the pole (rotation axis) and bend direction.** The pole is perpendicular to the plane formed by $\mathbf{S}$, $\mathbf{H}$, and the "up" vector. The bend direction is chosen by a *pole vector* constraint (e.g., the elbow points slightly backward for a human arm). **Step 5 (1 mark) — Compute the elbow position via rotation around the pole:** $$\mathbf{E} = \mathbf{S} + R_{\text{pole}}(\alpha) \cdot \left( L_1 \cdot \hat{u}_{\text{arm}} \right)$$ Where $\hat{u}_{\text{arm}}$ is the unit vector from shoulder to hand, and $R_{\text{pole}}(\alpha)$ is the rotation matrix that swings the upper arm by angle $\alpha$ toward the pole. **Step 6 (1 mark) — VR-specific note.** Unity's Animation Rigging package and Unreal's *Two Bone IK* node implement exactly this solver. In VR, the hand target $\mathbf{H}$ is the **predicted** controller position (from Section 3.3) to avoid visible IK lag. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks on this topic — Examiner's Pitfall List:** > 1. **Confusing *Animation* with *Particle System*.** Animation modifies object **transforms** over time; particles are a specific *emission-and-integration* subsystem. Mixing the two loses definition marks. > 2. **Forgetting units in the ITD formula.** Always state $a$ in metres, $c$ in m/s, and ITD in seconds. Writing "ITD = 0.66" without units is a **−0.5 mark** deduction. > 3. **Stating the HMD refresh rate as 60 Hz.** Modern KTU lab equipment (Meta Quest 3, HTC Vive XR Elite) runs at **90 Hz or 120 Hz**. Using 60 Hz loses currency marks. > 4. **Omitting the prediction step in interaction.** If a student writes only the raycast equation without mentioning the **20 ms pose prediction**, they forfeit the "feedback-loop completeness" mark. > 5. **Mixing up *Doppler* (sound) and *inverse-square* (intensity).** Doppler changes *frequency*; inverse-square changes *amplitude*. Conflating them is a common error. > 6. **Forgetting to label the Mermaid node IDs in alphanumeric form.** A node literally called `End` will crash the renderer in the KTU digital-evaluation portal. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision — Print This Block** - [ ] **Immersive Environment = Visual + Auditory + Interactive loop closed inside ~20 ms.** - [ ] **HMD refresh rate ≥ 90 Hz**; animation tick ≥ 180 Hz. - [ ] **Animation pipeline:** Sample → Blend → Mask → IK → Skin → Submit. - [ ] **Lerp:** $P(t) = (1-t)P_0 + t P_1$, with $t \in [0,1]$. - [ ] **Cubic Bezier** uses 4 control points $P_0, P_1, P_2, P_3$. - [ ] **Inverse-square law:** $I(r) = I_0/r^2$ (sound intensity in free field). - [ ] **Doppler:** $f' = f \cdot (c + v_L)/(c + v_S)$, with $c = 343 \text{ m/s}$ at 20 °C. - [ ] **HRTF** is a pair of FIR filters $h_L(\theta,\phi,t)$ and $h_R(\theta,\phi,t)$ convolved with source $s(t)$. - [ ] **ITD max ≈ 0.656 ms** at $\theta = 90°$, $a = 0.0875$ m, $c = 343$ m/s (Woodworth). - [ ] **ILD up to ~20 dB** for high frequencies due to head shadow. - [ ] **Interaction pipeline:** Poll → Predict (20 ms) → Raycast → State machine → Spring-damper → Feedback. - [ ] **Ray-AABB hit:** $t_{\text{hit}} = \max(t_{x,\min}, t_{y,\min}, t_{z,\min})$. - [ ] **Spring-damper grab:** $\mathbf{F} = k(\mathbf{x}_T - \mathbf{x}) - c \mathbf{v}$. - [ ] **Critical damping:** $c_{\text{crit}} = 2\sqrt{mk}$ — fastest return with no overshoot. - [ ] **State machine:** Idle → Hover → Grabbed → Released. - [ ] **Haptic feedback:** LRA motors, 60–250 Hz, ≤ 20 ms command latency. - [ ] **Pose prediction:** $x(t+\Delta t) = x + v\Delta t + \tfrac{1}{2} a \Delta t^2$ with $\Delta t = 20$ ms. - [ ] **Spatial audio update rate ≥ 1× display rate** (Unity default: 2× frame). ---

topics.title: Create a Runner Game Using Unity3D Template with Custom Assets and Features (Increase/Decrease Player Speed)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCNL508 - VR LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Create a Runner Game Using Unity3D Template with Custom Assets and Features (Increase/Decrease Player Speed) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Runner Game** in Unity3D is an **endless side-scrolling (or third-person) arcade-style game** in which the player character continuously moves forward through a procedurally generated environment while the player inputs only directional, vertical, or action commands (jump, slide, turn). The defining characteristic is that the **world is the illusion of motion**, achieved by translating obstacles, terrain tiles, and scenery backward relative to the player, while the player character itself either runs in place or moves forward at a constant velocity vector. In the context of the **KTU PCCNL508 – VR Lab (2024 Scheme)** Module 2 experiment, a Runner Game is constructed using the **Unity 3D Engine** by importing custom assets (3D models, textures, audio, animations), assembling them into a **Scene**, and attaching **C# scripts as Components** to GameObjects to govern behavior. The "**Increase/Decrease Player Speed**" feature is implemented as a gameplay modifier that alters the player's translation velocity (or the world-scroll velocity) in response to **in-game triggers, collectibles, power-ups, or UI input**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 of PCCNL508 focuses on *Game Engine Fundamentals using Unity3D*. The runner game is the prescribed template because it exercises every core engine subsystem: **Input, Physics, Animation, UI, Asset Pipeline, Prefab Instantiation, and Script-based State Management**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The Moving Sidewalk at an Airport** > > Imagine you are standing on a **moving walkway (travelator)** at an airport. You are not actually walking forward — the floor is moving *under* your feet. To an outside observer, you are progressing through the terminal. Now imagine that the terminal is filled with **stationary obstacles** (pillars, benches, people). Your job is simply to *shift left or right* (or jump over) to avoid them. The faster the walkway moves, the harder the game becomes. A "**speed boost**" power-up in the runner game is the equivalent of the walkway suddenly accelerating; a "**slow-down trap**" is the walkway briefly decelerating or reversing. In this analogy: - The **player GameObject** = You standing on the walkway. - The **obstacles** = Pillars and benches sliding past you. - The **player speed variable** = The belt speed of the walkway. - The **Input system** = Your left/right and jump muscles. - The **ground tiles / world** = The conveyor belt surface. ## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board Definition Format) | Term | KTU-Standard Definition | |---|---| | **GameObject** | The fundamental entity in Unity; an empty container in the Scene that can hold **Components** (Transform, Mesh, Script, Collider). | | **Component** | A modular piece of behavior or data attached to a GameObject (e.g., `Rigidbody`, `BoxCollider`, custom C# script). | | **Prefab** | A reusable, serialized GameObject template stored in the **Project Window**; instances are spawned at runtime via `Instantiate()`. | | **Transform** | The component that defines a GameObject's **Position, Rotation, and Scale** in 3D space. | | **Rigidbody** | A physics component that places the GameObject under the control of Unity's **PhysX engine**, enabling gravity and forces. | | **Collider** | An invisible geometric shape used for **collision detection**; pairs with `OnCollisionEnter()` callbacks. | | **ScriptableObject** | A data container asset (used here to store speed presets) that lives in the Project, not the Scene. | | **Delta Time (`Time.deltaTime`)** | The time in **seconds** between the current and previous frame; the canonical multiplier for frame-rate-independent movement. | | **Asset** | Any file imported into Unity's Project window: **.fbx, .png, .wav, .cs, .unity, .prefab, .mat**. | ## 1.4 Physical / Engine Constants Used in This Experiment - **Gravity (Unity Default):** $g = -9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ along the world Y-axis. - **Fixed Timestep (Physics):** $\Delta t_{\text{phys}} = 0.02 \text{ s}$ (default 50 Hz). - **Standard Player Speed:** $v_{\text{base}} = 5 \text{ m/s}$ (typical for tutorial runner games). - **Speed Multiplier Range:** $k \in [0.5, \; 3.0]$ (50% to 300% of base speed). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Core Mathematical Model of a Runner Game A runner game's motion is governed by a simple first-order kinematic equation applied per frame. The student's job in the lab is to translate this equation into Unity C# code. The **position update** of the player (or the equivalent world-scroll) is given by: $$ \vec{p}(t + \Delta t) = \vec{p}(t) + \vec{v}(t) \cdot \Delta t $$ Where: - $\vec{p}(t)$ — Position vector of the player at time $t$ (in meters, world coordinates). - $\vec{v}(t)$ — Velocity vector of the player at time $t$ (in m/s). - $\Delta t$ — Frame delta time (`Time.deltaTime` in Unity, typically $\approx 0.0167$ s at 60 FPS). For a runner game moving strictly along the **+Z axis** (or -Z, depending on orientation), the velocity vector reduces to: $$ \vec{v}(t) = \left(0, \; 0, \; v_{\text{base}} \cdot k(t)\right) $$ Where $k(t)$ is the **speed multiplier**, a time-dependent scalar that is **modified by power-ups**. ## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown The runner game system can be decomposed into **five cooperating subsystems**. Each subsystem is a Unity Component or script pair. ### 2.2.1 The Input Subsystem - **Role:** Translates keyboard / mouse / touch into discrete game actions. - **Why:** Decouples hardware input from game logic. - **How:** Unity's `Input.GetKey(KeyCode.LeftArrow)` (legacy) or the new `Input System Package` (`Keyboard.current.leftArrowKey.isPressed`). ### 2.2.2 The Player Locomotion Subsystem - **Role:** Updates the Transform's position each frame. - **Why:** Implements the kinematic equation $\vec{p}_{n+1} = \vec{p}_n + \vec{v} \cdot \Delta t$. - **How:** `transform.Translate(Vector3.forward * speed * Time.deltaTime);` ### 2.2.3 The Speed Modifier Subsystem - **Role:** Listens for **power-up collision events** and modifies $k(t)$. - **Why:** Provides gameplay variety and player agency. - **How:** Two public methods — `IncreaseSpeed(float amount)` and `DecreaseSpeed(float amount)` — clamped between min and max bounds. ### 2.2.4 The Obstacle / Spawner Subsystem - **Role:** Periodically instantiates obstacle prefabs ahead of the player. - **Why:** Maintains gameplay density; the world must never feel empty. - **How:** A timed coroutine or `InvokeRepeating` calling `Instantiate(obstaclePrefab, spawnPoint.position, Quaternion.identity)`. ### 2.2.5 The UI / HUD Subsystem - **Role:** Displays the current speed, score, and lives remaining. - **Why:** Provides feedback — the heart of **game feel**. - **How:** Updates a `UnityEngine.UI.Text` or `TextMeshProUGUI` element in the `Update()` loop. ## 2.3 The Speed Increase / Decrease Logic (Detailed) The speed multiplier is governed by a **bounded linear update rule**: $$ k_{n+1} = \text{Clamp}\!\left(k_n + \Delta k, \; k_{\min}, \; k_{\max}\right) $$ Where: - $k_n$ — Current multiplier (dimensionless, initially 1.0). - $\Delta k$ — Increment (positive for boost, negative for slow-down). - $k_{\min}, k_{\max}$ — Lower and upper bounds (typically 0.5 and 3.0). - The `Clamp` function is implemented as: $$ \text{Clamp}(x, a, b) = \max\!\left(a, \; \min(x, b)\right) $$ This ensures the multiplier never falls below $k_{\min}$ or exceeds $k_{\max}$, **preventing the player from becoming unplayably slow or uncontrollably fast**. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Constants Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Constant | Symbolic Form | Numerical Default | Purpose in Game | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Position Update | $\vec{p}_{n+1} = \vec{p}_n + \vec{v}_n \cdot \Delta t$ | — | Per-frame movement | | 2 | Speed Multiplier Update | $k_{n+1} = \text{Clamp}(k_n + \Delta k, k_{\min}, k_{\max})$ | $k_{\min}=0.5, k_{\max}=3.0$ | Power-up effect | | 3 | Effective Player Velocity | $v_{\text{eff}}(t) = v_{\text{base}} \cdot k(t)$ | $v_{\text{base}} = 5$ m/s | Total translation speed | | 4 | Score Update (distance-based) | $S(t) = S_0 + \int_0^t v_{\text{eff}}(\tau) \, d\tau$ | — | Score in meters traveled | | 5 | Unity Gravity | $\vec{g} = (0, -9.81, 0)$ m/s² | $-9.81$ m/s² | Jump arc | | 6 | Jump Impulse | $v_y = \sqrt{2 \cdot g \cdot h}$ | $h = 1.5$ m | Initial vertical velocity | | 7 | Spawn Period | $T_{\text{spawn}} = T_0 / k(t)$ | $T_0 = 1.5$ s | Obstacle frequency scaling | | 8 | Delta Time | $\Delta t = \text{Time.deltaTime}$ | $\approx 0.0167$ s @ 60 FPS | Frame-rate independence | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility Beyond Gaming:** The bounded scalar update rule $k_{n+1} = \text{Clamp}(k_n + \Delta k, \ldots)$ is identical in form to **PI controller anti-windup clamping**, **battery charge state of charge limits (SoC $\in [0\%, 100\%]$)**, and **signal saturation in operational amplifiers**. This is why runner games make excellent pedagogical tools for control theory students. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Parallels | Game Concept | Engineering Parallel | |---|---| | Player speed multiplier | **Throttle position** in a cruise control system | | Power-up collision | **Sensor trigger event** in an IoT pipeline | | Spawner coroutine | **Real-time task scheduler** in an RTOS | | Rigidbody physics | **Newtonian dynamics simulation** in robotics | | Prefab instantiation | **Object pooling / factory pattern** in software engineering | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix This section provides the **exhaustive, board-exam-quality derivations** for every formula, code block, and logical step the student is expected to reproduce during the lab viva and external examination. ## 3.1 Derivation of the Frame-Rate Independent Movement Equation **Problem:** A player moves with intended velocity $v_{\text{base}} = 5$ m/s. On a 60 FPS machine, $\Delta t = 1/60$ s per frame. On a 30 FPS machine, $\Delta t = 1/30$ s per frame. Show why multiplying by `Time.deltaTime` is mathematically required. ### Step 1: Define the Continuous-Time Velocity By definition, instantaneous velocity is the time derivative of position: $$ v(t) = \frac{dp(t)}{dt} $$ ### Step 2: Discretize via Forward Euler Integration For a game running in discrete frames $t_n = n \cdot \Delta t$, we approximate the derivative as a first-order forward difference: $$ v(t_n) \approx \frac{p(t_{n+1}) - p(t_n)}{\Delta t} $$ ### Step 3: Solve for the Next Position Multiplying both sides by $\Delta t$ and rearranging: $$ p(t_{n+1}) = p(t_n) + v(t_n) \cdot \Delta t $$ ### Step 4: Verify Frame-Rate Independence On a 60 FPS machine: $$ p_{60} = p_0 + v \cdot \frac{1}{60} \cdot 60 = p_0 + v \cdot 1 \text{ s} $$ On a 30 FPS machine: $$ p_{30} = p_0 + v \cdot \frac{1}{30} \cdot 30 = p_0 + v \cdot 1 \text{ s} $$ Both yield identical displacement after one second. **This is why `Time.deltaTime` is mandatory in any `Update()` method that moves an object.** ### Step 5: Translate to C# Code ```csharp void Update() { float displacement = playerSpeed * Time.deltaTime; transform.Translate(Vector3.forward * displacement); } ``` ## 3.2 Derivation of the Jump Initial Velocity from Desired Height **Problem:** The player should jump to a maximum height of $h = 1.5$ m under default gravity $g = -9.81$ m/s². Find the required initial vertical velocity $v_{y,0}$. ### Step 1: Use Conservation of Energy (or Kinematic Equation) At the apex of the jump, vertical velocity is zero. Using the kinematic equation: $$ v_y^2 = v_{y,0}^2 + 2 g \Delta y $$ ### Step 2: Apply Boundary Conditions At apex: $v_y = 0$ and $\Delta y = h$ (displacement upward is positive). $$ 0 = v_{y,0}^2 - 2 \cdot 9.81 \cdot h $$ ### Step 3: Solve for $v_{y,0}$ $$ v_{y,0} = \sqrt{2 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 1.5} = \sqrt{29.43} \approx 5.42 \text{ m/s} $$ ### Step 4: Translate to C# Code ```csharp public float jumpHeight = 1.5f; private Rigidbody rb; void Jump() { float v = Mathf.Sqrt(2 * Mathf.Abs(Physics.gravity.y) * jumpHeight); rb.linearVelocity = new Vector3(rb.linearVelocity.x, v, rb.linearVelocity.z); } ``` > [!NOTE] > In Unity 6 (used in the 2024 Scheme), the property `Rigidbody.velocity` was renamed to `Rigidbody.linearVelocity` for clarity. The older syntax is still valid but generates a deprecation warning. ## 3.3 Complete Analytical Matrix — Speed Control Script Logic The Increase/Decrease Speed feature is the **single most heavily weighted section** in the viva. The following table presents the **complete state transition matrix** that examiners love to test. | Current $k$ | Event | $\Delta k$ | New $k$ (Before Clamp) | After Clamp $[0.5, 3.0]$ | C# Effect | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1.0 | Boost pickup | +0.5 | 1.5 | **1.5** | Player speeds up | | 1.5 | Boost pickup | +0.5 | 2.0 | **2.0** | Faster | | 2.5 | Boost pickup | +0.5 | 3.0 | **3.0** | Max speed reached | | 3.0 | Boost pickup | +0.5 | 3.5 | **3.0** (clamped) | No change — clamped | | 1.0 | Trap hit | -0.3 | 0.7 | **0.7** | Player slows down | | 0.7 | Trap hit | -0.3 | 0.4 | **0.5** (clamped) | Min speed reached | | 0.5 | Trap hit | -0.3 | 0.2 | **0.5** (clamped) | No change — clamped | ## 3.4 Reference C# Scripts (Model Solutions for the Lab Record) ### 3.4.1 `PlayerController.cs` — Locomotion + Speed Control ```csharp using UnityEngine; public class PlayerController : MonoBehaviour { [Header("Movement Settings")] public float baseSpeed = 5f; public float laneDistance = 2.5f; public float jumpHeight = 1.5f; [Header("Speed Control Bounds")] public float minMultiplier = 0.5f; public float maxMultiplier = 3.0f; public float speedStep = 0.5f; private float currentMultiplier = 1.0f; private Rigidbody rb; void Start() { rb = GetComponent(); } void Update() { // Forward auto-run (frame-rate independent) float effectiveSpeed = baseSpeed * currentMultiplier; transform.Translate(Vector3.forward * effectiveSpeed * Time.deltaTime); // Lane switching if (Input.GetKeyDown(KeyCode.LeftArrow)) transform.Translate(Vector3.left * laneDistance); if (Input.GetKeyDown(KeyCode.RightArrow)) transform.Translate(Vector3.right * laneDistance); // Jump if (Input.GetKeyDown(KeyCode.Space) && Mathf.Abs(rb.linearVelocity.y) < 0.01f) { float v = Mathf.Sqrt(2f * Mathf.Abs(Physics.gravity.y) * jumpHeight); rb.linearVelocity = new Vector3(rb.linearVelocity.x, v, 0f); } } public void IncreaseSpeed() { currentMultiplier = Mathf.Clamp( currentMultiplier + speedStep, minMultiplier, maxMultiplier); } public void DecreaseSpeed() { currentMultiplier = Mathf.Clamp( currentMultiplier - speedStep, minMultiplier, maxMultiplier); } public float GetCurrentMultiplier() => currentMultiplier; } ``` ### 3.4.2 `SpeedPickup.cs` — Collectible Trigger ```csharp using UnityEngine; public class SpeedPickup : MonoBehaviour { public enum PickupType { Boost, Slowdown } public PickupType type = PickupType.Boost; void OnTriggerEnter(Collider other) { if (other.CompareTag("Player")) { PlayerController pc = other.GetComponent(); if (type == PickupType.Boost) pc.IncreaseSpeed(); else pc.DecreaseSpeed(); Destroy(gameObject); } } } ``` ### 3.4.3 `UIManager.cs` — HUD Display ```csharp using UnityEngine; using TMPro; public class UIManager : MonoBehaviour { public TextMeshProUGUI speedText; public PlayerController player; void Update() { float effectiveSpeed = player.baseSpeed * player.GetCurrentMultiplier(); speedText.text = "Speed: " + effectiveSpeed.ToString("F1") + " m/s"; } } ``` ## 3.5 Unity Editor Workflow (Step-by-Step Lab Procedure) 1. **Create Project:** Unity Hub → New Project → 3D (URP) → Name: `KTU_RunnerGame`. 2. **Import Custom Assets:** Drag `.fbx` (player model), `.png` (textures), `.wav` (sound) into `Assets/`. 3. **Build the Player:** Create an empty GameObject → Add `MeshFilter`, `MeshRenderer`, `BoxCollider`, `Rigidbody`, `PlayerController.cs`. 4. **Build the Ground:** Create a long Plane (Scale Z = 50). Tag it `"Ground"`. 5. **Build Obstacle Prefab:** Cube with `BoxCollider` → Drag into `Assets/Prefabs/` to create prefab. 6. **Build Pickup Prefab:** Sphere with `Is Trigger = true` Collider → Attach `SpeedPickup.cs`. 7. **Add Spawner:** Empty GameObject → Attach `ObstacleSpawner.cs` (uses `InvokeRepeating`). 8. **Add UI:** Canvas → Text (TMP) → Anchor top-left. 9. **Add Lighting:** Directional Light with shadows enabled. 10. **Play & Test:** Press ▶ — verify forward motion, lane switching, jump, and speed pickup behavior. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 System Block Diagram — Runner Game Architecture The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **complete functional architecture** of the runner game, showing the data flow between all major subsystems. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input System
Keyboard Touch Mouse] --> B[Player Controller Script] B --> C[Transform Component
Position Update] C --> D[Rigidbody Physics Engine] D --> E[Collider System
OnCollisionEnter OnTriggerEnter] E --> F{Speed Pickup
Collision Check} F -->|Boost Type| G[Increase Speed Method
k = k + delta k] F -->|Slowdown Type| H[Decrease Speed Method
k = k - delta k] G --> I[Clamp Function
k in 0.5 to 3.0] H --> I I --> J[Effective Velocity
v = v base times k] J --> K[Transform Translate
per Frame] K --> L[Rendered Scene Output] M[Obstacle Spawner
InvokeRepeating] --> N[Instantiate Prefab] N --> C O[UI Manager Script] --> P[TextMeshPro HUD
Display Speed] J --> O L --> Q[Player Visual Feedback] P --> Q ``` ## 4.2 State Machine — Player Speed States The player exists in one of several discrete speed states, modeled as a **finite state machine**. This is the diagram the examiner will ask you to redraw during the viva. ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Normal Normal --> Fast: Boost Pickup Collected
k = k + 0.5 Normal --> Slow: Trap Hit
k = k - 0.3 Fast --> Normal: Clamp Reached
k greater than 3.0 Fast --> VeryFast: Another Boost
k less than 3.0 VeryFast --> Normal: k = 3.0 Clamped Slow --> Normal: k = 0.5 Clamped Normal --> [*]: Game Over Fast --> [*]: Collision with Obstacle VeryFast --> [*]: Collision with Obstacle Slow --> [*]: Collision with Obstacle ``` ## 4.3 GameObject Hierarchy in the Unity Hierarchy Window ```mermaid graph TD SceneRoot[Scene Root] --> Player[Player
Tag Player] Player --> PlayerModel[3D Model Child] Player --> PlayerCollider[Box Collider] Player --> PlayerRigidbody[Rigidbody] Player --> PlayerScript[Player Controller Script] SceneRoot --> Ground[Ground Plane
Tag Ground] SceneRoot --> Spawner[Obstacle Spawner
Script] SceneRoot --> Pickup1[Speed Boost Pickup
Sphere Trigger] SceneRoot --> Pickup2[Slowdown Trap Pickup
Cube Trigger] SceneRoot --> MainCamera[Main Camera
Follows Player] SceneRoot --> DirectionalLight[Directional Light] SceneRoot --> Canvas[UI Canvas] Canvas --> SpeedText[Speed Display TMP] Canvas --> ScoreText[Score Display TMP] SceneRoot --> PrefabFolder[Obstacle Prefab
Stored in Assets Prefabs] ``` ## 4.4 Asset Pipeline Flow Diagram This diagram shows how raw source files (created in external tools like Blender, Photoshop, Audacity) flow through Unity's import pipeline and end up as runtime game assets. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[External Source Files
FBX from Blender
PNG from Photoshop
WAV from Audacity] --> B[Unity Project Window
Assets Folder] B --> C[Asset Importer
Texture Compressor
Model Optimizer] C --> D[Imported Assets
Materials Meshes Clips] D --> E[Prefab Creation
Reusable Templates] E --> F[Scene Assembly
Hierarchy Window] F --> G[Component Attachment
Scripts Colliders] G --> H[Build Process
PC Android WebGL] H --> I[Runtime Game Executable] J[C Sharp Script
MonoBehaviour] --> F K[ScriptableObject Data] --> E ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define a Runner Game. List any four core components that must be implemented in a basic Unity3D runner game template.** **Model Answer:** A **Runner Game** is an endless arcade genre where the player character moves continuously forward through a procedurally generated environment while the player provides only directional or action input (lane change, jump). **Four core components:** 1. **Player Controller Script** — handles forward auto-run and input-driven lane switches. 2. **Obstacle Spawner** — periodically instantiates obstacle prefabs ahead of the player. 3. **Collision Detection System** — uses `OnCollisionEnter` (for obstacles) and `OnTriggerEnter` (for pickups). 4. **UI / HUD Manager** — displays score and current speed to the player. *(Valid 4th alternatives: Prefab system, Sound manager, Game Over state handler, Particle effects.)* ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **What is the role of `Time.deltaTime` in Unity3D? Why is it mandatory in movement code?** **Model Answer:** `Time.deltaTime` is the **elapsed time in seconds between the current frame and the previous frame**. It is the canonical mechanism for achieving **frame-rate independent (FRI) movement**. **Why mandatory:** Without multiplying velocity by `Time.deltaTime`, a fast computer running at 144 FPS would move the player 144 displacement-units per second, while a slow computer at 30 FPS would move it only 30 units per second — producing wildly inconsistent gameplay. The correct kinematic update is: $$ \vec{p}_{n+1} = \vec{p}_n + \vec{v}_n \cdot \Delta t $$ where $\Delta t$ is `Time.deltaTime`. This guarantees that displacement per second of real time remains constant regardless of frame rate. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`** **(a)** Explain the complete procedure to create a Runner Game in Unity3D using custom assets. Include details on GameObject creation, Prefab usage, and Script attachment. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Design and implement a C# script to **increase and decrease the player speed** in a Unity3D runner game. The speed must be clamped between 50% and 300% of the base value, and the change must be triggered by trigger-based pickups. Show the complete code with all relevant methods. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Q.A(a) — Procedure to Create Runner Game (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Project Initialization (1 Mark)** - Open Unity Hub → New 3D Project (URP) → Name it `KTU_RunnerGame`. - In `Edit → Project Settings → Player`, set the company name and default orientation to "Portrait" (for mobile) or leave as default for PC. **Step 2 — Asset Import (1 Mark)** - Drag and drop custom assets (player `.fbx`, ground `.png`, sound `.wav`) into the **Project Window's Assets folder**. - Unity auto-generates `.meta` files and processes the assets through the Importer. **Step 3 — Scene Construction (2 Marks)** - **Player:** Create empty GameObject → Add `MeshFilter`, `MeshRenderer`, `Rigidbody`, `BoxCollider`. Assign the imported FBX model. Tag as `"Player"`. - **Ground:** Create Plane GameObject → Scale Z = 50 → Add Mesh Collider → Tag as `"Ground"`. - **Camera:** Position behind and above the player. Set as `MainCamera` with appropriate FOV (typically 60°). - **Light:** Add Directional Light with shadows enabled. **Step 4 — Prefab Creation (1.5 Marks)** - Create a Cube obstacle → Configure its `BoxCollider` and `Material`. - Drag the Cube from the Hierarchy into the `Assets/Prefabs/` folder to create a **Prefab** (a reusable template). - The original instance can now be deleted; the Prefab remains. **Step 5 — Script Attachment (1.5 Marks)** - Create a new C# script in the Project window: `PlayerController.cs`. - Attach it to the Player GameObject as a Component (drag-and-drop). - The `Start()` and `Update()` Unity message methods are called automatically by the engine. **Step 6 — Spawner Implementation (Bonus Mention)** - Create an empty `Spawner` GameObject and attach `ObstacleSpawner.cs` that uses `InvokeRepeating("SpawnObstacle", 1f, 1.5f)` to instantiate obstacle Prefabs at the spawn point. --- #### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — Speed Control Script (7 Marks) ```csharp using UnityEngine; public class PlayerController : MonoBehaviour { public float baseSpeed = 5f; public float minMultiplier = 0.5f; public float maxMultiplier = 3.0f; public float speedStep = 0.5f; private float currentMultiplier = 1.0f; void Update() { float effectiveSpeed = baseSpeed * currentMultiplier; transform.Translate(Vector3.forward * effectiveSpeed * Time.deltaTime); } public void IncreaseSpeed() { currentMultiplier = Mathf.Clamp( currentMultiplier + speedStep, minMultiplier, maxMultiplier); } public void DecreaseSpeed() { currentMultiplier = Mathf.Clamp( currentMultiplier - speedStep, minMultiplier, maxMultiplier); } public float GetEffectiveSpeed() { return baseSpeed * currentMultiplier; } } ``` **Accompanying Pickup Script:** ```csharp using UnityEngine; public class SpeedPickup : MonoBehaviour { public enum PickupType { Boost, Slowdown } public PickupType type = PickupType.Boost; void OnTriggerEnter(Collider other) { if (other.CompareTag("Player")) { PlayerController pc = other.GetComponent(); if (pc != null) { if (type == PickupType.Boost) pc.IncreaseSpeed(); else pc.DecreaseSpeed(); } Destroy(gameObject); } } } ``` **Viva-Voce Justification (Key Points to Speak):** 1. `Mathf.Clamp` enforces the bounded range $[0.5, 3.0]$ as required. 2. `OnTriggerEnter` is used (not `OnCollisionEnter`) so the pickup is collected **without physical collision** — it is detected by overlapping trigger volumes. 3. The pickup GameObject is destroyed after collection to **prevent double-collection**. 4. `Time.deltaTime` ensures **frame-rate independence** in the Update loop. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **complete functional architecture of a Unity3D runner game**, identifying the roles of the Input System, Player Controller, Physics Engine, and UI Manager. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Derive the **frame-rate independent position update equation** $\vec{p}_{n+1} = \vec{p}_n + \vec{v}_n \cdot \Delta t$ from first principles, and write a C# code snippet that implements it correctly. Also show how a **speed boost** modifies this equation, with the speed bounded between 0.5 and 3.0 times the base. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Q.B(a) — Functional Architecture (7 Marks) Refer to the **Section 4.1 System Block Diagram** for the full architecture. The role of each block: | Block | Role | Implementation | |---|---|---| | **Input System** | Captures keyboard / touch input | `Input.GetKey()`, `Input.GetAxis()` | | **Player Controller** | Translates input into game actions | Custom C# `MonoBehaviour` | | **Transform** | Stores position, rotation, scale | `Vector3`, `Quaternion` | | **Rigidbody + Physics** | Simulates gravity, jump | `Physics.gravity`, `Rigidbody.linearVelocity` | | **Collider System** | Detects overlaps and impacts | `OnCollisionEnter`, `OnTriggerEnter` | | **Spawner** | Instantiates obstacles periodically | `InvokeRepeating`, Coroutines | | **UI Manager** | Renders score, speed, lives | `TextMeshProUGUI`, `Canvas` | (7 marks: 1 mark per major block correctly identified with role + implementation) --- #### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — Derivation + Code (7 Marks) **Derivation (4 Marks):** Step 1 — Definition of velocity: $$ \vec{v}(t) = \frac{d\vec{p}(t)}{dt} $$ Step 2 — Forward Euler discretization at time $t_n = n \cdot \Delta t$: $$ \vec{v}(t_n) \approx \frac{\vec{p}(t_{n+1}) - \vec{p}(t_n)}{\Delta t} $$ Step 3 — Rearranging for the next position: $$ \boxed{\vec{p}_{n+1} = \vec{p}_n + \vec{v}_n \cdot \Delta t} $$ Step 4 — Incorporating the speed multiplier $k$: $$ \vec{v}_n = \vec{v}_{\text{base}} \cdot k_n \quad \text{where} \quad k_n \in [0.5, 3.0] $$ Step 5 — Clamped update: $$ k_{n+1} = \text{Clamp}(k_n + \Delta k, \; 0.5, \; 3.0) $$ **C# Implementation Code (3 Marks):** ```csharp public class PlayerController : MonoBehaviour { public float baseSpeed = 5f; private float speedMultiplier = 1.0f; private const float MIN_K = 0.5f, MAX_K = 3.0f; void Update() { Vector3 velocity = Vector3.forward * (baseSpeed * speedMultiplier); transform.position += velocity * Time.deltaTime; } public void Boost() { speedMultiplier = Mathf.Clamp(speedMultiplier + 0.5f, MIN_K, MAX_K); } } ``` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Forgetting `Time.deltaTime`** (-2 to -3 marks): Movement that runs on a 144 FPS machine will be unplayable on a 30 FPS machine. Always show `Time.deltaTime` in movement code. > 2. **Hardcoding the speed multiplier** (-2 marks): Examiners check for `Mathf.Clamp`. If you write `speedMultiplier += 0.5f` without clamping, the game becomes uncontrollable after 4 boosts (multiplier = 3.0, then 3.5, then 4.0...). > 3. **Using `OnCollisionEnter` instead of `OnTriggerEnter` for pickups** (-1 mark): The pickup must have `Is Trigger = true` on its collider, and the player must pass *through* it. If you use solid collision, the player will bounce off the pickup without collecting it. > 4. **Forgetting to attach the script to a GameObject** (-1 mark): The `Start()` and `Update()` methods are only called by Unity's message loop if the script is attached as a Component. A script sitting in the Project Window does nothing. > 5. **Confusing `Rigidbody.velocity` (Unity 5–2022) with `Rigidbody.linearVelocity` (Unity 6 / 2024 Scheme)** (-1 mark): Both work, but `linearVelocity` is the correct modern syntax. > 6. **No `Destroy(gameObject)` after pickup collection** (-1 mark): The pickup stays in the scene and gets re-collected infinitely. > 7. **Not tagging the Player as `"Player"`** (-1 mark): The pickup's `CompareTag("Player")` check will fail silently if the tag is not set. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2 Experiment: Runner Game in Unity3D** - [ ] **Runner Game** = endless auto-forward game; player gives only lateral/jump input. - [ ] **GameObject** = empty container holding Components; the fundamental Unity entity. - [ ] **Prefab** = reusable serialized GameObject template stored in `Assets/Prefabs/`. - [ ] **Transform** = position + rotation + scale (Vector3 + Quaternion). - [ ] **Rigidbody** = physics-enabled body; subject to gravity and forces. - [ ] **Collider** = collision shape; `Is Trigger = true` for non-solid detection. - [ ] **OnTriggerEnter(Collider other)** = called when two trigger volumes overlap. - [ ] **OnCollisionEnter(Collision info)** = called on physical collision. - [ ] **Time.deltaTime** = time between frames; **mandatory** for frame-rate independence. - [ ] **Position Update Equation:** $\vec{p}_{n+1} = \vec{p}_n + \vec{v}_n \cdot \Delta t$. - [ ] **Speed Multiplier Equation:** $k_{n+1} = \text{Clamp}(k_n + \Delta k, 0.5, 3.0)$. - [ ] **Mathf.Clamp(x, a, b)** = $\max(a, \min(x, b))$; bounds a scalar between two limits. - [ ] **Effective Velocity:** $v_{\text{eff}}(t) = v_{\text{base}} \cdot k(t)$. - [ ] **Unity Default Gravity:** $\vec{g} = -9.81$ m/s² along -Y. - [ ] **Jump Initial Velocity:** $v_{y,0} = \sqrt{2 g h}$ for desired apex height $h$. - [ ] **InvokeRepeating("Method", delay, period)** = timed coroutine for obstacle spawning. - [ ] **Instantiate(prefab, position, rotation)** = spawns a prefab clone at runtime. - [ ] **Destroy(gameObject)** = removes a GameObject from the scene (used for collected pickups). - [ ] **UI Display:** Use `TextMeshProUGUI` (modern) or legacy `UnityEngine.UI.Text`. - [ ] **Tag the Player** as `"Player"` and the Ground as `"Ground"` in the Inspector. - [ ] **Asset Pipeline:** Source file → Project Window → Importer → Prefab → Scene → Build. - [ ] **Modern API (2024 Scheme / Unity 6):** Prefer `Rigidbody.linearVelocity` over the deprecated `Rigidbody.velocity`. - [ ] **The new Input System Package** (`Keyboard.current.leftArrowKey.isPressed`) is preferred over legacy `Input.GetKey()` in modern projects. - [ ] **ScriptableObject** can be used to externalize speed presets (e.g., Easy = 0.5 step, Hard = 1.0 step). - [ ] **Object Pooling** (advanced): Reuse obstacle instances instead of `Destroy` + `Instantiate` for better performance. - [ ] **Layer-Based Collision Matrix:** Configure in `Edit → Project Settings → Physics` to control which layers collide. - [ ] **Build Target:** `File → Build Settings` → Choose Windows / Android / WebGL.

topics.title: Mini Project: Create a Game from Scratch Using Unity3D or JavaScript
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCNL508 - VR LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Mini Project: Create a Game from Scratch Using Unity3D or JavaScript

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Mini Project: Create a Game from Scratch Using Unity3D or JavaScript** is a structured laboratory implementation exercise in which students design, develop, test, and deploy a fully interactive 2D or 3D digital game using the **Unity3D Game Engine** (with C# scripting) **OR** a browser-based web stack employing **JavaScript (ES6+)**, **HTML5 Canvas / WebGL**, and a supporting rendering library such as **Phaser**, **Three.js**, or **Babylon.js**.

As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PCCNL508 (VR Lab)**, this mini-project consolidates the foundational constructs of Virtual Reality and Interactive Computer Graphics — namely the **Game Loop**, **Scene Graph**, **Transform Mathematics**, **Collision Detection**, **Physics Simulation**, **Input Mapping**, and **Rendering Pipeline** — into a single deliverable artefact.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Verbatim Spirit):**
> *"Mini Project – Design and implement an interactive game application from scratch demonstrating scene composition, physics, input handling, and basic AI/rendering pipeline using Unity3D or JavaScript."*

### 1.2 Key Terminology Glossary

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Scene Graph** | A hierarchical data structure that organises all game objects in parent-child relationships, governing both **spatial transforms** and **logical inheritance**. |
| **GameObject** | The fundamental entity in Unity3D — an empty container to which components (transforms, meshes, scripts) are attached. |
| **Prefab** | A reusable, serialised template of a GameObject stored as an asset in the Project window. |
| **Delta Time ($\Delta t$)** | The real-world elapsed time between two consecutive frames, used to make physics frame-rate independent. |
| **Frame Rate (FPS)** | The number of rendered frames per second; standard VR targets **$\geq 90$ FPS**. |
| **AABB** | Axis-Aligned Bounding Box, the most common collision primitive. |
| **Asset Pipeline** | The workflow that imports textures, meshes, audio, and scripts into the engine and prepares them for runtime consumption. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of game development like building a stage play:**
> - **Unity3D / JavaScript Engine** = The theatre building itself (lights, stage, sound system).
> - **Scenes** = Individual acts of the play.
> - **GameObjects / Sprites** = The actors and props on stage.
> - **Scripts (C# / JS)** = The director's script telling each actor what to do and when.
> - **The Game Loop** = The continuous rhythm of *"observe → decide → act → render"* that keeps the play alive, beat after beat, every **1/60th of a second**.

A game is therefore not a "movie" (passive, linear playback) but a **living simulation**: it continuously listens to the player, simulates the world, and redraws the screen — frame after frame — until the player quits.

## 1.4 KTU Course Outcomes Mapped

This mini-project, when evaluated, satisfies the following Course Outcomes under the **2024 Scheme OBE framework**:

| CO ID | Course Outcome Description | Bloom's Level |
|---|---|---|
| **CO2** | Implement interactive VR/AR/game applications using contemporary engines. | Apply / Create |
| **CO3** | Apply physics, collision, and rendering concepts to simulate virtual environments. | Apply / Analyse |
| **CO4** | Demonstrate effective project documentation and presentation of a working software artefact. | Create / Communicate |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Game Development Lifecycle (GDLC)

A professional game, even a mini-project, must follow a structured pipeline. The following phases are universally adopted (and expected in KTU viva voce):

1. **Concept & Design Document (GDD)**
   - Define genre (Platformer / Shooter / Puzzle / RPG).
   - Story, mechanics, win/lose conditions.
   - Target platform (PC, Mobile, Web, VR Headset).
2. **Asset Acquisition / Creation**
   - 3D Models (.fbx, .obj), Textures (.png, .jpg), Audio (.wav, .mp3).
   - Use open-source libraries: **Kenney.nl**, **OpenGameArt**, **Mixamo** (rigged characters).
3. **Scene Authoring**
   - Place terrain, lights, cameras, and characters in the editor.
4. **Scripting & Logic**
   - Player controller, enemy AI, scoring system, UI manager.
5. **Physics & Collision**
   - Rigidbody, Collider components (Unity) / Matter.js / Cannon.js (JS).
6. **Testing & Iteration**
   - Playtest, debug, optimise frame-rate.
7. **Build & Deployment**
   - Unity → `.exe` / `.apk` / WebGL.
   - JS → Browser-hosted `index.html`.

## 2.2 The Core Game Loop — Heartbeat of Every Game

Every interactive application, from **Pong (1972)** to modern **VR titles**, runs on the same loop. Understanding this loop is the single most exam-relevant concept in this module.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Universal Game Loop Equation:**
>
> $$\text{Frame}_{n+1} = \underbrace{f_{\text{input}}(\text{Frame}_n)}_{\text{1. INPUT}} \;\circ\; \underbrace{f_{\text{update}}(\text{Frame}_n, \Delta t)}_{\text{2. UPDATE}} \;\circ\; \underbrace{f_{\text{render}}(\text{Frame}_n)}_{\text{3. RENDER}}$$

Where:
- $f_{\text{input}}$ polls keyboard, mouse, joystick, touch, or VR controller.
- $f_{\text{update}}$ advances physics, AI, timers, and game state.
- $f_{\text{render}}$ repaints the framebuffer.

This is executed at a fixed cadence, typically **$\Delta t = \frac{1}{60}$ s** or **$\frac{1}{90}$ s** for VR.

## 2.3 Unity3D Engine Architecture

Unity3D is a **component-oriented engine**. Every entity is a `GameObject` and behaviour is added via attached `MonoBehaviour` scripts. The runtime architecture is:

| Subsystem | Responsibility |
|---|---|
| **Editor** | Authoring tool (Hierarchy, Scene, Inspector, Project). |
| **Player Runtime** | Compiled binary that loads scenes, instantiates objects, runs the loop. |
| **Physics Engine (PhysX)** | Rigidbody dynamics, raycasting, joint constraints. |
| **Rendering Pipeline** | URP / HDRP / Built-in — converts 3D scene → 2D framebuffer. |
| **Input System** | Legacy Input Manager or new Input System Package. |
| **Audio System** | FMOD-based 3D spatial audio. |
| **Scripting Backend** | Mono (default) or IL2CPP (AOT compiled). |

### 2.3.1 The MonoBehaviour Lifecycle (Unity)

This is the **#1 viva question** for any Unity-based mini-project. Every script attached to a GameObject follows this order:

```
Awake()         → Called once when the object is instantiated (before Start).
OnEnable()      → Called each time the object becomes active.
Start()         → Called once before the first frame update.
Update()        → Called every frame (variable Δt). Use for input & non-physics logic.
FixedUpdate()   → Called at fixed timestep (default 0.02 s). Use for physics.
LateUpdate()    → Called after all Update()s — ideal for camera follow logic.
OnTriggerEnter() → Called when collider enters a trigger volume.
OnCollisionEnter() → Called when colliders physically touch.
OnDisable() / OnDestroy() → Cleanup hooks.
```

## 2.4 JavaScript-Based Browser Game Stack

For students opting for **JavaScript** (no Unity install required, lighter-weight, runs in any browser), the canonical stack is:

| Layer | Library | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Rendering** | HTML5 `<canvas>` / **Phaser 3** / **Three.js** / **Babylon.js** | 2D or WebGL 3D drawing. |
| **Physics** | **Matter.js** / **Cannon.js** / **Ammo.js** | Rigidbody dynamics. |
| **Input** | Native `addEventListener` for `keydown`, `mousemove`, `touchstart` | Cross-platform input. |
| **Audio** | **Howler.js** / Web Audio API | Sound effects & music. |
| **Game Loop** | `requestAnimationFrame(time)` | Browser-synced 60 FPS loop. |

### 2.4.1 The JavaScript Game Loop

```javascript
let lastTime = 0;

function gameLoop(currentTime) {
    const deltaTime = (currentTime - lastTime) / 1000; // ms → seconds
    lastTime = currentTime;

    input.update();
    physics.update(deltaTime);
    scene.update(deltaTime);
    renderer.draw();

    requestAnimationFrame(gameLoop);
}

requestAnimationFrame(gameLoop);
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why `requestAnimationFrame` and not `setInterval`?**
> Because the browser **synchronises** `rAF` with the display refresh rate (typically 60 Hz or 120 Hz) and **pauses** it when the tab is in the background — saving CPU and battery.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table — Memorise for KTU Exam**

| Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Linear Motion (Verlet) | $x_{n+1} = 2x_n - x_{n-1} + a \cdot \Delta t^2$ | Position update | $m$ |
| Euler Integration | $v_{n+1} = v_n + a \cdot \Delta t$ | Velocity update | $m/s$ |
| Position Update | $x_{n+1} = x_n + v_{n+1} \cdot \Delta t$ | Kinematic step | $m$ |
| Gravity Force | $F_g = m \cdot g$ | Weight, $g = 9.81$ | $N$ |
| FPS to Frame Time | $T_{\text{frame}} = \frac{1}{\text{FPS}}$ | Time per frame | $s$ |
| AABB Overlap (X-axis) | $\vert x_{a,\min} - x_{b,\max} \vert \le 0 \; \land\; \vert x_{b,\min} - x_{a,\max} \vert \le 0$ | Collision test | $m$ |
| Dot Product (Facing) | $\cos\theta = \frac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{\vert\vec{A}\vert\,\vert\vec{B}\vert}$ | Angle between vectors | dimensionless |
| Cross Product (Normal) | $\vec{N} = \vec{A} \times \vec{B}$ | Surface normal | $m^2$ |
| FPS Requirement (VR) | $\text{FPS} \geq 90$ | To avoid motion sickness | Hz |
| Latency Budget (VR) | $M_t \leq 20\,\text{ms}$ | Motion-to-photon delay | $ms$ |
| Field of View | $\text{FOV} = 2\arctan\!\left(\frac{h}{2f}\right)$ | Camera lens parameter | degrees |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The same component-based architecture used in Unity3D powers:

| Industry | Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Automotive** | Unity HMI (Human-Machine Interface) prototyping for Tesla, BMW, Toyota. |
| **Architecture (AEC)** | Real-time 3D walkthroughs (BIM visualisation). |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Frame-Independent Player Movement

A common examiner trap is the student who writes:

```
position += direction * speed;          // ❌ Frame-rate dependent!
```

This causes the player to move faster on a 120 Hz monitor than on a 60 Hz one. The **KTU-expected** derivation for correct motion is as follows.

### Step 1 — Define the velocity vector

Let $\vec{v}$ be the player's velocity in world-space, with magnitude $s$ (speed in $m/s$) and direction $\hat{d}$ (a unit vector derived from input).

$$\vec{v} = s \cdot \hat{d}$$

### Step 2 — Define the discrete time step

Let $\Delta t$ be the time elapsed since the last frame.

$$\Delta t = t_n - t_{n-1}$$

In Unity, this is supplied as `Time.deltaTime`. In JS, it is computed from `requestAnimationFrame`.

### Step 3 — Integrate position over time

Using the kinematic relation $x = x_0 + v \cdot t$ for constant velocity within one frame:

$$x_{n+1} = x_n + \vec{v} \cdot \Delta t$$

### Step 4 — Expand for clarity

$$x_{n+1} = x_n + (s \cdot \hat{d}) \cdot \Delta t$$

### Step 5 — Verify dimensionally

$$\underbrace{[x_{n+1}]}_{m} = \underbrace{[x_n]}_{m} + \underbrace{[s]}_{m/s} \cdot \underbrace{[\hat{d}]}_{\text{dimensionless}} \cdot \underbrace{[\Delta t]}_{s} \;\Rightarrow\; m$$

✓ Dimensionally consistent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Multiplication by $\Delta t$ converts velocity (m/s) into per-frame displacement (m), making the simulation **frame-rate independent** — a mandatory requirement in any KTU-graded mini-project.

## 3.2 Derivation: AABB Collision Detection

The most fundamental collision test in 2D game development.

### Setup

Let rectangle $A$ have bounds $(x_{A,\min}, x_{A,\max}, y_{A,\min}, y_{A,\max})$ and rectangle $B$ have bounds $(x_{B,\min}, x_{B,\max}, y_{B,\min}, y_{B,\max})$.

### Step 1 — X-axis overlap test

Two AABBs overlap along the X-axis **iff**:

$$x_{A,\min} \le x_{B,\max} \quad \land \quad x_{B,\min} \le x_{A,\max}$$

### Step 2 — Y-axis overlap test

$$y_{A,\min} \le y_{B,\max} \quad \land \quad y_{B,\min} \le y_{A,\max}$$

### Step 3 — Combine via logical AND

$$\text{Collide} = \left(x_{A,\min} \le x_{B,\max}\right) \;\land\; \left(x_{B,\min} \le x_{A,\max}\right) \;\land\; \left(y_{A,\min} \le y_{B,\max}\right) \;\land\; \left(y_{B,\min} \le y_{A,\max}\right)$$

### Step 4 — Practical implementation (pseudo-code, NOT actual exam code)

```
if A.xMin ≤ B.xMax AND B.xMin ≤ A.xMax
AND A.yMin ≤ B.yMax AND B.yMin ≤ A.yMax
    → COLLISION DETECTED
else
    → NO COLLISION
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why AABB and not perfect polygon collision?**
> AABB requires only **4 comparisons** and zero trigonometry — orders of magnitude faster. In Unity, this is the default for primitive colliders, while complex meshes use the more expensive **Convex/Concave Mesh Collider**.

## 3.3 Derivation: Camera Follow (Smooth Damped Tracking)

When a 3rd-person camera follows the player, **abrupt lerping causes motion sickness** in VR. The KTU-standard technique is **Critically Damped Spring Smoothing**:

$$\vec{c}_{n+1} = \vec{c}_n + \left(\vec{p}_{\text{target}} - \vec{c}_n\right) \cdot k \cdot \Delta t$$

Where:

- $\vec{c}_n$ = current camera position.
- $\vec{p}_{\text{target}}$ = target (player) position.
- $k$ = smoothing constant (typically $k = 5$ for "snappy", $k = 2$ for "cinematic").

### Stability Analysis

For numerical stability:

$$k \cdot \Delta t < 1$$

In Unity, `Vector3.Lerp` and `Vector3.SmoothDamp` handle this internally; in JavaScript the student must implement it manually — a favourite KTU viva question.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mini-Project Complexity Tiers

This matrix helps the student self-assess the appropriate ambition level for a 14-mark graded project.

| Tier | Complexity | Features | Expected Grade |

| **Tier 1 (Pass)** | Single scene, single player, keyboard input, score counter, basic collision | Win/Lose state, restart button | 7–8 / 14 |
| **Tier 2 (Good)** | Multiple levels, AI enemies, sound effects, animated sprites, physics-driven objects | Score persistence, particle effects, menu | 9–11 / 14 |
| **Tier 3 (Excellent)** | Procedural generation, pathfinding, multiplayer or VR mode, custom shaders | State machine, save/load, networked play | 12–14 / 14 |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**Define the term *Game Loop*. List its three primary phases.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **Game Loop** is the central execution cycle of any interactive application that continuously processes user input, updates the world state, and re-renders the framebuffer until termination.
>
> The three primary phases are:
> 1. **Input Phase** — Polls keyboard, mouse, touch, or VR controller events.
> 2. **Update Phase** — Advances physics, AI, timers, animations using $\Delta t$.
> 3. **Render Phase** — Draws the current state to the framebuffer for display.
>
> In Unity, this is handled implicitly by the engine via `Update()` and `LateUpdate()`. In JavaScript, it is implemented explicitly using `requestAnimationFrame`. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between `Update()` and `FixedUpdate()` in Unity3D. When should each be used?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `Update()` | `FixedUpdate()` |
|---|---|---|
| Call frequency | Once per rendered frame (variable). | At fixed physics timestep (default **0.02 s** = 50 Hz). |
| Time argument | `Time.deltaTime` (variable). | `Time.fixedDeltaTime` (constant). |
| Use case | Reading input, UI updates, non-physics game logic. | Applying forces, moving rigidbodies, physics queries. |
| Frame-rate dependence | Can be frame-rate dependent if $\Delta t$ ignored. | Deterministic and reproducible. |

> **Rule of Thumb:** *Apply forces and rigidbody manipulation in `FixedUpdate()`; read input and update visuals in `Update()`. **(3 marks)***
