## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Unity3D Track

#### (a) Design and explain the architecture of a 2D platformer mini-project in Unity3D. Include the GameObject hierarchy, scripts, and prefabs you would create. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Architecture Overview (2 marks):**
The 2D platformer will consist of four primary GameObject categories organised in a parent-child scene graph:

- `Environment` (parent)
  - `Ground` (tilemap collider)
  - `Platforms` (multiple instances)
  - `Hazards` (spikes, pits)
- `Player` (parent)
  - `SpriteRenderer`
  - `Rigidbody2D`
  - `BoxCollider2D`
  - `PlayerController.cs`
- `Enemies`
  - `EnemyPrefab` (instanced N times)
  - `EnemyAI.cs`
- `UI_Canvas`
  - `ScoreText`
  - `LivesText`
  - `GameOverPanel`

**Core Scripts (3 marks):**

1. `PlayerController.cs` — Handles horizontal movement via `Input.GetAxis("Horizontal")` and jump via `Input.GetButtonDown("Jump")`. Movement uses $\Delta t$ to remain frame-rate independent:
   `transform.Translate(direction * speed * Time.deltaTime)`
2. `EnemyAI.cs` — Uses a state machine (`Patrol → Chase → Attack`) and raycast to detect player within a configurable radius.
3. `GameManager.cs` — Singleton that manages score, lives, win/lose state, and scene transitions via `SceneManager.LoadScene()`.

**Prefabs (2 marks):**
- `EnemyPrefab` with attached Rigidbody2D, Collider, and `EnemyAI.cs` — instantiated multiple times.
- `CoinPrefab` — triggers `OnTriggerEnter2D` to increment score.

> The architecture is modular, decoupled, and re-usable across scenes.

#### (b) Write the algorithm and explain the physics-based collision response between the player and an enemy in the above project. How would you differentiate *trigger* vs *collision* events? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Algorithm (3 marks):**

```
OnCollisionEnter2D(Collision2D col):
    IF col.gameObject.tag == "Enemy":
        rigidBody.velocity = Vector2.up * jumpForce   // bounce
        TakeDamage(1)

OnTriggerEnter2D(Collider2D other):
    IF other.gameObject.tag == "Coin":
        score += 10
        Destroy(other.gameObject)
```

**Trigger vs Collision (4 marks):**

| Property | `Collider` | `Trigger Collider` |
|---|---|---|
| `isTrigger` flag | `false` (default) | `true` |
| Physical response | Yes (bounces, stops) | No (passes through) |
| Callback | `OnCollisionEnter2D` | `OnTriggerEnter2D` |
| Use case | Player ↔ Ground | Player ↔ Coin, Player ↔ Checkpoint |

> **Trigger** events fire when objects *overlap* but no physics resolution occurs; **Collision** events fire when objects *physically contact* and the physics engine resolves the interaction.

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — JavaScript Track

#### (a) Design a browser-based 2D game using HTML5 Canvas and JavaScript. Describe the complete file structure, game loop, and asset-loading strategy. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**File Structure (2 marks):**

```
/my-game
    index.html       ← Canvas + script tags
    /css
        style.css    ← Page styling
    /js
        main.js      ← Entry point + game loop
        player.js    ← Player class
        enemy.js     ← Enemy class
        input.js     ← Input handler
        physics.js   ← Matter.js wrapper
    /assets
        /sprites     ← .png files
        /audio       ← .mp3 / .wav files
```

**Game Loop (3 marks):**

The loop uses `requestAnimationFrame` and computes a delta time for frame-rate independence:

```javascript
let last = performance.now();
function loop(now) {
    const dt = (now - last) / 1000;
    last = now;
    handleInput();
    update(dt);
    render();
    requestAnimationFrame(loop);
}
requestAnimationFrame(loop);
```

**Asset Loading (2 marks):**
All images and audio are pre-loaded into a global `assets` object before the loop starts, displayed via a loading bar. This prevents mid-game stutter.

> **Best Practice:** Pre-load all assets in an `init()` async function; only call `requestAnimationFrame(loop)` after `Promise.all(images.map(load))` resolves.

#### (b) Implement (algorithmically) the player jump physics and AABB collision against a platform. Show the formulas used. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Jump Algorithm (3 marks):**

```
onKeyDown('Space'):
    IF player.onGround:
        player.velocityY = -jumpStrength   // negative = upward
    ELSE:
        do nothing

each frame:
    player.velocityY += gravity * dt       // g = 9.81 m/s² scaled
    player.y += player.velocityY * dt
    IF player collides with platform:
        player.y = platform.y - player.height
        player.velocityY = 0
        player.onGround = true
```

**Formulas (4 marks):**

Verlet/Euler integration for vertical motion:

$$v_{y,n+1} = v_{y,n} + g \cdot \Delta t$$

$$y_{n+1} = y_n + v_{y,n+1} \cdot \Delta t$$

AABB overlap with the platform $(x_p, y_p, w_p, h_p)$:

$$\text{Hit} = \left(y_{p} - h_{p} \le y_{n+1} \le y_{p}\right) \;\land\; \left(x_{p} \le x_{n+1} \le x_{p} + w_{p}\right)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting $\Delta t$** is the #1 reason students lose 2–3 marks. Movement must be multiplied by `Time.deltaTime` (Unity) or `dt` (JS), or the game will be **non-deterministic across machines**.
> 2. **Misnaming MonoBehaviour methods** (e.g., `update()` lowercase) — Unity will throw silent errors; students blame the engine.
> 3. **No prefab / no modular design** — examiner deducts marks for monolithic code.
> 4. **No win/lose state machine** — the project is considered incomplete.
> 5. **No asset attribution** — open-source assets must be credited in the GDD.
> 6. **Viva trap:** If you cannot explain why `FixedUpdate` is used for physics, expect 2-mark deduction in the viva.

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Night Cram Sheet)**

- [ ] A **Game Loop** has exactly three phases: **Input → Update → Render**.
- [ ] In Unity, physics lives in `FixedUpdate()`; logic in `Update()`; camera follow in `LateUpdate()`.
- [ ] **`Time.deltaTime` / `dt` is non-negotiable** for frame-rate independence.
- [ ] **AABB collision** = 4 comparisons; **Circle collision** = distance < sum of radii.
- [ ] **Trigger vs Collision**: `isTrigger = true` means no physical response.
- [ ] **Prefabs** enable reuse; **Singletons** enable global access (use sparingly).
- [ ] **VR-specific**: Target $\geq 90$ FPS and $M_t \leq 20\,ms$ latency.
- [ ] **JavaScript loop**: Always use `requestAnimationFrame`, never `setInterval`.
- [ ] **Pre-load assets** before starting the loop to avoid stutter.
- [ ] **Document** the project with a Game Design Document (GDD) — KTU requires this in the mini-project report.
- [ ] **Modular architecture**: Separate scripts for Player, Enemy, UI, GameManager.
- [ ] **Version control**: Use Git; commit incrementally; KTU evaluators appreciate a clean repo.
- [ ] **Build target**: Unity → WebGL / Standalone / Android; JS → Chrome / Firefox.
- [ ] **Test for win, lose, pause, and restart states** — all four must be demonstrable.
